Patients often present with chronic pruritic lesions that mimic eczema or psoriasis. Diagnosis requires skin biopsy and immunophenotyping. Advanced disease may involve lymph nodes, blood, and visceral organs. Complications include infection, erythroderma, systemic dissemination, and reduced survival in advanced stages.
1.management: Early disease is treated with skin-directed therapies such as topical corticosteroids, phototherapy, or radiation. Advanced disease may require systemic immunomodulatory treatment.

clues.text: A 23 year old woman is found difficult to arouse
Her breathing appears shallow and infrequent
Examination of the eyes reveals pinpoint pupils
Oxygen saturation is low
Bradycardia and hypotension
Her symptoms improve rapidly after naloxone administration

1.description: Opioids activate μ-opioid receptors, causing CNS depression, respiratory depression, and miosis. The classic triad is coma, respiratory depression, and pinpoint pupils.
1.management: Airway support, oxygen/ventilation as needed, naloxone, monitor for recurrence because naloxone may wear off before the opioid.

clues.text: A patient recently started taking morphine for pain after surgery
Their pain has improved, but they now have infrequent bowel movements.
They report hard stools, straining, bloating, and abdominal discomfort.
They deny vomiting, fever, or signs of bowel obstruction.
They have no history of bowel disease or syndromes
There are μ-opioid receptors in the gut

1.description: Opioids bind μ-receptors in the GI tract, decreasing gut motility and secretions. Unlike nausea or sedation, constipation often persists.
1.management: Start bowel regimen: stimulant laxative such as senna or bisacodyl ± osmotic laxative such as polyethylene glycol. Consider peripherally acting μ-opioid antagonists if refractory.

clues.text: A 57 year old man presents with fatigue
Review of his medications include regular aspirin and ibuprofen for aches
He reports black, tarry stools
Labs reveal a slight normocytic anemia
He reports severe epigastric pain
Endoscopy reveals a bleeding duodenal ulcer

1.description: NSAIDs and aspirin inhibit prostaglandin production, reducing gastric mucosal protection and increasing risk of ulceration and bleeding.
1.management: Stop NSAID/ASA if appropriate, stabilize ABCs, IV fluids/blood if needed, IV PPI, urgent endoscopy for significant bleeding.

clues.text: A patient with heavy alcohol use presents after suddenly stopping drinking two days ago
Within hours, they developed anxiety, tremor, sweating, nausea, and palpitations.
Vitals show tachycardia, and hypertension
The patient is agitated
In the emergency room, the patient begins to seize
Severe cases progress to delirium tremens with confusion, autonomic instability, and visual hallucinations 48–96 hours after their last drink.

1.description: Chronic alcohol enhances GABA activity and suppresses NMDA activity. Abrupt cessation causes CNS hyperexcitability.
1.management: Benzodiazepines, thiamine before glucose, fluids/electrolyte correction, monitor for seizures/delirium tremens.

1.diagnosis: Constitutional Delay of Growth and Puberty (CDGP)
aliases: Constitutional Delay of Growth and Puberty

clues.text: A 12-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of short stature after his parents notice he is shorter than most classmates.
He was born at term with normal birth weight, was slow to grow as a toddler, and his father recalls starting puberty later than peers.
Physical examination reveals proportionate short stature with prepubertal Tanner stage I development and an arm span equal to height.
Height is below the 3rd percentile, but growth velocity over the past year is normal for age and screening bloodwork is unremarkable.
Bone age assessment is significantly delayed relative to chronological age.
Predicted adult height is near the mid-parental target range.

1.description: Constitutional Delay of Growth and Puberty is a normal variant of growth characterized by delayed skeletal maturation and delayed pubertal onset with otherwise normal health. It is more common in boys and often has a family history of delayed puberty. Children are proportionately short, have normal growth velocity after early childhood, delayed bone age, and typically achieve near-normal adult height. Diagnosis is clinical after excluding pathologic causes of poor growth.
1.management: Reassurance, growth and pubertal monitoring, and consideration of short-course sex steroid therapy only in selected patients with significant psychosocial distress.

clues.text: A 7-year-old girl is referred because her height has remained near the bottom of the growth chart since early childhood.
Both parents are shorter than average, and several relatives were described as small throughout life.
Physical examination reveals proportionate short stature without dysmorphic features or signs of chronic illness.
Serial measurements show normal growth velocity, and height is appropriate for the parental target range.
Bone age assessment is consistent with chronological age.
Pubertal development is expected to begin at the usual age, and predicted adult height remains short but consistent with family height.

1.description: Familial Short Stature is a normal growth variant in which a child is short because their genetic height potential is short. Growth velocity is normal, puberty occurs at the usual time, bone age is consistent with chronological age, and final adult height is short but appropriate for mid-parental height. It is distinguished from constitutional delay by normal skeletal maturation and normal timing of puberty.
1.management: Reassurance, accurate serial growth measurements, and monitoring to ensure growth velocity remains normal.

clues.text: A school-aged child is assessed because parents report that clothing sizes have not changed for several years.
There is no history of poor nutrition, gastrointestinal symptoms, renal disease, or chronic inflammatory illness.
Examination reveals proportionate short stature with preserved weight percentile and no dysmorphic features.
Growth chart review demonstrates downward crossing of height percentiles with low IGF-1 on screening investigations.
Bone age assessment is delayed relative to chronological age.
Stimulation testing shows an inadequate rise in growth hormone after provocative testing.

1.description: Growth Hormone Deficiency is an endocrine cause of short stature characterized by impaired linear growth, downward crossing of height percentiles, delayed bone age, and relatively preserved or increased weight. Screening may show low IGF-1, but diagnosis requires formal growth hormone stimulation testing. Management involves specialist assessment and recombinant growth hormone therapy when appropriate.
1.management: Referral to pediatric endocrinology, growth hormone replacement when confirmed, and monitoring of growth response and associated pituitary hormone deficiencies.

clues.text: A 6-week-old infant is brought to clinic because of prolonged crying episodes that are exhausting the parents.
The episodes occur mostly in the evening and have no obvious trigger despite feeding, changing, and soothing attempts.
Physical examination is normal, and the infant appears well between episodes.
Weight gain, feeding, stooling, and hydration are appropriate for age.
There is no fever, bilious vomiting, blood in stool, lethargy, or abnormal neurologic finding.
Crying occurs for more than 3 hours per day on more than 3 days per week over several weeks.

1.description: Infantile Colic is excessive unexplained crying in an otherwise healthy and thriving infant, classically described by the rule of threes. It usually begins in the first weeks of life, peaks around 6 weeks, and resolves by 3–4 months. Diagnosis is clinical and requires exclusion of concerning features such as fever, poor growth, abnormal examination, bilious vomiting, or blood in stool. Management focuses on parental reassurance and support.
1.management: Reassurance, caregiver support, review of red flags, soothing strategies, and close follow-up if symptoms change; no specific medication is routinely indicated.

clues.text: A 3-month-old infant is seen for frequent spit-up after feeds.
The infant is feeding eagerly and has no blood or bile in the emesis.
Physical examination is normal, with no respiratory distress or abdominal abnormalities.
Growth parameters are tracking appropriately along the infant's usual curve.
Episodes occur several times per day but do not cause distress, feeding refusal, or interruption of activity.
Symptoms are expected to improve with maturation during the first year of life.

1.description: Physiologic gastroesophageal reflux is common in infancy because of developmental immaturity of anti-reflux mechanisms and liquid feeds. Infants are often described as happy spitters: they regurgitate but feed well, grow normally, and have no complications. Diagnosis is clinical. Management is reassurance and conservative feeding adjustment, with expected improvement by the end of the first year.
1.management: Reassurance and conservative feeding advice such as avoiding overfeeding; no routine acid suppression or diagnostic testing is needed in a thriving infant.

clues.text: An infant is brought to clinic for recurrent vomiting after feeds.
Caregivers report increasing feeding refusal and irritability during feeds.
Examination reveals poor weight gain compared with prior visits.
Growth chart review demonstrates falling weight percentile despite attempts to maintain intake.
The infant has recurrent cough and choking episodes temporally associated with feeds.
Symptoms persist despite conservative feeding changes and are associated with impaired growth.

1.description: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease refers to reflux associated with complications such as poor weight gain, feeding refusal, esophagitis, or respiratory symptoms. Unlike physiologic infant reflux, GERD affects health or development. Diagnosis is primarily clinical; investigations are reserved for complications, refractory cases, or concern for alternative diagnoses. Management starts conservatively and escalates only when needed.
1.management: Assess total caloric intake and feeding technique, avoid overfeeding, consider thickened feeds or dietary protein modification, and use a limited trial of acid suppression only when conservative measures fail and complications are present.

clues.text: A toddler is brought for episodes in which he briefly loses consciousness.
Events are triggered by frustration, crying, minor injury, or sudden pain.
During episodes he becomes cyanotic or pale, then limp, and recovers quickly without prolonged confusion.
Neurologic examination between episodes is normal, and the history is not typical of unprovoked seizures.
ECG is normal when cardiac syncope is considered.
CBC and ferritin demonstrate iron deficiency.

1.description: Breath-holding spells are benign paroxysmal events in children, typically between 6 months and 6 years. Cyanotic spells follow crying or frustration, while pallid spells often follow pain or fright. Episodes may include brief loss of consciousness and abnormal tone but resolve rapidly with a normal post-event state. Diagnosis is clinical, with evaluation for seizure or cardiac syncope if atypical. Iron deficiency should be assessed and treated.
1.management: Reassurance, caregiver education, avoidance of reinforcing the preceding behaviour, and iron supplementation when iron deficiency is present.

clues.text: A 7-month-old infant is brought in for a new rash in the diaper area.
The rash developed after prolonged exposure to wet diapers during a recent period of frequent stooling.
Examination shows erythema over the convex surfaces of the diaper region.
The infant is afebrile and otherwise well.
The rash predominantly affects areas in direct contact with the diaper while largely sparing the inguinal skin folds.
There are no satellite papules, pustules, honey-coloured crusts, or sharply demarcated perianal erythema.

1.description: Irritant Diaper Dermatitis is the most common diaper rash and results from moisture, friction, occlusion, and irritants from urine and stool. It classically affects convex diaper-contact surfaces while sparing skin folds. Diagnosis is clinical. Management focuses on keeping the area dry, reducing irritant exposure, and protecting the skin barrier.
1.management: Frequent diaper changes, diaper-free time, gentle cleansing, thick barrier paste or ointment such as zinc oxide or petrolatum, and short course low-potency topical corticosteroid only if needed.

clues.text: An infant is brought to clinic for a diaper rash that has worsened over several days.
The rash began after several days of irritation in the diaper area and has not improved with barrier cream alone.
Examination shows bright erythematous plaques involving the inguinal folds.
The infant is otherwise well, but the rash appears more inflamed than a simple irritant eruption.
The eruption extends into the inguinal skin folds and is accompanied by multiple small satellite papules and pustules.
The eruption persists despite frequent diaper changes and barrier ointment alone.

1.description: Candidal Diaper Dermatitis is a secondary yeast infection that often complicates irritant dermatitis, especially when rash persists for several days. It classically presents with beefy red plaques, involvement of skin folds, and satellite papules or pustules. Diagnosis is usually clinical. Treatment requires topical antifungal therapy along with general diaper care.
1.management: Topical antifungal therapy such as nystatin or clotrimazole plus ongoing diaper care with frequent changes and barrier protection.

1.diagnosis: Iron Deficiency Anemia Secondary to Excess Cow's Milk Intake

clues.text: A 24-month-old child is brought in for fatigue, pallor, and reduced energy.
Diet history reveals very high daily cow's milk intake from bottles and limited solid food variety.
The child appears pale but has preserved height and weight percentiles.
CBC demonstrates severe microcytic anemia.
Ferritin is low, and dietary history reveals low intake of iron-rich foods.
Caregivers report the child frequently fills up on milk between meals and refuses many offered foods.

1.description: Iron Deficiency Anemia from excess cow's milk intake is a common pediatric nutritional problem in toddlers. Large volumes of milk displace iron-rich foods and may reduce appetite for solids. Children may present with pallor, fatigue, picky eating, and microcytic anemia with low ferritin. Management includes iron replacement and dietary counselling, especially limiting milk intake and promoting iron-containing foods.
1.management: Iron supplementation, reduction of cow's milk intake to about 500 mL/day, transition away from bottles when appropriate, increased iron-rich foods, and vitamin C-containing foods to improve absorption.

clues.text: A toddler is assessed for delayed walking and progressive leg bowing.
The child was exclusively breastfed in infancy without vitamin D supplementation and has limited dietary vitamin D intake.
Physical examination reveals bowed legs and widened wrists.
Bloodwork demonstrates elevated alkaline phosphatase with low vitamin D.

clues.detail: Radiograph of the lower limb demonstrates widening, cupping, and fraying of the metaphyses at the growth plates

clues.text: Symptoms occur during a period of rapid skeletal growth when mineralization demand is high.

1.description: Nutritional Rickets is defective mineralization of growing bone, most commonly due to vitamin D deficiency. Risk factors include exclusive breastfeeding without supplementation, darker skin, northern latitude, prematurity, and limited dietary intake. Findings include delayed motor milestones, bowed legs, widened wrists, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and metaphyseal widening, cupping, or fraying on radiographs. Management is vitamin D repletion and prevention through supplementation.
1.management: Vitamin D and calcium repletion as indicated, nutritional counselling, and prevention with routine vitamin D supplementation in infancy.

clues.text: A school-aged child has recurrent frightening episodes during sleep.
Parents describe sudden screaming, thrashing, and intense agitation at night.
During events the child appears awake but is difficult to comfort or fully awaken.
Neurologic examination and daytime behaviour are normal.
Episodes occur early in the night, often within the first third of sleep.
The child has no memory of the episode the next morning and returns to sleep afterward.

1.description: Night Terrors are non-REM parasomnias that occur most often in preschool or school-aged children. They usually arise from slow-wave sleep in the first third of the night. Children may scream, appear terrified, have autonomic activation, and be difficult to awaken, but they usually have no memory afterward. Management is reassurance, safety precautions, and sleep hygiene.
1.management: Reassurance, sleep hygiene, ensuring safety during episodes, avoiding attempts to forcibly awaken the child, and scheduled awakenings if episodes are frequent and predictable.

clues.text: An older adult presents with a slowly enlarging lesion on a sun-exposed area of the face.
The lesion occasionally bleeds and “never fully heals.”
Exam shows a pearly pink papule with rolled borders.
Fine telangiectasias are visible over the surface.
Biopsy shows basaloid cells with peripheral palisading.
clues.detail: https://doi.org/10.1038/modpathol.3800512

1.description: BCC is the most common skin cancer and usually grows locally with very low metastatic risk. Presents as Pearly or translucent papule/nodule with rolled borders, surface telangiectasias, and possible central ulceration.
1.management: Biopsy, then excision, Mohs surgery for high-risk sites, or other local therapies depending on subtype/location.

clues.text: An older adult with chronic sun exposure presents with a growing tender lesion on the face
The lesion is firm, rough, and scaly.
It may ulcerate or bleed.
Exam shows an indurated hyperkeratotic plaque.
Biopsy shows invasive atypical keratinocytes extending into the dermis.
clues.detail: https://www.discc.com/conditions/

1.description: SCC arises from keratinocytes and has greater metastatic potential than BCC, especially on lips, ears, genitalia, or in immunosuppressed patients Often presents as a firm erythematous hyperkeratotic papule, plaque, or nodule that may ulcerate or crust.
1.management: Biopsy and surgical excision; Mohs for high-risk lesions.

clues.text: Patient presents with a pigmented lesion that has changed over months.
The lesion is asymmetric with irregular borders.
It has multiple colors such as brown, black, red, or blue.
Diameter is greater than 6 mm and it continues to evolve.
Biopsy shows malignant melanocytes with invasion; Breslow depth is reported.
clues.detail: https://en.wikipedia.org/

1.description: Malignant tumor of melanocytes; early recognition using ABCDE is key.
1.management: Excisional biopsy, wide local excision, staging ± sentinel lymph node biopsy depending on depth.

clues.text: Child presents with chronic itchy rash that waxes and wanes.
Significant family history of asthma, allergic rhinitis, or eczema.
Skin is dry with ill-defined erythematous scaly patches.
Infants: cheeks/extensors; older children/adults: flexures.
Severe pruritus causes sleep disturbance and excoriations.
clues.detail: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/

1.description: Chronic inflammatory skin disease driven by barrier dysfunction and immune dysregulation. Often presents as poorly demarcated erythematous patches or plaques with scale, excoriations, and lichenification in chronic disease.
1.management: Emollients, trigger avoidance, topical corticosteroids/calcineurin inhibitors for flares.

clues.text: Patient develops an itchy rash after exposure to a plant while hiking
The rash is localized to the area of contact.
Exam shows erythematous, scaly and some vesicular plaques.
Borders are confined to the region of contact with the plant
Patch testing may identify the allergen.
clues.detail: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/

1.description: Irritant or allergic dermatitis; allergic contact dermatitis is a type IV hypersensitivity reaction. Often presents as pruritic erythematous patches, papules, or vesicles sharply localized to areas of contact.
1.management: Avoid trigger, topical corticosteroids, barrier protection.

clues.text: An adult presents with rough bumps on the hands and fingers.
Lesions are skin-coloured to gray-brown and have a verrucous surface.
The lesions interrupt normal skin lines.
Tiny black dots are visible within the lesion.
Caused by human papillomavirus infection of keratinocytes.
clues.detail: https://www.webpathology.com/images

1.description: Common warts are benign HPV-related epidermal proliferations. Often presents as rough, hyperkeratotic, flesh-colored papules with a verrucous surface and pinpoint black dots.
1.management: Observation, salicylic acid, or cryotherapy.

clues.text: A child presents with multiple small painless bumps
Lesions are dome-shaped, smooth, and flesh-coloured or pink.
Each papule has central umbilication.
Lesions may spread by scratching or close contact.
Caused by a poxvirus.
clues.detail: https://www.bangkoksafeclinic.com

1.description: Molluscum is a contagious viral infection that is usually self-limited. Often presents as small, dome-shaped, flesh-colored or pink papules with central umbilication.
1.management: Reassurance/observation; curettage, cryotherapy, or topical therapy if needed.

clues.text: A patient with a history of rheumatologic disease develops joint pain and morning stiffness.
There is swelling of the elbows
Nail pitting or onycholysis is present.
Pain may involve DIP joints, spine, or entheses.
Dactylitis produces a classic sausage digit.
clues.detail: Advanced Rheumatology of Houston

1.description: Psoriatic arthritis is an inflammatory seronegative arthritis associated with psoriasis. Often presents with associated psoriatic plaques, nail pitting, onycholysis, and dactylitis ("sausage digits")
1.management: NSAIDs for mild disease; DMARDs/biologics and rheumatology referral for persistent disease.

clues.text: A patient presents with chronic recurrent scaly plaques.
Lesions are well-demarcated, erythematous, and symmetric.
Thick silvery scale is present.
Common sites include elbows, knees, scalp, umbilicus, and gluteal cleft.
Nail pitting is present.
clues.detail: https://www.webmd.com/skin-problems-and-treatments

1.description: Plaque psoriasis is an immune-mediated papulosquamous disorder. Often presents as well-demarcated erythematous plaques covered by thick silvery-white scale.
1.management: Topical corticosteroids/vitamin D analogs; phototherapy or systemic therapy for moderate-severe disease.

clues.text: A child or develops a sudden widespread rash.
The rash appears 1–3 weeks after a sore throat.
Exam shows many small drop-like scaly papules on the trunk and proximal limbs.
Lesions are thinner and smaller than classic plaque psoriasis.
Recent group A streptococcal infection is identified.
clues.detail: Image link

1.description: Guttate psoriasis is an acute psoriasis variant often triggered by streptococcal infection. Often presents as numerous small (<1 cm), drop-like erythematous scaly papules scattered on the trunk and extremities.
1.management: Treat strep if present; topical steroids or phototherapy if extensive.

clues.text: A young adult presents with patchy discoloration on back
Lesions are lighter than the surrounding skin
Fine scale is seen when the surface is scraped.
It is worse in warm, humid weather or with sweating.
KOH shows spaghetti and meatballs.
clues.detail: https://www.nhs.uk

1.description: Pityriasis versicolor is caused by Malassezia yeast affecting pigmentation. Often presents as hypo- or hyperpigmented finely scaly macules that may coalesce into larger patches.
1.management: Topical selenium sulfide or azole antifungals; oral antifungals if extensive/recurrent.

clues.text: A young adult presents with a new oval scaly patch on the trunk.
Days to weeks later, many smaller oval lesions appear.
Lesions follow skin cleavage lines on the trunk.
The rash forms a Christmas tree pattern on the back.
The initial larger lesion is called a herald patch.
clues.detail: https://hellopediatrics.com/

1.description: Pityriasis rosea is a self-limited papulosquamous eruption, often post-viral. Often presents as oval salmon-colored plaques with collarette scale following skin cleavage lines in a "Christmas tree" pattern.
1.management: Reassurance; antihistamines/topical steroids for itch.

clues.text: An adolescent presents with bumps on the face, chest, and back.
Lesions include open and closed comedones.
Some lesions are inflammatory papules and pustules.
Severe disease may have nodules, cysts, or scarring.
Pathogenesis involves follicular hyperkeratinization, sebum, Cutibacterium acnes, and inflammation.
clues.detail: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/

1.description: Acne is a disorder of the pilosebaceous unit. Often presents as a mixture of open and closed comedones, inflammatory papules, pustules, nodules, and possible scarring.
1.management: Topical retinoid ± benzoyl peroxide; antibiotics, hormonal therapy, or isotretinoin depending on severity.

clues.text: A child presents with a contagious rash around the mouth.
Lesions begin as vesicles or pustules that rupture.
Exam shows erosions with honey-coloured crust.
The child is otherwise well or mildly symptomatic.
Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause.
clues.detail: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impetigo

1.description: Impetigo is a superficial bacterial infection caused by Staph aureus or group A strep. Often presents as honey-colored crusted erosions or superficial vesicles, typically on the face or extremities.
1.management: Topical mupirocin/fusidic acid if localized; oral antibiotics if extensive.

clues.text: A patient presents with flaky itchy rash in oily skin areas.
It affects the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, ears, or chest.
Scale is greasy and yellow-white.
In infants, it appears as cradle cap.
Associated with Malassezia and improves with antifungal shampoo.
clues.detail: https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/

1.description: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic inflammatory reaction in sebaceous areas. Often presents as erythematous patches or plaques with greasy yellow scale in seborrheic areas.
1.management: Antifungal shampoos/creams; low-potency topical steroids for flares.

clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with a reticular oral rash
The patient has white changes on the inside of the cheeks that do not wipe off.
Common sites include wrists, ankles, lower back, and oral mucosa.
Fine white lacy lines are seen on the surface.
Wickham striae are present.
clues.detail: Image link

1.description: Lichen planus is an inflammatory condition classically described by the 6 Ps. Often presents as pruritic, polygonal, flat-topped, violaceous papules with fine white reticulations (Wickham striae).
1.management: Topical corticosteroids; systemic therapy if severe/widespread.

clues.text: An older fair-skinned patient presents with rough spots on sun-exposed skin.
Lesions are often easier to feel than see.
Exam shows gritty pink-red scaly papules or plaques.
Common sites include face, scalp, ears, and dorsal hands.
It is a premalignant keratinocyte lesion that can progress to SCC.
clues.detail: image link

1.description: Actinic keratosis represents UV-induced keratinocyte atypia confined to the epidermis. Often presents as rough, gritty erythematous or skin-colored papules or plaques with adherent scale.
1.management: Cryotherapy for isolated lesions; field therapy for multiple lesions.

clues.text: A patient in a crowded living shelter presents with severe generalized itch.
Itching is worse at night.
Close contacts are also itchy.
Papules and burrows are found in finger webs, wrists, axillae, waistline, or genitals.
Skin scraping shows mites, eggs, or scybala.
clues.detail: Wikipedia

1.description: Scabies is an infestation by Sarcoptes scabiei. Often presents as excoriated papules, nodules, and thin serpiginous burrows, especially in interdigital spaces and flexures.
1.management: Permethrin 5% cream or oral ivermectin; treat close contacts and wash bedding/clothing.

clues.text: A sexually active patient presents with painless genital bumps.
Lesions are soft, flesh-coloured, and exophytic.
They may have a cauliflower-like surface.
Lesions occur on genital, perianal, or mucosal skin.
Most commonly caused by HPV types 6 and 11.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: Condyloma acuminata are benign HPV-induced genital warts. Often presents as soft, flesh-colored papillomatous or cauliflower-like verrucous papules and plaques.
1.management: Patient-applied imiquimod/podophyllotoxin or clinician-applied cryotherapy/TCA; assess STI risk.

clues.text: An adult presents with thickened discoloured toenails.
Nails are yellow-white, brittle, and crumbly.
Subungual debris is present.
Tinea pedis may also be present.
KOH or culture confirms dermatophyte infection of the nail.
clues.detail: Wikipedia

1.description: Onychomycosis is a fungal infection of the nail unit. Often presents as thickened, brittle, yellow or white nails with subungual debris and onycholysis
1.management: Confirm diagnosis; oral terbinafine for most toenail dermatophyte disease if appropriate.

clues.text: A patient develops sudden itchy raised lesions.
Individual lesions come and go within 24 hours.
Lesions are edematous wheals with surrounding redness.
Triggers may include foods, medications, infections, pressure, cold, or heat.
No scale, crust, or bruising remains after lesions resolve.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: Urticaria is mast-cell mediated dermal edema. Presents as transient blanchable edematous wheals with surrounding erythema and variable shape
1.management: Second-generation antihistamines; epinephrine if anaphylaxis.

clues.text: A newborn has little or no lesion at birth but develops a red growth in the first weeks of life.
The lesion grows rapidly during infancy.
It appears as a bright red vascular plaque or nodule.
Most lesions later involute gradually.
High-risk sites include periocular, airway/beard distribution, large facial segmental, or ulcerated lesions.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: Infantile hemangiomas are benign vascular tumors with proliferative and involution phases. Bright red to deep purple vascular papule, plaque or nodule that blanches with pressure
1.management: Observation if low risk; oral propranolol for high-risk lesions.

clues.text: An adult notices small red bumps on the trunk.
Lesions are asymptomatic and increase with age.
Exam shows bright red to violaceous dome-shaped papules.
They may bleed if traumatized.
Multiple benign papillary proliferations are present.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: Cherry angiomas are common benign vascular papules.
1.management: Reassurance; cosmetic removal if desired.

clues.text: A patient develops fever, malaise, and painful skin after starting a new medication.
Common triggers include sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs, or infections.
Exam shows dusky tender macules and atypical targets.
Oral, ocular, or genital mucosal erosions are present.
Epidermal detachment involves less than 10% body surface area.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: SJS is a severe mucocutaneous reaction with keratinocyte necrosis. Often appears as painful dusky macules with blistering, mucosal erosions and epidermal detachment
1.management: Stop culprit drug; urgent hospital/burn unit care, fluids, wound care, and mucosal/eye care.

clues.text: A patient is acutely ill with fever, hypotension, and widespread painful skin.
A high-risk medication was started 1–3 weeks earlier.
Skin shows dusky erythema, bullae, and sheet-like sloughing.
Multiple mucosal surfaces are involved.
Epidermal detachment involves more than 30% body surface area.
clues.detail: Image Link

1.description: TEN is the most severe end of the SJS/TEN spectrum and has high mortality. Presents as widespread confluent erythema with sheet-like epidermal sloughing and extensive mucosal involvement
1.management: Stop culprit drug; ICU/burn unit supportive care, fluids/electrolytes, infection prevention, and ophthalmology.

1.diagnosis: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (DRESS)
aliases: Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms

clues.text: A patient develops rash and fever several weeks after starting a new medication.
Facial swelling and diffuse morbilliform eruption are present.
Lymphadenopathy is noted.
Labs show eosinophilia and abnormal liver enzymes.

1.description: Severe combined immunodeficiency is a life-threatening primary immunodeficiency characterized by absent or profoundly impaired T-cell function, with secondary B-cell dysfunction and variable NK-cell defects depending on genotype. Infants present early with failure to thrive, recurrent severe infections, thrush, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, and complications from live vaccines. TRECs are low or absent on newborn screening. SCID is fatal without prompt hematopoietic stem cell transplant.
1.management: Protective isolation, urgent immunology and infectious diseases referral, avoidance of live vaccines, antimicrobial prophylaxis including PJP prophylaxis, and hematopoietic stem cell transplant.

clues.text: A newborn boy presents with hypocalcemic seizures and cyanosis shortly after birth
Prenatal imaging identified a congenital cardiac outflow tract abnormality
Physical examination reveals abnormal facies and a cleft palate
Laboratory studies show hypocalcemia with low parathyroid hormone

clues.detail: Chest radiograph demonstrates absence or marked reduction of the normal thymic shadow, supporting thymic hypoplasia/aplasia

clues.text: Chromosomal microarray identifies a deletion affecting development of pharyngeal pouch-derived structures

1.description: DiGeorge syndrome is caused by 22q11.2 deletion leading to abnormal development of pharyngeal pouch structures. Classic findings include cardiac abnormalities, abnormal facies, thymic hypoplasia or aplasia, cleft palate, and hypocalcemia due to hypoparathyroidism. Thymic dysfunction impairs T-cell development, producing variable cellular immunodeficiency; complete DiGeorge can resemble SCID. Diagnosis is confirmed by chromosomal testing.
1.management: Manage cardiac defects and hypocalcemia, avoid live vaccines if severe T-cell deficiency is present, provide immunology follow-up, and coordinate multidisciplinary care for palate, developmental, and neuropsychiatric manifestations.

clues.text: A 2-year-old boy presents with recurrent otitis media and another chest X-ray-confirmed pneumonia
He was healthy for the first several months of life but now seems to be constantly on antibiotics
Physical examination shows pneumonia and absent tonsillar tissue
Quantitative immunoglobulins show profoundly low IgG, absent IgA, and very low IgM
T-cell numbers and CH50 are normal
Genetic testing identifies a defect in a tyrosine kinase required for B-cell maturation

1.description: X-linked agammaglobulinemia is caused by mutations in BTK, which is required for B-cell development. B-cell maturation arrests, leading to absent mature B cells, absent plasma cells, very low levels of all immunoglobulin isotypes, and absent tonsillar/lymphoid tissue. Patients typically present after 6 months of age when maternal IgG wanes, with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, otitis, pneumonia, gastrointestinal infections, and Giardia.
1.management: Lifelong immunoglobulin replacement with IVIG or SCIG, prompt treatment of infections, avoidance of selected live vaccines, and immunology follow-up.

clues.text: A 3-year-old boy presents with recurrent pneumonia, otitis media, and chronic diarrhea
He has also had an opportunistic pulmonary infection unusual for a simple antibody deficiency
Physical examination reveals poor growth and recurrent oral ulcers
Quantitative immunoglobulins show very high IgM with very low IgG and IgA
B cells are present, but class-switched antibody responses are poor
T cells fail to provide the signal needed for B-cell class switching and effective macrophage activation

1.description: Hyper-IgM syndromes are class-switch recombination disorders. In CD40L deficiency, T cells cannot provide CD40L-CD40 signaling to B cells, impairing class switching from IgM to IgG, IgA, and IgE. CD40L also supports macrophage activation, so patients can develop recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections plus opportunistic infections such as Pneumocystis jirovecii. Laboratory findings show normal or elevated IgM with low IgG and IgA.
1.management: Immunoglobulin replacement, antimicrobial prophylaxis including PJP prophylaxis when indicated, aggressive infection treatment, immunology follow-up, and hematopoietic stem cell transplant for severe CD40L deficiency.

clues.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with recurrent sinus infections and several severe pneumonias requiring hospitalization
She reports chronic diarrhea and a history of autoimmune thrombocytopenia
Physical examination reveals chronic sinus tenderness and mild splenomegaly
Quantitative immunoglobulins show low IgG with low IgA and/or IgM
Vaccine antibody titres fail to rise appropriately after immunization
Secondary causes of antibody deficiency, including medications, protein loss, malignancy, and HIV, are excluded

1.description: Common variable immunodeficiency is the most common clinically significant primary antibody deficiency seen in adults. It is characterized by low IgG plus low IgA and/or IgM with impaired specific antibody responses. Patients develop recurrent sinopulmonary infections, otitis, pneumonia, chronic Giardia, bronchiectasis, autoimmune cytopenias, and increased lymphoma risk. Many cases have no single identified genetic cause. Management centers on immunoglobulin replacement and infection prevention.
1.management: Regular immunoglobulin replacement with IVIG or SCIG, prompt antibiotics for infections, monitoring for autoimmune disease, bronchiectasis, and malignancy, and immunology follow-up.

clues.text: A patient awaiting kidney transplant is found to have high levels of pre-formed circulating antibodies against donor antigens
She has a history of prior pregnancies and blood transfusions
Physical examination is unremarkable, but transplant testing shows high panel-reactive antibody levels
Crossmatch testing suggests increased risk of antibody-mediated rejection
The clinical goal is to rapidly reduce circulating antibody concentrations before transplantation
Blood is processed to remove the plasma fraction containing pathogenic antibodies while returning cellular components

1.description: Plasmapheresis is not a disease but a key immunology intervention for conditions mediated by circulating pathogenic antibodies. Plasma is removed and discarded, taking autoantibodies or alloantibodies with it, while blood cells are returned with replacement fluid. It is useful when rapid reduction of preformed antibodies is needed, such as before transplantation with high panel-reactive antibodies or in selected severe antibody-mediated autoimmune diseases. Concurrent immunosuppression is required to reduce new antibody production.
1.management: Plasma exchange to remove circulating antibodies, combined with immunosuppression to reduce ongoing antibody production, with specialist-directed transplant or autoimmune disease management.

clues.text: A 68-year-old woman develops new groin pain after several weeks of high-dose prednisone for inflammatory vasculitis
She has difficulty walking and pain with weight-bearing
Physical examination shows reduced range of motion of the hip due to pain
Inflammatory markers are improving on prednisone, and there is no fever or septic arthritis picture

clues.detail: Imaging may demonstrate femoral head osteonecrosis; early disease can be radiographically subtle and MRI is more sensitive
clues.reference: Gaillard F, Rasuli B, Knipe H, et al. Osteonecrosis of the femoral head. Reference article, Radiopaedia.org (Accessed on 16 Jun 2026) https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-7577

clues.text: The concern is ischemic bone injury that may ultimately require joint replacement

1.description: Systemic corticosteroids are broad immunosuppressive and anti-inflammatory drugs affecting innate and adaptive immune cells. Important toxicities include infection, adrenal suppression, growth suppression, bone mineral loss, osteonecrosis, hyperglycemia, weight gain, skin thinning, mood changes, cataracts, and glaucoma. Osteonecrosis is a serious complication that can present with new hip or groin pain and may require joint replacement. Long-term steroid use should be minimized with steroid-sparing strategies when possible.
1.management: Evaluate promptly with imaging, reduce corticosteroid exposure when clinically feasible, use steroid-sparing therapy where appropriate, manage pain and mobility, and refer to orthopedics if osteonecrosis is confirmed.

clues.text: A 61-year-old woman develops recurrent sinus infections and two severe pneumonias after treatment for lymphoma
Her recent cancer therapy included a monoclonal antibody targeting a B-cell surface marker
Physical examination shows chronic sinus tenderness without T-cell opportunistic infections
Serum protein electrophoresis shows a low gamma fraction and quantitative immunoglobulins are reduced
Flow cytometry demonstrates depletion of circulating B cells
The medication reduces humoral immunity by targeting CD20-positive B cells

1.description: Rituximab is an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells. Because B cells are required for humoral immunity, patients may develop secondary antibody deficiency with recurrent sinopulmonary infections. Laboratory findings may include low immunoglobulin levels or low gamma fraction on SPEP. This illustrates how targeted immunosuppression can still have clinically important downstream immune effects.
1.management: Monitor immunoglobulins and infections, treat infections promptly, consider immunoglobulin replacement for clinically significant recurrent infections, and coordinate with hematology/immunology.

clues.text: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis presents with fatigue, nausea, and painful mouth sores after a recent medication dose increase
He has been taking a folic acid analogue used as an immunosuppressive therapy
Physical examination shows oral stomatitis without focal infection
Bloodwork demonstrates leukopenia and reduced lymphocyte counts
Liver enzymes are mildly elevated
The drug impairs DNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells, including lymphocytes and gastrointestinal epithelium

1.description: Methotrexate is a folic acid analogue that inhibits DNA synthesis, repair, and cellular replication. It is used for rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, inflammatory arthropathies, and some cancers because lymphocytes are rapidly dividing cells. Toxicities include myelosuppression, mucositis/stomatitis, gastrointestinal symptoms, liver toxicity, lung toxicity, and infection risk. New fatigue, oral ulcers, and cytopenias after dose escalation should raise concern for toxicity.
1.management: Hold or adjust methotrexate, assess CBC/liver/renal function, provide folinic acid rescue when indicated, treat complications, and use specialist guidance for future dosing.

clues.text: A 22-year-old man develops asymmetric knee and ankle pain several weeks after a diarrheal illness
He had a self-limited gastrointestinal infection before the joint symptoms began
Physical examination shows asymmetric oligoarthritis and tenderness at tendon insertions
Inflammatory markers are elevated while synovial fluid cultures are negative
Testing does not suggest immune-complex systemic disease or septic arthritis
The syndrome illustrates molecular mimicry after infection, where immune responses to microbial antigens cross-react with host tissues

1.description: Reactive arthritis is an autoimmune inflammatory arthritis that occurs after certain gastrointestinal or genitourinary infections. It is a classic example of molecular mimicry, where immune responses generated against pathogen antigens cross-react with host tissues. Patients often develop asymmetric oligoarthritis, enthesitis, and sometimes ocular or genitourinary symptoms. The joint is sterile, distinguishing it from septic arthritis.
1.management: NSAIDs for symptom control, treat any persistent triggering infection when present, physical therapy, and rheumatology-directed therapy for persistent or severe disease.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling and discomfort of her left calf that developed over the past three days
Physical examination reveals unilateral calf swelling, warmth, erythema, and tenderness along the medial aspect of the inner thigh
She returned from a 14-hour international flight one week ago and recently completed treatment for colon cancer
D-dimer is markedly elevated, while CBC and basic metabolic panel are otherwise unremarkable

clues.detail: Compression ultrasound demonstrates a non-compressible thrombus within the left popliteal vein

1.description: Deep Vein Thrombosis is the formation of a thrombus within the deep venous system, most commonly in the lower extremities. Major risk factors are summarized by Virchow’s triad: venous stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability. Common causes include immobilization, surgery, malignancy, pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and inherited thrombophilias. Patients typically present with unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema. Laboratory findings often include an elevated D-dimer, although it is nonspecific. Diagnosis is confirmed with compression ultrasonography demonstrating a non-compressible vein.
1.management: Anticoagulation with agents such as direct oral anticoagulants or heparin, with thrombolysis or thrombectomy reserved for selected high-risk cases.

clues.text: A 43-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset shortness of breath and chest pain during inhalation that began while walking to work this morning
She has no significant PMHx, only taking estrogen-containing oral contraceptives
Physical examination reveals tachycardia, tachypnea, and mild hypoxemia. Lung auscultation is otherwise unremarkable
Arterial blood gas demonstrates respiratory alkalosis, and D-dimer is markedly elevated

clues.detail: CTPA demonstrates occlusion at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery, extending to the segmental and subsegmental branches of both pulmonary artery branches. Dilated pulmonary artery and enlarged right ventricle is seen, suggestive of secondary pulmonary arterial hypertension

clues.text: Lower-extremity ultrasonography identifies a popliteal deep vein thrombosis

1.description: Pulmonary Embolism occurs when thrombotic material, usually originating from a deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremities, embolizes to the pulmonary arterial circulation. Risk factors include recent surgery, immobilization, malignancy, pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and inherited thrombophilias. Patients classically present with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxemia. Laboratory findings often include elevated D-dimer levels and respiratory alkalosis on arterial blood gas analysis. CT pulmonary angiography typically demonstrates an intraluminal filling defect.
1.management: Anticoagulation, while thrombolysis or thrombectomy may be required for hemodynamically unstable patients with massive pulmonary embolism.

clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with pain and redness along the inner aspect of her right leg that has progressively worsened over the past four days
She has a history of symptomatic varicose veins and recently returned from a prolonged road trip
Physical examination reveals a tender, erythematous, palpable cord running along the course of the great saphenous vein
CBC is normal, inflammatory markers are mildly elevated, and D-dimer is minimally increased
Compression ultrasonography demonstrates a thrombus confined to the superficial venous system without involvement of the deep veins
The patient is diagnosed with thrombosis of the great saphenous vein producing a painful palpable venous cord

1.description: Superficial Thrombophlebitis is characterized by thrombus formation and inflammation within a superficial vein, most commonly the great saphenous vein. Risk factors include varicose veins, recent intravenous catheterization, immobility, pregnancy, malignancy, and hypercoagulable states. Patients typically present with localized pain, erythema, warmth, and a tender palpable cord along the affected vein. Laboratory findings are often normal or show mild inflammatory marker elevation. Compression ultrasonography is used to confirm superficial venous involvement and exclude concurrent deep vein thrombosis.
1.management: Mobilization, compression therapy, NSAIDs, and anticoagulation for extensive disease or thrombi located near the deep venous system.

clues.text: A 63-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden severe pain and rapidly progressive swelling of her entire left leg over the past 12 hours
She was recently diagnosed with pancreatic cancer and has been largely bedridden following chemotherapy
Physical examination reveals a markedly swollen, tense, cyanotic leg with severe pain and diminished distal pulses
Bloodwork demonstrates markedly elevated D-dimer levels, hemoconcentration, and rising serum lactate
Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates extensive thrombosis involving the femoral, iliac, and popliteal veins with near-complete venous outflow obstruction
Progressive venous obstruction causes severe limb cyanosis and threatens arterial inflow, creating a high risk of venous gangrene and limb loss

1.description: Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens is a rare but life-threatening complication of extensive proximal deep vein thrombosis, most commonly involving the iliofemoral venous system. Severe venous outflow obstruction causes massive limb swelling, pain, cyanosis, and elevated compartment pressures that may ultimately compromise arterial inflow. Risk factors include malignancy, hypercoagulable states, major surgery, and prolonged immobilization. Laboratory findings often include markedly elevated D-dimer levels and rising lactate in advanced disease. Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates extensive proximal venous thrombosis.
1.management: Vascular emergency and includes immediate anticoagulation, catheter-directed thrombolysis, thrombectomy, or other interventions to prevent venous gangrene, limb loss, and death.

clues.text: A 36-year-old woman presents with recurrent spontaneous nosebleeds that have gradually become more frequent since adolescence
She reports that her father and several relatives experienced similar lifelong nosebleeds and required treatment for vascular abnormalities
Physical examination reveals multiple small blanching red lesions on the lips, tongue, and fingertips
CBC demonstrates microcytic iron-deficiency anemia, while coagulation studies are normal
CT angiography identifies multiple pulmonary arteriovenous malformations producing right-to-left shunting
Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic mutation in the ENG gene, resulting in abnormal TGF-β signaling and fragile vascular connections

1.description: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia is an autosomal dominant vascular disorder caused by mutations affecting TGF-β signaling, most commonly involving the ENG or ACVRL1 genes. It is characterized by recurrent epistaxis, mucocutaneous telangiectasias, and arteriovenous malformations involving the lungs, brain, liver, and gastrointestinal tract. Patients frequently develop chronic iron-deficiency anemia from recurrent bleeding, while coagulation studies are typically normal. Pulmonary AVMs can cause hypoxemia, paradoxical emboli, and brain abscesses. Diagnosis is based on clinical criteria and genetic testing.
1.management: Iron supplementation, treatment of bleeding lesions, embolization of AVMs, and surveillance for complications in affected organ systems

clues.text: A 19-year-old woman presents with a lifelong history of easy bruising and recurrent nosebleeds. She recently sought medical attention because of increasingly heavy menstrual bleeding
She reports that her mother and maternal grandfather experienced similar bleeding symptoms but never required major medical intervention
Physical examination reveals scattered ecchymoses and mild mucosal bleeding without hemarthroses or deep tissue hematomas
CBC demonstrates mild iron-deficiency anemia. Platelet count and PT are normal, while aPTT is normal to mildly prolonged
Further testing demonstrates reduced von Willebrand factor antigen levels and decreased ristocetin cofactor activity
Administration of desmopressin results in a significant increase in circulating von Willebrand factor and improvement in bleeding symptoms

1.description: Type 1 von Willebrand Disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder and is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. It results from a partial quantitative deficiency of von Willebrand factor, impairing platelet adhesion and reducing factor VIII stability. Patients typically present with mucocutaneous bleeding, including epistaxis, easy bruising, menorrhagia, and prolonged bleeding after procedures. Laboratory findings often show normal platelet count and PT, with normal or mildly prolonged aPTT. Diagnostic testing demonstrates reduced von Willebrand factor activity and antigen levels.
1.management: Desmopressin for many patients, antifibrinolytics for mucosal bleeding, and von Willebrand factor replacement in severe cases or major surgery.

clues.text: 24-year-old woman presents with recurrent nosebleeds, easy bruising, and prolonged bleeding following a recent dental extraction
She reports similar symptoms in multiple family members across several generations, including both men and women
Physical examination demonstrates scattered ecchymoses and ongoing oozing from the extraction site, without hemarthroses or deep muscle hematomas
CBC and platelet count are normal. PT is normal, while aPTT is mildly prolonged. Von Willebrand factor antigen levels are near normal despite significantly reduced functional activity
Ristocetin cofactor activity is markedly reduced, and multimer analysis demonstrates loss of high-molecular-weight von Willebrand factor multimers
Further classification identifies a Type 2A variant, explaining the defective platelet adhesion despite relatively preserved von Willebrand factor antigen levels

1.description: Type 2 von Willebrand Disease is an inherited bleeding disorder caused by qualitative abnormalities of von Willebrand factor that impair platelet adhesion and, in some subtypes, factor VIII stabilization. Unlike Type 1 disease, von Willebrand factor levels may be normal or only mildly reduced, but functional activity is disproportionately decreased. Patients typically present with mucocutaneous bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia, easy bruising, and prolonged bleeding after procedures. Laboratory findings often demonstrate normal platelet counts and PT, mildly prolonged aPTT, reduced ristocetin cofactor activity, and abnormal multimer analysis.
1.management: Management depends on subtype and may include desmopressin, antifibrinolytics, or von Willebrand factor concentrates for significant bleeding or surgical procedures.

clues.text: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with painful swelling of his right knee that developed after a minor fall during recess
His maternal uncle experienced recurrent bleeding episodes and required frequent transfusions during childhood
Physical examination reveals a warm, swollen knee with decreased range of motion. Several large ecchymoses are present on the extremities
CBC, platelet count, and PT are normal, while aPTT is markedly prolonged
Mixing studies correct the prolonged aPTT, and factor assays demonstrate severely reduced factor VIII activity
Genetic testing identifies a mutation in the F8 gene, and treatment with recombinant factor VIII rapidly controls the bleeding episode

1.description: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of coagulation factor VIII (Factor IX for Hemophilia B), resulting in impaired intrinsic pathway function. Patients typically present with recurrent hemarthroses, deep muscle hematomas, prolonged bleeding after trauma or surgery, and spontaneous bleeding in severe cases. Laboratory findings classically demonstrate a markedly prolonged aPTT with normal platelet count and PT. Mixing studies correct the aPTT, and factor assays confirm reduced factor VIII activity. Diagnosis is established through coagulation testing and genetic analysis of the F8 gene (F9 for Hemophilia B).
1.management: Factor VIII (or IX for B) replacement therapy, bleeding prophylaxis in severe disease, and avoidance of medications that increase bleeding risk.

clues.text: A 32-year-old woman is transferred to the intensive care unit after developing severe postpartum hemorrhage following a complicated delivery
She developed placental abruption shortly before delivery and rapidly became hypotensive despite aggressive resuscitation
Physical examination reveals diffuse oozing from intravenous catheter sites, mucosal bleeding, and extensive ecchymoses
CBC demonstrates thrombocytopenia. Coagulation studies reveal prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, and markedly elevated D-dimer levels

clues.detail: Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous schistocytes consistent with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia caused by widespread fibrin deposition within the microvasculature

clues.text: The patient develops acute kidney injury and respiratory failure due to diffuse microthrombi formation while continuing to experience severe bleeding

1.description: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation is a life-threatening disorder characterized by widespread activation of the coagulation cascade, resulting in diffuse microvascular thrombosis and consumption of platelets and clotting factors. Common triggers include sepsis, placental abruption, trauma, malignancy, and severe transfusion reactions. Patients often present with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding, including oozing from venipuncture sites, mucosal hemorrhage, and organ dysfunction. Laboratory findings classically include thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes on peripheral smear.
1.management: Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, supportive transfusion therapy when indicated, and close monitoring for life-threatening bleeding and organ failure. Platelets are not typically given.

clues.text: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain that began shortly after a motor vehicle collision
He becomes increasingly anxious and dyspneic while awaiting further assessment, and his oxygen requirements continue to rise
Physical examination reveals hypotension, tachycardia, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds over the right hemithorax
Arterial blood gas demonstrates hypoxemia and respiratory alkalosis

clues.detail: Portable chest radiography demonstrates a large right-sided pneumothorax with marked mediastinal shift toward the left

clues.text: Immediate needle decompression results in rapid improvement in blood pressure and oxygenation, followed by definitive chest tube placement

1.description: Tension Pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space through a one-way valve mechanism, causing progressively increasing intrathoracic pressure. This pressure collapses the affected lung, shifts the mediastinum, and impairs venous return to the heart, resulting in obstructive shock. Patients typically present with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypotension, tachycardia, jugular venous distension, and absent breath sounds on the affected side. Arterial blood gas often demonstrates hypoxemia. Diagnosis is primarily clinical and treatment should not be delayed for imaging.
1.management: Immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube thoracostomy to prevent cardiovascular collapse and death.

clues.text: A 35-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He complains of severe chest pain and increasing shortness of breath
He was restrained during the collision but sustained significant blunt trauma to the right side of his chest
Physical examination reveals tachycardia, hypotension, decreased breath sounds over the right hemithorax, and dullness to percussion
CBC demonstrates a falling hemoglobin level, and serum lactate is elevated

clues.detail: Portable chest radiography demonstrates increased density to the right hemithorax and well-defined homogenous density laterally. There is midline shift to the left. Multiple bilateral rib fractures are seen including fractures posteriorly and laterally on the right. Nasogastric tube and ETT are appropriately positioned. Blunting of the costophrenic angle

clues.text: Tube thoracostomy drains 1.5 liters of frank blood immediately after insertion

1.description: Hemothorax occurs when blood collects within the pleural cavity, most commonly following blunt or penetrating chest trauma. Patients typically present with chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, hypotension, decreased breath sounds, and dullness to percussion over the affected hemithorax. Laboratory findings often demonstrate acute blood loss anemia and elevated lactate in severe cases. Chest radiography and ultrasound reveal pleural fluid accumulation, while diagnosis is confirmed when pleural fluid hematocrit exceeds 50% of the peripheral blood hematocrit.
1.management: Prompt chest tube thoracostomy, volume resuscitation, and surgical intervention for ongoing hemorrhage or massive hemothorax.

clues.text: A 54-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being crushed between two vehicles. He complains of severe chest pain and increasing difficulty breathing
He sustained high-energy blunt trauma to the left side of his thorax and has become progressively more dyspneic since arrival
Physical examination reveals a segment of the left anterior chest wall moving inward during inspiration and outward during expiration
Arterial blood gas demonstrates hypoxemia, and serum lactate is mildly elevated
Chest CT demonstrates fractures of the fourth through seventh ribs in two separate locations, along with an underlying pulmonary contusion
The unstable chest wall segment contributes to respiratory failure despite supplemental oxygen, requiring positive-pressure ventilation

1.description: Flail Chest occurs when three or more consecutive ribs are fractured in at least two places, creating a free-floating segment of the chest wall. This results in paradoxical chest movement, with the flail segment moving inward during inspiration and outward during expiration. The condition usually follows high-energy blunt trauma and is frequently associated with pulmonary contusion, which is often the primary cause of hypoxemia. Laboratory findings may demonstrate hypoxemia and elevated lactate in severe cases. Diagnosis is made clinically and confirmed with imaging.
1.management: Aggressive pain control, respiratory support, pulmonary hygiene, and, in selected cases, surgical rib fixation

clues.text: A 26-year-old man develops sudden shortness of breath and confusion two days after undergoing surgical fixation of a femoral shaft fracture
He sustained multiple long-bone fractures in a motorcycle collision and initially appeared to be recovering well
Physical examination reveals tachypnea, hypoxemia, and numerous petechiae over the upper chest, neck, and axillae
Arterial blood gas demonstrates significant hypoxemia. CBC reveals thrombocytopenia and mild anemia

clues.detail: Coronal lung CT shows patchy ground-glass opacities with sharp margination between regions of involved and noninvolved lung resulting in a geographic appearance
Brain MRI reveals numerous punctate lesions producing a "starfield" appearance

1.description: Fat Embolism Syndrome is a complication of long-bone and pelvic fractures in which marrow fat enters the circulation and causes widespread microvascular obstruction and inflammation. Symptoms typically develop 24–72 hours after injury and classically include the triad of respiratory distress, neurologic dysfunction, and a petechial rash. Laboratory findings often demonstrate hypoxemia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia. Imaging may reveal diffuse bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and a characteristic “starfield” pattern on brain MRI. Diagnosis is clinical and supported by imaging and laboratory findings.
1.management: Management is primarily supportive, including oxygen therapy, mechanical ventilation when necessary, and early stabilization of fractures to reduce risk.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, recurrent respiratory infections, and early satiety over the past several months
He reports no significant lymph node enlargement but has noticed increasing abdominal fullness after eating small meals
Physical examination reveals positive Castell's sign without palpable lymphadenopathy
CBC demonstrates pancytopenia with profound monocytopenia. Peripheral blood smear reveals lymphoid cells with irregular cytoplasmic projections
Bone marrow aspiration results in a ‘dry tap,’ and biopsy demonstrates diffuse infiltration by mature B cells with a characteristic fried-egg appearance
Molecular testing identifies a BRAF V600E mutation, and treatment with a purine analog induces a durable remission

1.description: Hairy Cell Leukemia is a rare chronic B-cell malignancy characterized by infiltration of the bone marrow and spleen by abnormal lymphocytes with cytoplasmic projections. Patients typically present with fatigue, recurrent infections, splenomegaly, and early satiety. Laboratory findings classically include pancytopenia, profound monocytopenia, and circulating “hairy cells” on peripheral smear. Bone marrow aspiration often produces a dry tap because of marrow fibrosis, while biopsy reveals cells with a fried-egg appearance. Most cases harbor a BRAF V600E mutation. Diagnosis is confirmed by morphology, immunophenotyping, and molecular testing.
1.management: Purine analogs such as cladribine, which produce high rates of long-term remission.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and worsening abdominal fullness over the past year
He reports early satiety and has experienced drenching night sweats several times per week
Physical examination reveals massive splenomegaly extending well below the left costal margin
CBC demonstrates anemia with a leukoerythroblastic blood picture. Peripheral smear reveals teardrop-shaped erythrocytes and circulating nucleated red blood cells
Bone marrow aspiration results in a dry tap, while biopsy demonstrates extensive reticulin and collagen fibrosis with atypical megakaryocytic proliferation
Molecular testing identifies a JAK2 V617F mutation, and imaging demonstrates extramedullary hematopoiesis within the spleen

1.description: Primary Myelofibrosis is a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by abnormal megakaryocyte proliferation and progressive bone marrow fibrosis. Common driver mutations include JAK2, CALR, and MPL. Patients typically present with fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, anemia, and massive splenomegaly caused by extramedullary hematopoiesis. Laboratory findings often reveal anemia, a leukoerythroblastic blood picture, nucleated red blood cells, and teardrop-shaped erythrocytes. Bone marrow aspiration frequently produces a dry tap, while biopsy demonstrates reticulin and collagen fibrosis.
1.management: Management depends on risk stratification and may include JAK inhibitors such as ruxolitinib, transfusion support, and allogeneic stem cell transplantation in selected patients.

clues.text: A 52-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, early satiety, and a 10-kg unintentional weight loss over the past six months
He reports increasing abdominal fullness but denies recurrent infections or significant bleeding episodes
Physical examination reveals marked splenomegaly extending several centimeters below the left costal margin
CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 180 × 10⁹/L with marked neutrophilia, basophilia, eosinophilia, and a left shift including myelocytes and metamyelocytes
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates marked hypercellularity with granulocytic predominance and relatively few blasts
Cytogenetic analysis identifies a t(9;22) translocation producing the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein

1.description: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia is a chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm caused by the Philadelphia chromosome, a t(9;22) translocation that creates the constitutively active BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase. Patients commonly present with fatigue, weight loss, night sweats, and splenomegaly. Laboratory findings classically demonstrate marked leukocytosis with neutrophilia, basophilia, eosinophilia, and immature granulocytes at multiple stages of maturation, along with a low leukocyte alkaline phosphatase score. Bone marrow is hypercellular with granulocytic expansion. Diagnosis is confirmed by detecting the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene.
1.management: Management primarily consists of tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as imatinib, which have dramatically improved long-term survival. Due to progression to blast crisis during an accelerated phase, treatment should be started promptly.

clues.text: A 27-year-old graduate student presents with intermittent abdominal pain, fatigue, and episodes of blood in his stool several months after returning from East Africa
During his travels, he frequently swam in freshwater lakes and rivers despite being advised against doing so
Physical examination reveals mild hepatosplenomegaly without significant abdominal tenderness
CBC demonstrates marked eosinophilia. Liver enzymes are mildly elevated, and stool occult blood testing is positive
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates periportal fibrosis, and stool microscopy identifies ova with a lateral spine
The patient's symptoms are caused by a granulomatous inflammatory response to parasite eggs deposited within tissues rather than by the adult worms themselves

1.description: Schistosomiasis is a helminth infection acquired through contact with freshwater containing cercariae released from infected snails. Major species include Schistosoma mansoni, S. japonicum, and S. haematobium. Clinical manifestations result primarily from granulomatous inflammation surrounding deposited eggs rather than the adult worms. Patients may present with abdominal pain, diarrhea, hematochezia, hematuria, hepatosplenomegaly, or portal hypertension depending on the species involved. Laboratory findings commonly include eosinophilia. Stool or urine microscopy can identify characteristic eggs, and imaging may reveal periportal fibrosis in chronic disease.
1.management: Treatment consists of praziquantel, while prevention focuses on avoiding contaminated freshwater exposure in endemic regions

clues.text: A 29-year-old humanitarian worker presents with several months of progressive fatigue, intermittent fevers, and unintentional weight loss after returning from rural India
He spent several months working in rural villages and recalls frequent insect bites while sleeping outdoors at night
Physical examination reveals marked splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and generalized wasting
CBC demonstrates pancytopenia with anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia. Serum protein electrophoresis reveals polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia
Bone marrow aspiration demonstrates macrophages filled with intracellular oval organisms containing a nucleus and kinetoplast
Histologic examination reveals amastigotes within macrophages of the reticuloendothelial system, each containing a rod-shaped kinetoplast adjacent to the nucleus

1.description: Visceral Leishmaniasis is a protozoal infection transmitted by the bite of infected female sandflies. The disease is caused primarily by the Leishmania donovani complex and affects the reticuloendothelial system. Patients classically present with prolonged fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, and progressive pancytopenia. Laboratory findings often demonstrate anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia. Diagnosis is confirmed by identifying intracellular amastigotes (Leishman-Donovan bodies) within macrophages from bone marrow, splenic, or lymph node specimens. Untreated disease is often fatal.
1.management: Liposomal amphotericin B, while prevention focuses on vector control and reducing exposure to sandflies in endemic regions.

clues.text: An 18-year-old university student presents with two weeks of worsening fatigue, sore throat, and low-grade fevers
He recently moved into a college residence and reports sharing drinks with several friends during orientation events
Physical examination reveals bilateral posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, pharyngeal erythema with tonsillar exudates, and mild splenomegaly
CBC demonstrates leukocytosis with an absolute lymphocytosis, including numerous atypical lymphocytes. Mild elevations in AST and ALT are also present
The heterophile antibody test is positive, and peripheral blood smear demonstrates large reactive lymphocytes with abundant basophilic cytoplasm
Flow cytometry confirms that the atypical lymphocytes are activated CD8⁺ cytotoxic T cells responding to Epstein-Barr virus-infected B lymphocytes

1.description: Infectious Mononucleosis is a clinical syndrome caused by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), which infects B lymphocytes through the CD21 receptor. It is transmitted primarily through saliva and commonly affects adolescents and young adults. Patients classically present with fever, pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and splenomegaly. Laboratory findings include leukocytosis with atypical lymphocytosis and mild elevations in liver enzymes. Heterophile antibody testing is often positive. The atypical lymphocytes seen on peripheral smear are activated CD8⁺ cytotoxic T cells responding to infected B cells.
1.management: Management is supportive and includes avoidance of contact sports during splenic enlargement because of the risk of splenic rupture.

clues.text: A 62-year-old woman presents with recurrent bacterial skin infections and progressively worsening fatigue over the past year
She has a 20-year history of severe seropositive rheumatoid arthritis with longstanding hand deformities despite treatment
Physical examination reveals marked ulnar deviation of the fingers, rheumatoid nodules over the elbows, and a positive Castell's Sign
CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 1.8 × 10⁹/L with severe neutropenia.
Hand radiographs demonstrate advanced erosive rheumatoid arthritis with joint-space narrowing and marginal erosions
Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are strongly positive

1.description: Felty Syndrome is a rare extra-articular complication of long standing seropositive rheumatoid arthritis characterized by the classic triad of rheumatoid arthritis, splenomegaly, and neutropenia. It typically occurs in patients with severe, erosive disease and high titers of rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Patients commonly present with recurrent bacterial infections, fatigue, leg ulcers, and manifestations of advanced rheumatoid arthritis. Laboratory findings reveal persistent neutropenia, positive rheumatoid serologies, and occasionally anemia or thrombocytopenia. Diagnosis is clinical after excluding other causes of neutropenia.
1.management: Controlling rheumatoid arthritis with disease-modifying therapy, particularly methotrexate, and addressing infection risk; granulocyte colony-stimulating factor may be used in selected cases

clues.text: A 74-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, and increasing bruising over the past six months
He previously received chemotherapy and radiation therapy for lymphoma 10 years ago
Physical examination reveals scattered ecchymoses and pallor but no significant lymphadenopathy and Castell's Sign is negative.
CBC demonstrates macrocytic anemia with low reticulocyte count, thrombocytopenia, and mild neutropenia
Bone marrow biopsy reveals a hypercellular marrow with dysplastic erythroid precursors and hypolobated megakaryocytes. Peripheral smear demonstrates hyposegmented and hypogranular neutrophils
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates pseudo–Pelger-Huët neutrophils, and cytogenetic analysis identifies a deletion of chromosome 5q

1.description: Myelodysplastic Syndrome is a clonal hematopoietic stem cell disorder characterized by ineffective blood cell production, dysplasia, and risk of progression to acute myeloid leukemia. It primarily affects older adults and may occur following chemotherapy or radiation exposure. Patients commonly present with symptoms of cytopenias, including fatigue, infections, and bleeding. Laboratory findings often reveal macrocytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, and a low reticulocyte count. Bone marrow is typically hypercellular despite peripheral cytopenias. Characteristic findings include dysplastic hematopoietic cells, pseudo–Pelger-Huët neutrophils, and cytogenetic abnormalities such as deletion 5q.
1.management: Management ranges from supportive transfusions and growth factors to hypomethylating agents and allogeneic stem cell transplantation in selected patients.

clues.text: 67-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, recurrent nosebleeds, and fever over the past several weeks
He previously received chemotherapy for Hodgkin lymphoma and has recently noticed increasing bruising with minimal trauma
Physical examination reveals pallor, scattered petechiae, and multiple ecchymoses. There is no significant lymphadenopathy
CBC demonstrates anemia, thrombocytopenia, and a white blood cell count of 48 × 10⁹/L with numerous circulating blasts. Serum LDH and uric acid levels are markedly elevated
Bone marrow biopsy reveals replacement of normal marrow by myeloblasts accounting for 35% of nucleated cells. Cytochemical staining is positive for myeloperoxidase
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates needle-shaped cytoplasmic inclusions formed by fused primary granules within the blasts

1.description: Acute Myeloid Leukemia is an aggressive hematologic malignancy characterized by clonal proliferation of immature myeloid precursors that accumulate in the bone marrow and peripheral blood. Risk factors include advanced age, prior chemotherapy or radiation exposure, myelodysplastic syndromes, and certain genetic abnormalities. Patients typically present with symptoms of marrow failure, including fatigue, infections, fever, petechiae, and bleeding. Laboratory findings often demonstrate anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukocytosis with circulating blasts, elevated LDH, and hyperuricemia. Diagnosis requires ≥20% myeloblasts in the bone marrow or blood. Auer rods are a classic morphologic finding.
1.management: Induction chemotherapy, targeted therapies for specific mutations, and stem cell transplantation in selected patients
