clues.text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of progressive fatigue, intermittent fevers, and leg pain that has caused him to stop participating in sports
His parents report increasing bruising over the past month and note that he has been sleeping significantly more than usual
Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae, generalized lymphadenopathy, and mild hepatosplenomegaly
CBC demonstrates anemia, thrombocytopenia, and a white blood cell count of 65 × 10⁹/L with numerous circulating blasts. Serum LDH and uric acid levels are elevated

Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates replacement of normal marrow by lymphoblasts accounting for more than 25% of nucleated cells. Flow cytometry demonstrates expression of terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT)
Flow cytometry demonstrates CD10 positivity on precursor B lymphoblasts, and cytoplasmic Auer rods are absent

1.description: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia is the most common childhood cancer and results from malignant proliferation of immature lymphoid precursors within the bone marrow. Patients typically present with symptoms of marrow failure, including fatigue, pallor, infections, fever, petechiae, and bleeding, along with lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory findings often reveal anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukocytosis with circulating blasts, elevated LDH, and hyperuricemia. Diagnosis is established by bone marrow examination demonstrating lymphoblasts and immunophenotyping showing markers such as TdT and CD10 in precursor B-cell disease. Prognosis is generally excellent in children, with cure rates exceeding 80–90% in many modern treatment protocols. Intrathecal CNS prophylaxis given to all patients given high rate of CNS infiltration (20%).
1.management: Treatment consists of multi-agent chemotherapy with central nervous system prophylaxis.

clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with worsening fatigue, confusion, and severe headaches that developed over several days
She recently recovered from a viral illness and has no personal history of bleeding disorders
Physical examination reveals scattered petechiae, mild jaundice, and fluctuating neurologic deficits
CBC demonstrates severe thrombocytopenia and anemia. Lactate dehydrogenase, indirect bilirubin, and reticulocyte count are elevated, while coagulation studies are normal
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous schistocytes consistent with red blood cell fragmentation within the microvasculature
ADAMTS13 activity is markedly reduced, resulting in accumulation of ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers that promote widespread platelet-rich microthrombi

1.description: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura is a life-threatening thrombotic microangiopathy caused by severe deficiency of ADAMTS13, a metalloprotease responsible for cleaving ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers. The deficiency is usually acquired through autoantibodies but may rarely be inherited. Patients classically present with thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, and fever, although the full pentad is uncommon. Laboratory findings include thrombocytopenia, schistocytes, elevated LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin, reticulocytosis, and normal PT and aPTT. Diagnosis is supported by markedly reduced ADAMTS13 activity.
1.management: Management is a hematologic emergency requiring urgent plasma exchange, corticosteroids, and often immunosuppressive therapy such as rituximab.

clues.text: 28-year-old woman presents with easy bruising and recurrent nosebleeds that began several weeks ago
She reports recovering from an upper respiratory viral infection approximately one month ago and is otherwise healthy.
Physical examination reveals petechiae on the lower extremities and scattered ecchymoses. There is no lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly
CBC demonstrates a platelet count of 12 × 10⁹/L with otherwise normal hemoglobin and white blood cell counts. PT and aPTT are normal
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates markedly reduced platelets with occasional large immature platelets but no schistocytes or blasts
Autoantibodies directed against platelet membrane glycoproteins GPIIb/IIIa result in splenic macrophage-mediated platelet destruction

1.description: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura is an autoimmune disorder characterized by antibody-mediated destruction of platelets, most commonly through antibodies directed against platelet glycoproteins such as GPIIb/IIIa. It may occur after viral infections, in association with autoimmune diseases, or as a primary condition. Patients typically present with petechiae, purpura, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, and menorrhagia. Laboratory findings classically demonstrate isolated thrombocytopenia with normal hemoglobin, white blood cell counts, PT, and aPTT. Peripheral smear may reveal large immature platelets reflecting increased marrow production.
1.management: Management depends on bleeding severity and platelet count and may include corticosteroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, rituximab, thrombopoietin receptor agonists, or splenectomy in refractory cases.

clues.text: 71-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, early satiety, and unintentional weight loss over the past eight months
He reports increasing abdominal fullness but denies recurrent infections, bleeding, or significant lymph node enlargement
Physical examination reveals moderate splenomegaly without palpable lymphadenopathy

CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 65 × 10⁹/L with marked mature neutrophilia. Differential count shows >80% segmented neutrophils and bands, with minimal immature granulocytes. Hemoglobin and platelet counts are mildly reduced
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates marked granulocytic hyperplasia without significant dysplasia or blast excess. Cytogenetic testing is negative for the Philadelphia chromosome
Next-generation sequencing identifies an activating mutation in the CSF3R gene

1.description: Chronic Neutrophilic Leukemia is a rare myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by persistent mature neutrophilic leukocytosis and clonal proliferation of the granulocytic lineage. Patients commonly present with fatigue, weight loss, and splenomegaly. Laboratory findings reveal marked neutrophilia with predominantly mature neutrophils, minimal left shift, and few circulating blasts. Bone marrow demonstrates granulocytic hyperplasia without significant dysplasia. Unlike chronic myeloid leukemia, the Philadelphia chromosome and BCR-ABL1 fusion gene are absent. The defining molecular feature is an activating mutation in CSF3R, the receptor for granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.
1.management: Cytoreductive therapy, JAK-pathway-targeted treatments in selected cases, and allogeneic stem cell transplantation for eligible patients with advanced disease.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents with worsening headaches, dizziness, and generalized fatigue over the past several months
He reports intense itching after hot showers and recently experienced a painful episode of swelling and redness in his big toe
Physical examination reveals facial plethora, conjunctival injection, and mild splenomegaly
CBC demonstrates hemoglobin of 196 g/L, hematocrit of 0.61, leukocytosis, and thrombocytosis. Serum erythropoietin levels are suppressed
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates a hypercellular marrow with trilineage proliferation involving erythroid, granulocytic, and megakaryocytic precursors
Molecular testing identifies a JAK2 V617F mutation resulting in constitutive activation of the JAK-STAT signaling pathway

1.description: Polycythemia Vera is a chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by autonomous overproduction of red blood cells, often accompanied by leukocytosis and thrombocytosis. More than 95% of patients harbor a JAK2 mutation, most commonly JAK2 V617F, leading to constitutive JAK-STAT pathway activation. Patients may present with headaches, dizziness, facial plethora, aquagenic pruritus, erythromelalgia, thrombosis, or gout. Laboratory findings include elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit, low serum erythropoietin levels, and often panmyelosis. Bone marrow is hypercellular with trilineage proliferation. Major complications include thrombosis, myelofibrosis, and transformation to acute myeloid leukemia.
1.management: Periodic phlebotomy, low-dose aspirin, and cytoreductive therapy such as hydroxyurea for high-risk patients.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with recurrent headaches, intermittent blurry vision, and burning pain in her hands and feet that worsens with warmth
She has no history of infection or inflammatory disease but recently experienced a transient episode of unilateral arm weakness that resolved within minutes
Physical examination reveals mild splenomegaly and erythema with tenderness of the fingertips
CBC demonstrates a platelet count of 1,100 × 10⁹/L with otherwise near-normal hemoglobin and white blood cell counts. Iron studies are normal
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates marked megakaryocytic hyperplasia with enlarged mature megakaryocytes showing hyperlobulated nuclei
Molecular testing identifies a CALR mutation

1.description: Essential Thrombocythemia is a chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by clonal overproduction of platelets due to abnormal megakaryocyte proliferation. Common driver mutations involve JAK2, CALR, or MPL. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with headaches, visual disturbances, erythromelalgia, thrombosis, or bleeding. Laboratory findings classically demonstrate persistent thrombocytosis, often exceeding 450 × 10⁹/L and sometimes surpassing 1,000 × 10⁹/L. Bone marrow biopsy reveals increased numbers of enlarged hyperlobulated megakaryocytes. Diagnosis requires exclusion of reactive thrombocytosis and other myeloproliferative neoplasms. Normal iron studies help distinguish ET from reactive thrombocytosis secondary to iron deficiency. Major complications include arterial and venous thrombosis, bleeding, and progression to myelofibrosis or acute leukemia
1.management: Low-dose aspirin for symptom control and thrombosis prevention, with cytoreductive therapy such as hydroxyurea used in higher-risk patients.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, chills, and profound fatigue that began earlier in the day
She is currently receiving induction chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia and completed her most recent treatment cycle 10 days ago
Physical examination reveals a temperature of 39.2°C and mild oral mucositis, but no obvious source of infection
CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 0.4 × 10⁹/L with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.2 × 10⁹/L
Blood cultures are obtained and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics are initiated immediately. Chest radiography is unremarkable
Despite the absence of a clear infectious focus, antipseudomonal intravenous antibiotics are administered within one hour

1.description: Febrile Neutropenia is a life-threatening complication of chemotherapy and other causes of severe neutropenia. It is defined by fever in the setting of an absolute neutrophil count below 0.5 × 10⁹/L or anticipated to fall below that level. Because neutrophils are essential for bacterial and fungal defense, patients may develop severe infections with few localizing signs. Laboratory findings reveal neutropenia, and blood cultures should be obtained promptly. Imaging may be normal despite significant infection. Common pathogens include Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacterales, and gram-positive cocci.
1.management: Medical emergency requiring immediate empiric broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics with antipseudomonal coverage, supportive care, and consideration of antifungal therapy if fever persists.

clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with high fever, severe sore throat, and painful mouth ulcers that developed abruptly over the past three days
She was recently started on a new medication for hyperthyroidism six weeks ago
Physical examination reveals extensive oral ulcerations with necrotic-appearing gingival lesions and a temperature of 39.4°C
CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 0.8 × 10⁹/L with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.05 × 10⁹/L. Hemoglobin and platelet counts are normal
Bone marrow examination demonstrates marked depletion of granulocytic precursors with preservation of erythroid and megakaryocytic lineages.
Review of her medications identifies methimazole therapy

1.description: Agranulocytosis is a potentially life-threatening condition characterized by profound neutropenia, typically defined as an absolute neutrophil count below 0.5 × 10⁹/L and often below 0.1 × 10⁹/L. The most common causes are medications, particularly methimazole, propylthiouracil, and clozapine, although autoimmune disease, infections, and bone marrow disorders may also be responsible. Patients typically present with fever, pharyngitis, oral ulcers, and severe bacterial or fungal infections. Laboratory findings reveal severe isolated neutropenia with otherwise preserved blood cell lines.
1.management: Management requires immediate discontinuation of the offending agent, broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected infection, supportive care, and consideration of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) in severe cases.

clues.text: 24-year-old man is referred to hematology after routine blood work repeatedly demonstrates a low white blood cell count despite feeling completely well
He has no history of recurrent infections, oral ulcers, hospitalizations, or unusual infectious complications. Similar blood count findings have been reported in several family members
Physical examination is unremarkable, with no lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, mucosal lesions, or signs of infection
CBC demonstrates an absolute neutrophil count of 1.1 × 10⁹/L on multiple occasions over several years. Hemoglobin, platelet count, and differential are otherwise normal
Peripheral blood smear is normal, and bone marrow examination demonstrates normal granulopoiesis without dysplasia or maturation arrest
Genetic testing identifies the ACKR1 genotype

1.description: Duffy-Associated Neutrophil Count is a common inherited hematologic variant associated with the Duffy-null (ACKR1) genotype. Individuals have chronically lower baseline absolute neutrophil counts, often between 1.0–1.5 × 10⁹/L and sometimes lower, despite normal bone marrow function and preserved immunity. The condition is particularly common among people with ancestry from regions where the Duffy-null genotype is prevalent. Patients are asymptomatic and do not have an increased risk of infection. Laboratory findings demonstrate isolated chronic neutropenia with otherwise normal blood counts. Diagnosis is based on clinical context, exclusion of pathologic causes, and, when needed, confirmation of the ACKR1 genotype.
1.management: No treatment is required, and recognition helps prevent unnecessary investigations or medication discontinuation.

clues.text: A 64-year-old woman presents with several painless lumps in her neck that she first noticed more than a year ago. The lymph nodes have slowly enlarged but have not caused significant discomfort
She reports occasional fatigue but remains active and denies significant fevers or recent infections. Several enlarged lymph nodes have waxed and waned in size over time
Physical examination reveals multiple non-tender cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymph nodes. Mild splenomegaly is present
CBC demonstrates mild normocytic anemia and an elevated LDH level. Peripheral blood smear is otherwise unremarkable. PET-CT demonstrates widespread lymphadenopathy.

clues.detail: Excisional lymph node biopsy reveals closely packed neoplastic follicles. Taken from https://www.pathologyoutlines.com/topic/lymphomafollicularnodalusual.html

clues.text: Cytogenetic analysis identifies a t(14;18) translocation resulting in overexpression of the anti-apoptotic BCL2 protein

1.description: Follicular Lymphoma is the most common indolent non-Hodgkin lymphoma and arises from germinal center B cells. It typically presents with painless generalized lymphadenopathy and follows a slow, relapsing-remitting course. Laboratory findings may reveal mild anemia and elevated LDH, although blood counts are often normal initially. Lymph node biopsy demonstrates a follicular growth pattern composed of centrocytes and centroblasts. The hallmark molecular abnormality is the t(14;18) translocation, which places BCL2 under the control of the immunoglobulin heavy-chain promoter, preventing apoptosis. Transformation to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is a major long-term complication.
1.management: Management ranges from active surveillance in asymptomatic patients to immunochemotherapy, commonly including rituximab.

clues.text: A 19-year-old man presents to the emergency department with nausea, vomiting, profound fatigue, and decreased urine output that developed over several days
He reports a rapidly enlarging abdominal mass and drenching night sweats over the past month but has not yet received any treatment
Physical examination reveals dehydration, tachycardia, and a large palpable abdominal mass
Bloodwork demonstrates hyperuricemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperkalemia, elevated LDH, and rising creatinine
CT abdomen demonstrates bulky intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy
The patient is provided aggressive IV hydration and put under cardiac monitoring

1.description: Spontaneous Tumor Lysis Syndrome is a life-threatening oncologic emergency caused by rapid destruction of malignant cells before initiation of chemotherapy. It occurs most commonly in highly proliferative tumors such as Burkitt lymphoma, acute leukemias, and other aggressive hematologic malignancies with large tumor burdens. Release of intracellular contents leads to hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, secondary hypocalcemia, and acute kidney injury. Laboratory findings typically include markedly elevated LDH and electrolyte abnormalities. Diagnosis is based on characteristic metabolic derangements in the setting of malignancy.
1.management: Aggressive intravenous hydration, cardiac monitoring, correction of electrolyte abnormalities, and uric acid-lowering therapy such as rasburicase. Immediate recognition is critical because fatal arrhythmias and renal failure may occur.

clues.text: A 72-year-old man presents for evaluation of progressive fatigue and several painless lumps in his neck that were discovered incidentally by his family
He reports no recent infections but has noticed increasing fullness in his abdomen and occasional night sweats over the past year
Physical examination reveals generalized non-tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy along with moderate splenomegaly
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous small mature-appearing lymphocytes along with fragile disrupted lymphocytes created during slide preparation
CBC demonstrates a white blood cell count of 85 × 10⁹/L with marked absolute lymphocytosis. Hemoglobin is mildly decreased, and serum immunoglobulin levels are reduced
Flow cytometry demonstrates a clonal B-cell population expressing CD5, CD19, CD20(dim), and CD23

1.description: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia is the most common adult leukemia in Western countries and is characterized by the accumulation of mature but functionally incompetent monoclonal B lymphocytes. Patients are often asymptomatic at diagnosis but may develop fatigue, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, recurrent infections, and constitutional symptoms. Laboratory findings typically reveal persistent absolute lymphocytosis, hypogammaglobulinemia, and occasionally anemia or thrombocytopenia. Peripheral blood smear classically demonstrates smudge cells. Diagnosis is confirmed by flow cytometry showing a clonal B-cell population expressing CD5, CD19, CD20(dim), and CD23. Major complications include autoimmune hemolytic anemia, recurrent infections, and transformation to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (Richter transformation).
1.management: Management ranges from active surveillance in asymptomatic patients to targeted therapies such as BTK inhibitors or BCL2 inhibitors in symptomatic disease.

clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive fatigue, shortness of breath on exertion, and dark urine that developed over the past several weeks
She has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus and recently experienced a flare of her disease
Physical examination reveals pallor, mild scleral icterus, and splenomegaly
CBC demonstrates normocytic anemia. Reticulocyte count, LDH, and indirect bilirubin are elevated, while haptoglobin is undetectable
Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous spherocytes without schistocytes
The direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test is positive for IgG-coated red blood cells

1.description: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia is a disorder in which autoantibodies target red blood cells, resulting in premature destruction and hemolytic anemia. Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, the most common form, is typically mediated by IgG antibodies and is associated with conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and certain medications. Patients commonly present with fatigue, jaundice, dark urine, and splenomegaly. Laboratory findings include anemia, elevated reticulocyte count, increased LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin, and low haptoglobin. Peripheral smear often demonstrates spherocytes. The defining diagnostic test is a positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test.
1.management: Management generally includes corticosteroids, treatment of the underlying condition, rituximab, and occasionally splenectomy for refractory disease.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with progressively worsening lower back pain and fatigue over the past six months. The pain is most severe at night and is not relieved by rest
He reports increasing fatigue and recurrent respiratory infections over the past year
Physical examination reveals pallor and focal tenderness over the lumbar vertebrae without lymphadenopathy
CBC demonstrates normocytic anemia. Serum calcium and creatinine are elevated, while serum protein electrophoresis reveals a monoclonal IgG spike

clues.detail: Xray demonstrates multiple foci of endosteal scalloping (erosion of the inner cortex) within this long bone, consistent with lytic myelomatous lesions

clues.text: Urine protein electrophoresis identifies monoclonal free light chains, and bone marrow plasma cells demonstrate abundant cytoplasmic immunoglobulin with eccentric nuclei and a perinuclear hof. Rouleaux formation is seen on blood smear.

1.description: Multiple Myeloma is a clonal plasma cell malignancy characterized by monoclonal immunoglobulin production and bone marrow infiltration. Patients commonly present with bone pain, fatigue, recurrent infections, and manifestations of CRAB features: hypercalcemia, renal dysfunction, anemia, and lytic bone lesions. Laboratory findings include an M-protein spike on serum protein electrophoresis, elevated serum free light chains, anemia, hypercalcemia, and renal impairment. Urine studies may demonstrate Bence Jones proteins. Imaging classically reveals punched-out lytic lesions. Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates clonal plasma cells, often exceeding 10% of marrow cellularity.
1.management: Combination chemotherapy, immunomodulatory agents, proteasome inhibitors, anti-CD38 monoclonal antibodies, bisphosphonates, and autologous stem cell transplantation in eligible patients.

clues.text: A 70-year-old man is referred to hematology after routine bloodwork performed during an annual physical reveals an elevated total serum protein level
He feels completely well and denies fatigue, weight loss, recurrent infections, or constitutional symptoms
Physical examination is unremarkable, with no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, bone tenderness, or neurologic deficits
Serum protein electrophoresis demonstrates a monoclonal IgG spike measuring 1.5 g/dL. CBC, calcium, creatinine, and serum albumin are normal
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates 4% clonal plasma cells, and skeletal imaging reveals no lytic lesions
The monoclonal protein is present, but the bone marrow plasma cell percentage remains below 10%

1.description: Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance is a premalignant plasma cell disorder characterized by the presence of a monoclonal immunoglobulin without evidence of end-organ damage. It is common in older adults and is usually discovered incidentally during evaluation of elevated serum protein levels. Diagnostic criteria include an M-protein concentration below 3 g/dL, fewer than 10% clonal plasma cells in the bone marrow, and absence of CRAB features or myeloma-defining events. Patients are asymptomatic and require no immediate treatment.
1.management: Periodic monitoring because MGUS carries an approximately 1% annual risk of progression to multiple myeloma, amyloidosis, or another plasma cell or lymphoproliferative disorder.

clues.text: A 66-year-old woman is referred to hematology after an abnormal protein level is identified on routine bloodwork performed during an annual health examination
She feels well and denies fatigue, weight loss, recurrent infections, or constitutional symptoms
Physical examination is unremarkable, with no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, focal bone tenderness, or neurologic deficits
Serum protein electrophoresis demonstrates an M-protein concentration of 3.8 g/dL. CBC, calcium, and creatinine are all within normal limits
Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates 18% clonal plasma cells. Whole-body low-dose CT reveals no lytic bone lesions
The patient is managed with close observation rather than immediate therapy

1.description: Smoldering Multiple Myeloma is an intermediate plasma cell disorder that lies between monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) and symptomatic multiple myeloma. It is characterized by a serum monoclonal protein ≥3 g/dL and/or 10–60% clonal plasma cells in the bone marrow without myeloma-defining events or end-organ damage. Patients are asymptomatic and lack CRAB features (hypercalcemia, renal dysfunction, anemia, and bone lesions). Laboratory findings reveal a monoclonal gammopathy, while imaging demonstrates no lytic lesions. Diagnosis requires exclusion of active myeloma.
1.management: Management consists primarily of risk stratification and close surveillance, as patients remain at significant risk of progression to symptomatic multiple myeloma over time.

clues.text: An asymptomatic 42 year-old female presenting for a routine check-up has a blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg.
She is a healthy weight and physically active and denies smoking
Physical exam is unremarkable
Blood work (Triglycerides, cholesterol, renal function, electrolytes and HbA1c) is all within normal range
Repeat blood pressure measurements over the next 3 months remain consistently elevated at 138–145/88–92 mmHg.
She reports that both of her parents were diagnosed with high blood pressure in middle age.

1.description: Essential Hypertension is a diagnosis of exclusion. It is a term for hypertension where no underlying cause is found.
1.management: Lifestyle modifications, weight loss, exercise, sodium restriction and medications such as ACE Inhibitors, ARBs, Thiazides etc. DASH diet.

clues.text: A 57 year old man presents with recurrent episodes of chest discomfort
He says the pain improves with rest
He describes the pain as pressure, tightness, and a 'squeezing' feeling
Physical exam is normal
Troponin is normal.
The pain subsides with sublingual nitroglycerin

1.description: Angina Pectoris is caused by transient myocardial ischemia without permanent myocardial injury.
1.management: Risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet therapy when indicated, statins, beta blockers, nitrates, and evaluation for revascularization if needed.

clues.text: A 72 year old patient complains of getting tired more easily over the past few months
They become short of breath after walking a short distance
They require more pillows at night to sleep comfortably
On physical exam, the ankles are swollen
Crackles are heard at lung bases
An echocardiogram shows a reduced ejection fraction

1.description: Heart failure is a chronic condition where the heart is unable to pump enough blood to meet the body's demands. Common causes include coronary artery disease, HTN, valvular disease and previous MI
1.management: Restrict sodium and fluid intake, administer diuretics and guideline directed medical therapy such as ACEi, ARBs, B-blockers, SGLT2 inhibitors etc.

clues.text: A 58 year old man suddenly develops secere chest presure while shoveling
Pain radiates to left arm
Pain also radiates to jaw
Patient reports profound shortness of breath
Significant history of hypertension, smoking and diabetes

clues.detail: https://ecgmadesimple.ca/1-1/

1.description: Myocardial infarction results from an interruption of coronary blood flow leading to ischemia and necrosis. Most MIs are caused by rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque followed by thrombus formation.
1.management: Obtain 12-lead ECG and cardiac biomarkers, administer aspirin unless contraindicated, nitroglycerin, establish IV access and initiate continous cardiac monitoring. Arrange urgent reperfusion therapy and monitor closely

clues.text: A 67 year old woman reports feeling unusually tired
She developed nausea and a feeling of indigestion after dinner
She notices some discomfort in her upper back
Family members note that she is pale, sweaty and not acting like her self
She becomes increasingly short of breath
In the ED, her ECG shows ischemic changes and her troponin is elevated

1.description: Myocardial infarction results from an interruption of coronary blood flow leading to ischemia and necrosis. Most MIs are caused by rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque followed by thrombus formation. MI in women may present with atypical symptoms which can delay recognition and treatment. Instead of classic crushing chest pain, women may experience fatigue, nausea, shortness of breath, indigestion, back pain or jaw pain.
1.management: Obtain 12-lead ECG and cardiac biomarkers, administer aspirin unless contraindicated, nitroglycerin, establish IV access and initiate continous cardiac monitoring. Arrange urgent reperfusion therapy and monitor closely

clues.text: A 74-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe pain in her right leg that began while watching television two hours ago
She has a history of atrial fibrillation and stopped taking her anticoagulant medication several weeks ago because of concerns about bruising
Physical examination reveals a pale, cool right foot with diminished sensation and absent distal pulses
Bloodwork demonstrates an elevated serum lactate level and rising creatine kinase concentration

clues.detail: CT angiography demonstrates an abrupt occlusion of the right popliteal artery with minimal collateral circulation

clues.text: The affected limb demonstrates pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, and poikilothermia

1.description: Acute Limb Ischemia is a vascular emergency caused by a sudden reduction in arterial blood flow to a limb, most commonly due to embolism from atrial fibrillation or thrombosis of pre-existing peripheral arterial disease. Patients classically present with the “6 Ps”: pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, and poikilothermia. Laboratory findings may reveal elevated lactate and creatine kinase due to tissue ischemia. Diagnosis is confirmed with vascular imaging such as CT angiography, which identifies the site of arterial occlusion.
1.management: Immediate management includes systemic anticoagulation with heparin and urgent revascularization through thrombectomy, thrombolysis, or surgical bypass to prevent irreversible tissue loss and amputation.

clues.text: A 69-year-old man presents with progressively worsening calf pain that occurs after walking two city blocks and reliably resolves after several minutes of rest
He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and longstanding type 2 diabetes mellitus
Physical examination reveals diminished dorsalis pedis pulses, cool lower extremities, and loss of hair over the shins
Laboratory testing demonstrates elevated LDL cholesterol and an HbA1c of 9.2%
An ankle-brachial index is 0.58, and CT angiography demonstrates multilevel stenoses of the superficial femoral and popliteal arteries
Exercise testing reproduces symptoms of intermittent claudication

1.description: Peripheral Artery Disease is a manifestation of systemic atherosclerosis characterized by progressive narrowing of peripheral arteries, most commonly affecting the lower extremities. Major risk factors include smoking, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and advanced age. Patients classically present with intermittent claudication, defined as exertional muscle pain relieved by rest. Physical examination may reveal diminished pulses, cool extremities, trophic skin changes, and nonhealing ulcers. Laboratory findings often reflect underlying cardiovascular risk factors. Diagnosis is commonly established with an ankle-brachial index below 0.90 and confirmed with vascular imaging.
1.management: Smoking cessation, supervised exercise therapy, statins, antiplatelet therapy, risk-factor modification, and revascularization for severe or limb-threatening disease.

clues.text: A 73-year-old man presents with a sudden worsening of chronic left calf pain. For years he experienced discomfort only when walking, but today he developed severe pain in the leg while resting
He has a long history of smoking, diabetes mellitus, and describes intermittent claudication
Physical examination reveals a cool foot with diminished sensation and weak distal pulses. Comparison with prior records shows that pulses were reduced but still palpable at baseline
Bloodwork demonstrates elevated serum lactate and rising creatine kinase levels compared with prior measurements

clues.detail: CT angiography demonstrates distal outflow artery (arrowhead) enhanced by existing collaterals (arrows) in the left extremity

clues.text: Further history taking reveals the patient had previously documented peripheral artery disease

1.description: Acute-on-Chronic Limb Ischemia occurs when a sudden reduction in arterial blood flow develops in a limb with pre-existing peripheral artery disease. The most common mechanism is thrombosis of a chronically stenotic atherosclerotic artery. Patients often have a history of intermittent claudication and then experience an abrupt worsening characterized by rest pain, sensory deficits, and threatened limb viability. Laboratory findings may demonstrate elevated lactate and creatine kinase due to tissue ischemia. CT angiography typically reveals severe chronic atherosclerotic disease with superimposed acute thrombosis. Unlike embolic acute limb ischemia, collateral circulation is often present.
1.management: Immediate anticoagulation, urgent vascular assessment, and revascularization through endovascular or surgical intervention.

clues.text: 67-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling of both lower legs and a feeling of heaviness that worsens throughout the day
She worked as a cashier for over 30 years and has a history of multiple pregnancies
Physical examination reveals bilateral pitting edema, dilated tortuous superficial veins, lipodermatosclerosis, and venous stasis dermatitis
CBC, renal function, liver enzymes, serum albumin, and BNP are all within normal limits
Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates reflux within the great saphenous vein and incompetent venous valves without evidence of deep vein thrombosis
A shallow ulcer develops superior to the medial malleolus, accompanied by surrounding hemosiderin deposition

1.description: Chronic Venous Insufficiency is a common vascular disorder caused by failure of venous valves, resulting in venous reflux, chronic venous hypertension, and impaired venous return from the lower extremities. Risk factors include advanced age, obesity, pregnancy, prolonged standing, prior deep vein thrombosis, and varicose veins. Patients typically present with leg heaviness, edema, varicosities, skin hyperpigmentation, and venous ulcers. Laboratory studies are generally normal and are primarily used to exclude systemic causes of edema. Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates venous reflux and valvular incompetence.
1.management: Compression therapy, leg elevation, exercise, weight reduction, wound care, and endovenous or surgical interventions for selected patients.

clues.text: A 26-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe lower leg pain several hours after sustaining a tibial shaft fracture during a soccer match
The fracture was reduced and splinted in the emergency department, but his pain has continued to worsen despite escalating doses of opioid analgesics
Physical examination reveals a tense swollen calf and severe pain with passive dorsiflexion of the ankle
Bloodwork demonstrates elevated creatine kinase and mild hyperkalemia
Passive stretching of the affected muscle group reproduces excruciating pain
Delta pressure (diastolic BP − compartment pressure) is less than 30 mmHg

1.description: Compartment Syndrome is a surgical emergency caused by increased pressure within a closed fascial compartment, resulting in impaired capillary perfusion and tissue ischemia. Common causes include fractures, crush injuries, reperfusion injury, burns, and tight casts. Patients classically present with severe pain out of proportion to the injury, pain with passive stretch, tense swelling, paresthesias, and progressive neurologic deficits. Laboratory findings may reveal elevated creatine kinase, hyperkalemia, and evidence of rhabdomyolysis. Diagnosis is primarily clinical but may be supported by compartment pressure measurements.
1.management: Definitive treatment is emergent fasciotomy to prevent irreversible muscle necrosis, nerve injury, limb loss, and systemic complications.

clues.text: A 19-year-old man presents to the emergency department after experiencing a sudden episode of palpitations associated with lightheadedness while playing basketball
He reports several similar episodes since adolescence that begin and end abruptly without an obvious trigger
During the episode, his heart rate is 210 beats/min with a regular rhythm. Between episodes, physical examination is unremarkable
CBC, electrolytes, thyroid function tests, and cardiac biomarkers are normal

clues.detail: Electrocardiography obtained while asymptomatic demonstrates a short PR interval and a slurred upstroke of the QRS complex

clues.text: Electrophysiologic studies identify an accessory conduction pathway connecting the atria and ventricles, allowing impulses to bypass the AV node

1.description: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome is a pre-excitation syndrome caused by an accessory atrioventricular conduction pathway, known as the Bundle of Kent, that bypasses the AV node. This predisposes patients to atrioventricular reentrant tachycardia and, less commonly, atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular conduction. Patients typically present with episodic palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or supraventricular tachycardia. Laboratory studies are generally normal. The classic ECG findings are a short PR interval, delta wave, and widened QRS complex. Definitive diagnosis is established through electrophysiologic testing.
1.management: Management may include vagal maneuvers, antiarrhythmic therapy in selected cases, and catheter ablation, which is often curative.

clues.text: A 26-year-old man presents for evaluation after an abnormal ECG was discovered during a pre-employment medical examination. He denies chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, or syncope
He is a competitive endurance athlete who trains several hours daily and has no history of cardiovascular disease
Physical examination reveals a resting heart rate of 52 beats/min with an otherwise normal cardiovascular examination
CBC, electrolytes, thyroid function tests, and cardiac biomarkers are within normal limits

clues.detail: 1:1 conduction with a PR interval greater than 200 ms

clues.text: High vagal tone slows AV nodal conduction without causing dropped beats

1.description: First-Degree Atrioventricular Block is the mildest form of atrioventricular conduction delay and is characterized by a prolonged PR interval greater than 200 milliseconds with preservation of 1:1 AV conduction. The delay most commonly occurs within the AV node and may be seen in healthy individuals with high vagal tone, particularly athletes. Other causes include AV nodal blocking medications, ischemic heart disease, myocarditis, and degenerative conduction disease. Patients are usually asymptomatic. Laboratory findings are generally normal unless an underlying cause is present. ECG demonstrates a prolonged PR interval without dropped beats.
1.management: Treatment is typically unnecessary unless associated with symptomatic conduction disease or an underlying reversible cause.

clues.text: A 23-year-old man presents after experiencing several episodes of mild lightheadedness during a long-distance cycling event. He denies chest pain, palpitations, or syncope
He is a competitive endurance athlete with no history of structural heart disease and takes no medications
Physical examination reveals a resting heart rate of 48 beats/min. Blood pressure is normal, and the remainder of the examination is unremarkable
CBC, electrolytes, thyroid function studies, and cardiac biomarkers are all within normal limits

clues.detail: Progressive prolongation of PR interval with a subsequent non-conducted P wave and relatively constant P-P interval despite QRS complex irregularity

clues.text: Increased vagal tone during sleep accentuates the conduction abnormality.

1.description: Second-Degree Atrioventricular Block Type I is an atrioventricular conduction abnormality characterized by progressively increasing AV nodal conduction delay until a nonconducted P wave occurs. It most commonly results from increased vagal tone, medications that slow AV nodal conduction, or transient AV nodal ischemia. Patients are often asymptomatic but may experience mild dizziness or fatigue. Laboratory findings are usually normal unless a reversible cause is present. ECG demonstrates progressive PR interval prolongation followed by a dropped QRS complex, after which the cycle repeats. Because the block typically occurs within the AV node, progression to complete heart block is uncommon.
1.management: Observation and treatment of reversible causes.

clues.text: A 74-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue, lightheadedness, and two recent episodes of near-syncope while walking
He has a history of coronary artery disease and underwent a previous anterior myocardial infarction
Physical examination reveals a resting heart rate of 38 beats/min. During monitoring, occasional prolonged pauses are noted
CBC, electrolytes, thyroid function studies, and cardiac biomarkers are all within normal limits
Electrophysiologic testing localizes the conduction abnormality to the His-Purkinje system

1.description: Second-Degree Atrioventricular Block Type II is a potentially dangerous conduction abnormality caused by intermittent failure of impulse transmission through the His-Purkinje system. Unlike Mobitz I, the PR interval remains constant before and after the dropped beat. Patients may present with fatigue, dizziness, presyncope, or syncope due to intermittent loss of ventricular contraction. Laboratory findings are usually normal unless an underlying cause is present. ECG demonstrates fixed PR intervals with sudden nonconducted P waves.
1.management: Mobitz II frequently progresses to complete heart block and is associated with an unstable escape rhythm, permanent pacemaker implantation is generally recommended even in minimally symptomatic patients.

clues.text: A 78-year-old man presents to the emergency department after several episodes of dizziness, profound fatigue, and a recent syncopal event while walking
He has a history of coronary artery disease and underwent an inferior myocardial infarction several years ago
Physical examination reveals a heart rate of 32 beats/min. Intermittently, prominent pulsations are observed in the neck veins
CBC, electrolytes, thyroid function studies, and cardiac biomarkers are all within normal limits
The ventricles are activated by a junctional escape rhythm because no atrial impulses are conducted through the atrioventricular node

1.description: Third-Degree Atrioventricular Block is a severe conduction disorder in which no atrial impulses are transmitted to the ventricles, resulting in complete atrioventricular dissociation. The atria continue to depolarize under control of the sinoatrial node, while the ventricles rely on a junctional or ventricular escape rhythm to maintain cardiac output. Common causes include degenerative conduction disease, ischemic heart disease, myocardial infarction, cardiac surgery, and infiltrative disorders. Patients typically present with fatigue, dizziness, syncope, or heart failure symptoms. ECG demonstrates independent atrial and ventricular rhythms with no fixed PR interval.
1.management: Definitive treatment is permanent pacemaker implantation because of the risk of profound bradycardia and sudden cardiac death.

clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with worsening fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and intermittent palpitations that have become increasingly frequent over the past several months
She has a history of hypertension, obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, and recently developed worsening left atrial enlargement on echocardiography
Physical examination reveals an irregularly irregular pulse with variable intensity of the first heart sound.
CBC, electrolytes, and cardiac biomarkers are normal. Thyroid-stimulating hormone is within the reference range

clues.detail: Coarse fibrillatory waves visible in V1 and irregular ventricle response

clues.text: Electrophysiologic mapping demonstrates multiple rapidly firing ectopic foci originating near the pulmonary veins, producing chaotic atrial activation

1.description: Atrial Fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia and results from disorganized electrical activity within the atria, often originating from ectopic foci near the pulmonary veins. Common risk factors include hypertension, obesity, valvular heart disease, heart failure, hyperthyroidism, and obstructive sleep apnea. Patients may present with palpitations, fatigue, dyspnea, dizziness, or may be asymptomatic. ECG demonstrates an irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves and fibrillatory baseline activity. Major complications include thromboembolic stroke (due to blood stasis in the left atrial appendage) and tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy.
1.management: Management focuses on rate or rhythm control and stroke prevention through anticoagulation guided by thromboembolic risk assessment.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with intermittent palpitations, exertional dyspnea, and reduced exercise tolerance that have worsened over several weeks
He has a history of hypertension, obesity, and obstructive sleep apnea but no known coronary artery disease
Physical examination reveals a regular tachycardia of approximately 150 beats/min with no signs of heart failure
CBC, electrolytes, cardiac biomarkers, and thyroid function studies are all within normal limits

clues.detail: Inverted flutter waves in II, II, and aVF at a rate of 300 bpm

clues.text: Electrophysiologic mapping identifies a macro-reentrant circuit traveling around the tricuspid annulus through the cavotricuspid isthmus

1.description: Atrial Flutter is a supraventricular tachyarrhythmia caused by a large re-entrant electrical circuit, most commonly involving the cavotricuspid isthmus of the right atrium. Patients may present with palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue, lightheadedness, or may be asymptomatic. Laboratory studies are often normal but may identify precipitating factors such as thyroid disease or electrolyte abnormalities. ECG classically demonstrates sawtooth flutter waves, most prominently in the inferior leads, with atrial rates near 250–350 beats/min. Common conduction ratios include 2:1 and 4:1
1.management: Rate control, anticoagulation when indicated, cardioversion, and catheter ablation, which is highly effective and often curative.

clues.text: A 59-year-old man suddenly collapses while shoveling snow. Witnesses report that he briefly clutched his chest before losing consciousness
He has a history of coronary artery disease, prior myocardial infarction, and reduced left ventricular ejection fraction
Emergency medical services arrive to find the patient unresponsive, pulseless, and apneic
Following successful resuscitation, laboratory studies demonstrate elevated cardiac troponin levels consistent with acute myocardial injury

clues.detail: Rhythm strip demonstrates chaotic irregular deflections without identifiable P-QRS-T waves

clues.text: The absence of coordinated ventricular contraction results in instantaneous loss of perfusion

1.description: Ventricular Fibrillation is a lethal ventricular arrhythmia characterized by completely disorganized electrical activity that prevents effective ventricular contraction. It is the most common rhythm associated with sudden cardiac death and frequently occurs in the setting of acute myocardial infarction, ischemic cardiomyopathy, electrolyte abnormalities, or degeneration of ventricular tachycardia. Patients present with immediate loss of consciousness, pulselessness, and cardiac arrest. Laboratory findings may reveal an underlying precipitating cause such as myocardial ischemia or electrolyte disturbance. ECG demonstrates chaotic oscillatory electrical activity without organized QRS complexes.
1.management: Management requires immediate high-quality CPR and unsynchronized defibrillation, followed by advanced cardiac life support and treatment of the underlying cause.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man presents to the emergency department after experiencing sudden palpitations, dizziness, and near-syncope while climbing stairs
He has a history of a large anterior myocardial infarction and ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30%
Physical examination reveals hypotension and diaphoresis during the episode. Jugular venous pulsations intermittently become prominent despite a regular tachycardia
Electrolytes, magnesium, and cardiac biomarkers are within normal limits

clues.detail: ECG demonstrates uniform wide QRS complexes, indeterminant axis, and notching of the S wave in lead III

clues.text: Electrophysiologic mapping identifies a re-entrant circuit revolving around fibrotic myocardial scar tissue from a prior infarction

1.description: Management depends on hemodynamic stability and may include synchronized cardioversion, antiarrhythmic therapy, catheter ablation, and implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement for secondary prevention.
1.management: Management depends on hemodynamic stability and may include synchronized cardioversion, antiarrhythmic therapy, catheter ablation, and implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement for secondary prevention.

clues.text: 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after experiencing several episodes of sudden lightheadedness and transient loss of consciousness over the past 24 hours
She recently began treatment with an antiemetic medication for severe nausea and has had persistent vomiting for several days
Physical examination is largely unremarkable between episodes, although telemetry reveals brief periods of palpitations associated with near-syncope
Serum potassium is 2.9 mmol/L and magnesium is below the reference range. Cardiac biomarkers are normal

clues.detail: Sinus rhythm with inverted T waves, prominent U waves and a long Q-U interval due to hypokalemia. A premature atrial complex (beat #9 of the rhythm strip) lands on the end of the T wave, initiating the arrhythmia

clues.text: Upon further examination, the QRS complexes appear to rotate around the isoelectric baseline during the tachycardia

1.description: Torsades de Pointes is a potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia that occurs in the setting of prolonged ventricular repolarization. Causes include congenital long-QT syndromes, medications that prolong the QT interval, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and bradyarrhythmias. Patients typically present with palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Laboratory findings often reveal electrolyte abnormalities, particularly hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. ECG demonstrates QT prolongation and a characteristic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia in which the QRS complexes appear to twist around the baseline. The arrhythmia results from early afterdepolarizations.
1.management: Acute management includes intravenous magnesium sulfate, correction of electrolyte abnormalities, withdrawal of offending medications, and electrical cardioversion if the patient is unstable.

clues.text: A 47-year-old man presents with three weeks of intermittent fevers, progressive fatigue, and unintentional weight loss. He reports worsening shortness of breath with exertion
He has a history of intravenous drug use
Physical examination reveals a new holosystolic murmur, splinter hemorrhages beneath several fingernails, and multiple painless erythematous lesions on the palms
CBC demonstrates normocytic anemia and leukocytosis. ESR and CRP are markedly elevated. Three separate blood culture sets grow gram-positive cocci

clues.detail: Transesophageal echocardiogram shows large vegetation on the tricuspid valve

clues.text: Histopathology of the vegetation demonstrates fibrin and platelet aggregates containing colonies of staphylococcus aureus

1.description: Infective Endocarditis is a life-threatening infection of the cardiac valves or endocardial surface caused by persistent bacteremia. Common pathogens include Staphylococcus aureus, Viridans Streptococci, and enterococci. Risk factors include intravenous drug use, prosthetic valves, structural heart disease, and prior endocarditis. Patients typically present with fever, fatigue, a new or changing murmur, and embolic or immunologic phenomena. Laboratory findings often reveal positive blood cultures, elevated inflammatory markers, and anemia. Echocardiography demonstrates valvular vegetations. Diagnosis is based on the Duke criteria.
1.management: Prolonged intravenous antibiotics and, in selected cases, surgical valve repair or replacement.

clues.text: A 63-year-old man presents with progressive exertional dyspnea, increasing abdominal distension, and bilateral leg swelling that have worsened over the past year
He was treated with mediastinal radiation therapy for Hodgkin lymphoma 20 years ago
Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure, ascites, peripheral edema, and a paradoxical rise in jugular venous pressure during inspiration
CBC, troponin, and renal function are normal. BNP is mildly elevated.

clues.detail: CT demonstrates smooth diffuse thickening of the pericardium with associated left-sided pleural effusion. No calcification of the pericardium is seen

clues.text: Cardiac catheterization demonstrates equalization of diastolic pressures in all four chambers with a characteristic ‘square root sign’ during ventricular filling

1.description: Constrictive Pericarditis is a disorder caused by chronic inflammation leading to a rigid, fibrotic, and sometimes calcified pericardium that restricts ventricular filling. Common causes include prior cardiac surgery, mediastinal radiation, tuberculosis, and recurrent pericarditis. Patients typically present with manifestations of right-sided heart failure including peripheral edema, ascites, hepatomegaly, elevated jugular venous pressure, and exertional dyspnea. Laboratory findings are often nonspecific, and BNP elevations are usually less pronounced than in restrictive cardiomyopathy. Echocardiography may demonstrate ventricular interdependence and septal bounce, while CT can reveal pericardial calcification. Cardiac catheterization classically shows equalized diastolic pressures and a square root sign.
1.management: Definitive treatment is surgical pericardiectomy.

clues.text: A 71-year-old man presents after experiencing two episodes of transient left arm weakness and difficulty speaking, each lasting approximately 15 minutes before resolving completely
He has a history of smoking and hyperlipidemia
Physical examination reveals a right-sided carotid bruit. Neurologic examination is normal between episodes
Lipid profile demonstrates markedly elevated LDL cholesterol. CBC, glucose, and coagulation studies are normal
Carotid duplex ultrasonography demonstrates 80% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery with elevated peak systolic velocities
The patient describes a curtain descending over one eye that resolves within minutes

1.description: Carotid Artery Disease is a manifestation of systemic atherosclerosis characterized by narrowing of the carotid arteries, most commonly at the carotid bifurcation and proximal internal carotid artery. Major risk factors include smoking, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and advanced age. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with transient ischemic attacks, ischemic stroke, amaurosis fugax, or focal neurologic deficits. Laboratory findings typically reflect underlying cardiovascular risk factors rather than the disease itself. Diagnosis is established with carotid duplex ultrasonography and may be further characterized by CT or MR angiography.
1.management: Aggressive risk-factor modification, antiplatelet therapy, statins, and carotid endarterectomy or carotid artery stenting in selected patients with significant stenosis.

clues.text: A 72-year-old man presents with severe burning pain in his right foot that is worse at night and improves when he hangs the leg over the side of the bed
He has a 20-pack-year smoking history, and previously diagnosed peripheral artery disease
Physical examination reveals a cool foot with absent dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses. A nonhealing ulcer is present on the distal great toe
CBC demonstrates mild anemia, and inflammatory markers are mildly elevated. Renal function is normal
An ankle-brachial index is 0.35, and CT angiography demonstrates severe multilevel occlusive disease of the femoral and tibial arteries
Toe pressure is critically reduced, indicating severe distal hypoperfusion

1.description: Critical limb ischemia, now often termed chronic limb-threatening ischemia, represents the most advanced stage of peripheral arterial disease. It results from severe, chronic reduction in limb perfusion and is characterized by ischemic rest pain, nonhealing ulcers, or gangrene persisting for more than two weeks. Major risk factors include smoking, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and advanced age. Physical examination typically reveals diminished or absent pulses, cool skin, trophic changes, and tissue loss. Diagnosis is supported by a markedly reduced ankle-brachial index and vascular imaging demonstrating severe arterial occlusive disease.
1.management: Aggressive cardiovascular risk reduction, wound care, pain control, and prompt revascularization when feasible to prevent amputation and reduce mortality.

clues.text: A 74-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe abdominal and lower back pain that began while he was gardening. He becomes increasingly lightheaded during transport
He has a 30-pack-year smoking history and hypertension
Physical examination reveals diaphoresis, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass. The patient appears pale and distressed
CBC demonstrates a falling hemoglobin level, serum lactate is markedly elevated, and metabolic acidosis is present on arterial blood gas analysis

clues.detail: Ballooning, rupture, and permissive tamponade

clues.text: The patient develops hemorrhagic shock because of rapid blood loss into the retroperitoneal space.

1.description: Ruptured Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm is a vascular emergency caused by disruption of an abdominal aortic aneurysm, resulting in massive hemorrhage, most commonly into the retroperitoneal space. Major risk factors include advanced age, male sex, smoking, hypertension, and pre-existing aneurysmal disease. Patients classically present with the triad of sudden abdominal or back pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass, although all three findings are not always present. Laboratory findings often demonstrate acute blood loss anemia, elevated lactate, and metabolic acidosis. Diagnosis is confirmed with CT angiography in stable patients.
1.management: Immediate vascular surgical intervention, permissive hypotension, blood product resuscitation, and either open repair or endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR). IV hydromorphone, anti-impulse therapy to prevent false-lumen propagation (target SBP 100 mmHg, HR ~70 bpm), esmolol to reduce heart's workload

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with the sudden onset of severe chest pain that began while lifting a heavy object. He describes the pain as the worst he has ever experienced
He has a long history of poorly controlled hypertension
Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 190/110 mmHg in the right arm and 155/90 mmHg in the left arm. The right radial pulse is noticeably stronger than the left
Cardiac troponins are normal. Serum lactate is mildly elevated, and D-dimer is elevated

clues.detail: A true and false lumen is seen at the aortic arch

clues.text: An intimal flap extends from the ascending aorta to the infrarenal abdominal aorta

1.description: Aortic Dissection is a life-threatening vascular emergency caused by an intimal tear that allows blood to enter the aortic media, creating a false lumen. Chronic hypertension is the most important risk factor, although connective tissue disorders such as Marfan Syndrome and bicuspid aortic valve disease also increase risk. Patients classically present with sudden severe tearing chest or back pain, pulse deficits, and blood pressure discrepancies between limbs. CT angiography demonstrates an intimal flap separating true and false lumens.
1.management: Initial management includes rapid heart rate and blood pressure control with intravenous beta-blockers. Stanford type A dissections generally require emergent surgery, whereas uncomplicated type B dissections are often managed medically.

clues.text: A 21-year-old woman presents to the ED after briefly losing consciousness while standing in a crowded auditorium during a university ceremony
She reports feeling increasingly warm and anxious while standing for an extended period before the episode occurred
Witnesses describe her becoming pale and diaphoretic before slowly collapsing to the ground. She regained consciousness within seconds and was immediately oriented
CBC, serum glucose, electrolytes, pregnancy testing, and cardiac biomarkers are all normal
An exaggerated Bezold–Jarisch reflex produces transient cerebral hypoperfusion

1.description: Vasovagal Syncope is the most common cause of syncope and results from an exaggerated autonomic reflex that causes transient bradycardia, vasodilation, and cerebral hypoperfusion. Common triggers include prolonged standing, emotional distress, pain, heat exposure, and medical procedures. Patients typically experience a prodrome consisting of nausea, diaphoresis, warmth, pallor, visual dimming, and lightheadedness before losing consciousness. Laboratory investigations are usually normal and are primarily used to exclude alternative causes. ECG is generally normal. Tilt-table testing may reproduce symptoms.
1.management: Reassurance, trigger avoidance, hydration, physical counterpressure maneuvers, and education regarding recognition of prodromal symptoms.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents after a sudden loss of consciousness while walking to his mailbox.
He has a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy with a prior myocardial infarction
Physical examination is largely unremarkable, but the patient reports that the episode occurred without warning and was not preceded by nausea, dizziness, or visual changes
CBC, electrolytes, glucose, and cardiac biomarkers are within normal limits
Telemetry monitoring reveals several episodes of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia associated with transient hypotension
He recovered within minutes and has no memory of the event.

1.description: Cardiac Syncope is a potentially life-threatening cause of transient loss of consciousness resulting from an arrhythmia that abruptly reduces cerebral perfusion. Both bradyarrhythmias (e.g., high-grade atrioventricular block, sinus node dysfunction) and tachyarrhythmias (e.g., ventricular tachycardia, supraventricular tachycardia) may be responsible. Patients typically experience sudden syncope with little or no prodrome and rapid recovery. Risk factors include structural heart disease, prior myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, and inherited arrhythmia syndromes. Laboratory studies are often normal. Diagnosis relies on ECG, telemetry, ambulatory monitoring, or implantable loop recorders.
1.management: Management targets the underlying rhythm disturbance and may include antiarrhythmic therapy, catheter ablation, pacemaker implantation, or implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement.

clues.text: A 76-year-old man presents after two episodes of sudden transient loss of consciousness over the past month
Both episodes occurred without chest pain, palpitations, or postictal confusion.
He reports that one episode occurred while shaving and another while turning his head sharply to reverse his car
Physical examination is unremarkable. Neurologic examination is normal, and there are no focal deficits
CBC, electrolytes, glucose, cardiac biomarkers, and thyroid function tests are all within normal limits

Continuous cardiac monitoring reveals transient sinus pauses during episodes of neck stimulation. Echocardiography demonstrates no significant structural heart disease. Carotid sinus massage reproduces symptoms and causes a prolonged ventricular pause

1.description: Carotid Sinus Syncope is a form of reflex syncope caused by an exaggerated response of the carotid sinus baroreceptors. It most commonly affects older adults and may be triggered by neck turning, tight collars, shaving, or external pressure on the carotid sinus. Excessive vagal activation produces bradycardia, sinus arrest, atrioventricular block, hypotension, or a combination of these effects, resulting in transient cerebral hypoperfusion and syncope. Routine laboratory studies are typically normal. Diagnosis is supported by reproduction of symptoms during monitored carotid sinus massage.
1.management: Avoiding triggers, treating contributing medications, and, in patients with a predominant cardioinhibitory response, permanent pacemaker implantation.

clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with progressively worsening exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance over the past several years.
She recently developed episodes of palpitations and occasional streaks of blood in her sputum
CBC demonstrates mild microcytic anemia. BNP is mildly elevated
Physical examination reveals an irregularly irregular pulse and a low-pitched diastolic murmur best heard at the cardiac apex
Echocardiography demonstrates thickened mitral valve leaflets with commissural fusion and a characteristic ‘hockey-stick’ appearance of the anterior leaflet
She immigrated from a region where rheumatic fever remains common and recalls a severe febrile illness with migratory joint pain during childhood

1.description: Mitral Stenosis is most commonly caused by rheumatic heart disease and results in impaired blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle during diastole. Patients typically present with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, hemoptysis, atrial fibrillation, and symptoms of pulmonary congestion. Physical examination classically reveals a loud first heart sound, opening snap, and low-pitched diastolic rumble heard best at the apex. Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and demonstrates leaflet thickening, commissural fusion, and reduced valve area. Complications include atrial fibrillation, thromboembolism, pulmonary hypertension, and right-sided heart failure.
1.management: Mitral stenosis management includes medical therapy (rate control with beta-blockers, diuretics for congestion, anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation) and intervention for severe MS (MVA ≤1.5 cm²). Percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy (PMBC) is first-line for favorable valve morphology; mitral valve surgery (commissurotomy or replacement) is indicated when anatomy is unfavorable or PMBC fails

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and fatigue over the past year. She reports occasional palpitations but denies chest pain
She has no history of coronary artery disease
BNP is mildly elevated. CBC, electrolytes, and cardiac biomarkers are otherwise normal
Physical examination reveals a high-pitched blowing systolic murmur heard best at the cardiac apex and radiating toward the axilla
Echocardiography demonstrates left atrial enlargement, left ventricular dilation, and regurgitant flow from the left ventricle into the left atrium during systole
Echocardiography further demonstrates prolapse of a myxomatously degenerated posterior mitral leaflet into the left atrium during systole

1.description: Primary Mitral Regurgitation is caused by intrinsic abnormalities of the mitral valve apparatus, including leaflet degeneration, chordae tendineae rupture, papillary muscle dysfunction, or mitral valve prolapse. During systole, blood regurgitates from the left ventricle into the left atrium, producing chronic volume overload of both chambers. Patients typically present with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, palpitations, or heart failure symptoms. Physical examination reveals a high-pitched holosystolic murmur heard best at the apex and radiating to the axilla. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis and evaluates severity. Progressive disease can lead to atrial fibrillation, pulmonary hypertension, left ventricular dysfunction, and heart failure.
1.management: Surgical MV repair (preferred over replacement) when severe and symptomatic, or asymptomatic with LVEF ≤60% or LVESD ≥40 mm. No medical therapy reduces MR severity. TEER is reserved for high-surgical-risk patients. Serial echocardiographic surveillance guides intervention timing

clues.text: A 71-year-old man presents with progressively worsening exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, and lower extremity swelling over the past six months. He now requires three pillows to sleep comfortably
He suffered a large anterior myocardial infarction five years ago and has a known left ventricular ejection fraction of 30%
Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure, bibasilar crackles, and a blowing holosystolic murmur best heard at the cardiac apex
BNP is markedly elevated. Cardiac troponin levels are normal
Echocardiography demonstrates severe left ventricular dilation and reduced systolic function
The mitral valve leaflets appear structurally normal

1.description: Secondary Mitral Regurgitation occurs when left ventricular dilation or dysfunction prevents normal coaptation of otherwise structurally normal mitral valve leaflets. The condition most commonly develops in patients with ischemic cardiomyopathy or dilated cardiomyopathy. Patients typically present with symptoms of heart failure, including dyspnea, fatigue, orthopnea, and peripheral edema. Physical examination reveals a holosystolic murmur at the apex similar to primary mitral regurgitation. Echocardiography demonstrates left ventricular enlargement, reduced systolic function, and mitral regurgitation with normal valve anatomy.
1.management: Guideline-directed heart failure therapy and, in selected patients, transcatheter or surgical intervention.

clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents with gradually worsening exertional dyspnea and occasional episodes of exertional lightheadedness while jogging. He has no known history of coronary artery disease
He reports that his father underwent valve replacement surgery in his fifties and that several family members have been told they have heart murmurs
Physical examination reveals a systolic ejection murmur heard best at the right upper sternal border with radiation toward the carotid arteries
CBC, electrolytes, renal function, and cardiac biomarkers are normal
Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrates calcific aortic valve disease with mild ascending aortic dilation
Short-axis echocardiographic imaging reveals a ‘fish-mouth’ opening during systole

1.description: Bicuspid Aortic Valve is the most common congenital cardiac valvular abnormality and results from fusion of two aortic valve cusps during development. Although many patients are asymptomatic early in life, accelerated valve degeneration often leads to premature aortic stenosis or aortic regurgitation. Patients may present with exertional dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, or an incidentally detected murmur. Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and demonstrates a valve with two cusps rather than the normal three. Associated complications include ascending aortic dilation, aortic aneurysm formation, and aortic dissection, necessitating lifelong surveillance.
1.management: lifelong surveillance of both valve function and aortopathy. Valve intervention (SAVR or repair) follows standard AS/AR indications. Aortic replacement is recommended at ≥5.5 cm (or ≥5.0 cm with risk factors like family history of dissection or rapid growth). Concomitant aortic replacement is reasonable at ≥4.5 cm during AVR

clues.text: A 76-year-old man presents with progressive exertional shortness of breath and occasional episodes of lightheadedness while walking uphill. Over the past several months, he has also developed exertional chest discomfort
He has a history of hypertension
Physical examination reveals a harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border with radiation to both carotid arteries and pulsus parvus et tardus
CBC, electrolytes, renal function, and cardiac biomarkers are within normal limits
Echocardiography demonstrates a heavily calcified aortic valve with restricted leaflet motion and concentric left ventricular hypertrophy
Doppler echocardiography demonstrates a markedly reduced aortic valve area

1.description: Aortic Stenosis is a progressive valvular disorder most commonly caused by age-related calcific degeneration or congenital bicuspid aortic valve disease. Progressive narrowing of the valve increases left ventricular afterload, resulting in concentric hypertrophy and reduced cardiac output. Patients classically present with exertional dyspnea, angina, and syncope. Physical examination reveals a harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids and diminished carotid upstrokes. Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and quantifies disease severity. Symptomatic severe disease requires valve replacement because mortality rises substantially once symptoms develop (typically following the "SAD" mnemonic of Syncope -> Angina -> Dyspnea (heart failure)).
1.management: AVR for symptomatic severe high-gradient AS as a Class I indication, and for asymptomatic severe AS with LVEF <50%. In asymptomatic severe AS, AVR is reasonable when exercise testing shows decreased tolerance or BP drop ≥10 mmHg, aortic velocity ≥5 m/s, BNP >3× normal, or velocity increase ≥0.3 m/s/year. SAVR is preferred for patients <65 years; TAVR for those ≥80 years; shared decision-making guides ages 65–80. Asymptomatic patients not meeting intervention criteria require echocardiographic surveillance every 6–12 months.

clues.text: A 54-year-old man presents with progressively worsening exertional dyspnea and awareness of a forceful heartbeat, particularly when lying in bed at night.
He has a history of a congenital bicuspid aortic valve and was recently noted to have dilation of the ascending aorta
Physical examination reveals a wide pulse pressure and a high-pitched decrescendo diastolic murmur heard best along the left sternal border with the patient leaning forward
CBC, electrolytes, renal function, and cardiac biomarkers are normal
Echocardiography demonstrates left ventricular dilation with eccentric hypertrophy and retrograde flow
Color Doppler echocardiography demonstrates a broad diastolic regurgitant jet extending from the aorta into the left ventricle

1.description: Aortic Regurgitation occurs when the aortic valve fails to close completely during diastole, allowing blood to flow from the aorta back into the left ventricle. Common causes include bicuspid aortic valve disease, aortic root dilation, infective endocarditis, rheumatic heart disease, and aortic dissection. Chronic regurgitation produces progressive left ventricular volume overload, resulting in eccentric hypertrophy and ventricular dilation. Patients may present with exertional dyspnea, palpitations, and heart failure symptoms. Physical examination classically reveals a high-pitched blowing diastolic murmur and widened pulse pressure. Echocardiography is the primary diagnostic modality and guides management and timing of valve intervention.
1.management: Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) as the definitive treatment for chronic severe aortic regurgitation. Key indications include: symptomatic severe AR regardless of LV function (Class I), asymptomatic severe AR with LVEF ≤55% (Class I), and severe LV enlargement (LVESD >50 mm) with normal LVEF (Class IIa). Medical therapy with afterload reduction (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or dihydropyridine CCBs) is reserved for hypertension management or when surgery is contraindicated. TAVI should not be performed in surgical candidates with isolated severe AR.

clues.text: A 41-year-old woman presents with progressive fatigue, abdominal fullness, and worsening lower extremity swelling over the past several months.
She notes increasing discomfort from abdominal distension but minimal shortness of breath

Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure, hepatomegaly, and a blowing holosystolic murmur best heard along the left lower sternal border. The murmur becomes louder during inspiration
Liver enzymes are mildly elevated, while BNP is modestly increased. Renal function is normal
“Echocardiography demonstrates a flail tricuspid leaflet with destruction of valvular tissue
She has a history of infective endocarditis related to prior intravenous drug use

1.description: Primary Tricuspid Regurgitation results from intrinsic abnormalities of the tricuspid valve apparatus, including infective endocarditis, rheumatic heart disease, congenital abnormalities, trauma, carcinoid heart disease, or leaflet degeneration. During systole, blood regurgitates from the right ventricle into the right atrium, producing right-sided volume overload. Patients typically present with fatigue, peripheral edema, ascites, hepatomegaly, and symptoms of systemic venous congestion. Physical examination reveals a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that intensifies with inspiration (Carvallo sign).
