Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis and identifies the underlying structural lesion.
1.management: Severe disease may require surgical or transcatheter intervention.

clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with worsening fatigue, abdominal distension, and progressive lower extremity edema over the past year. She also reports increasing shortness of breath when climbing stairs
She has a history of group 2 pulmonary hypertension

Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure with prominent CV waves, hepatomegaly, ascites, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration
BNP is elevated, and liver enzymes demonstrate a mild cholestatic pattern
Echocardiography demonstrates marked right atrial enlargement, right ventricular dilation, and elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure
Echocardiography further reveals structurally unaffected tricuspid leaflets

1.description: Functional Tricuspid Regurgitation is the most common form of tricuspid regurgitation and occurs when right ventricular enlargement or tricuspid annular dilation prevents normal leaflet coaptation. Common causes include pulmonary hypertension, left-sided heart disease, right ventricular dysfunction, and chronic atrial fibrillation. Patients typically present with symptoms of systemic venous congestion, including edema, ascites, hepatomegaly, and fatigue. Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure and a holosystolic murmur that increases with inspiration. Echocardiography demonstrates severe regurgitation despite structurally normal valve leaflets.
1.management: Management focuses on treatment of the underlying cardiac or pulmonary disorder and, in selected patients, valve intervention.

clues.text: An 8-month-old infant is brought to clinic because of episodes of sudden bluish discoloration of the lips and fingers, particularly when crying or feeding.
The infant was has demonstrated poor weight gain since birth
Physical examination reveals central cyanosis and a harsh systolic ejection murmur heard best at the left upper sternal border
CBC demonstrates an elevated hemoglobin concentration and hematocrit

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates a "boot-shaped" heart with an upturned cardiac apex and concave pulmonary arterial segment. Retrived from https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-8049

clues.text: Echocardiography confirms the presence of a ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, right ventricular outflow tract obstruction, and right ventricular hypertrophy

1.description: Tetralogy of Fallot is the most common cyanotic congenital heart disease beyond infancy and consists of four abnormalities: ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, right ventricular outflow tract obstruction, and right ventricular hypertrophy. Right ventricular outflow obstruction promotes right-to-left shunting across the ventricular septal defect, resulting in cyanosis. Patients typically present with cyanosis, poor feeding, failure to thrive, and hypercyanotic (“tet”) spells (squatting increases SVR reducing right to left shunting. Laboratory findings may reveal secondary polycythemia. Chest radiography classically demonstrates a boot-shaped heart. Definitive diagnosis is established by echocardiography.
1.management: Surgical repair during infancy. Lifelong surveillance for pulmonary regurgitation and RV dysfunction with pulmonary valve replacement when indicated

clues.text: Two hours after birth, a full-term newborn develops profound cyanosis and tachypnea. Oxygen supplementation produces minimal improvement in oxygen saturation
The infant was born after an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. Prenatal ultrasounds were reportedly normal
Physical examination reveals marked central cyanosis with minimal respiratory distress. Cardiac examination is otherwise relatively unremarkable, and no harsh murmur is appreciated
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates severe hypoxemia despite administration of 100% oxygen

clues.detail: CXR shows a narrow superior mediastinum and a globular cardiac silhouette resembling an "egg-on-a-string"

clues.text: Administration of prostaglandin E1 improves oxygenation by maintaining ductal patency

1.description: Transposition of the Great Arteries is a cyanotic congenital heart defect in which the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery arises from the left ventricle. This creates parallel rather than series circulations, making survival dependent on mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood through a patent ductus arteriosus, atrial septal defect, ventricular septal defect, or patent foramen ovale. Newborns present with profound cyanosis shortly after birth that does not improve with supplemental oxygen. Chest radiography may demonstrate an egg-on-a-string appearance.
1.management: Immediate management includes Alprostadil infusion (PGE1) to maintain ductal patency, followed by definitive surgical correction with an arterial switch procedure.

clues.text: A 3-week-old infant is brought to clinic because of poor feeding, excessive sweating during feeds, and failure to gain weight. Parents also note persistent bluish discoloration of the lips
The infant was born at term but has developed progressively worsening tachypnea since the first week of life
Physical examination reveals central cyanosis, tachypnea, bounding peripheral pulses, and a hyperdynamic precordium
Pulse oximetry demonstrates persistent hypoxemia. CBC demonstrates mild erythrocytosis
Chest radiography demonstrates cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography reveals a large ventricular septal defect
Echocardiography demonstrates a single arterial vessel arising from the heart that gives rise to the systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations

1.description: Truncus Arteriosus is a rare cyanotic congenital heart defect caused by failure of embryologic truncal septation. Instead of separate aortic and pulmonary outflow tracts, a single arterial trunk supplies the systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations. A large ventricular septal defect is almost always present. Patients typically present during infancy with cyanosis, tachypnea, poor feeding, diaphoresis with feeding, and signs of congestive heart failure due to pulmonary overcirculation. Chest radiography often demonstrates cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary vascularity. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis.
1.management: Truncus arteriosus requires early surgical repair, typically within the first few weeks of life. The procedure involves separating the single truncal vessel into the aorta and pulmonary artery, closing the ventricular septal defect (VSD) with a patch, and establishing right ventricle-to-pulmonary artery continuity using a valved conduit (Rastelli procedure). Lifelong follow-up is essential for conduit replacement.

clues.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and decreasing exercise tolerance.
Over the past year she has noticed bluish discoloration of her lips and "ballooning" of her fingertips during physical activity
She was diagnosed with a congenital heart defect during childhood but never underwent corrective surgery because she remained relatively asymptomatic
CBC demonstrates a hemoglobin concentration of 205 g/L and a hematocrit of 63%
Echocardiography demonstrates severe pulmonary hypertension and a large ventricular septal defect
Cardiac catheterization reveals pulmonary vascular resistance exceeding systemic vascular resistance

1.description: Eisenmenger Syndrome is a late complication of unrepaired congenital cardiac shunts such as ventricular septal defects, atrial septal defects, or patent ductus arteriosus. Chronic left-to-right shunting causes progressive pulmonary vascular remodeling and pulmonary hypertension. As pulmonary vascular resistance rises above systemic vascular resistance, shunt reversal occurs, resulting in right-to-left flow and systemic cyanosis. Patients present with dyspnea, fatigue, cyanosis, clubbing, and secondary erythrocytosis. Echocardiography and cardiac catheterization establish the diagnosis. Once Eisenmenger physiology develops, closure of the original shunt is generally contraindicated because the pulmonary vascular disease is irreversible.
1.management: Eisenmenger's syndrome management focuses on supportive care and pulmonary vasodilator therapy, as surgical correction is contraindicated once irreversible pulmonary hypertension develops. Key strategies include pulmonary arterial hypertension-targeted therapies (e.g., bosentan, phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, prostanoids), avoidance of dehydration and pregnancy, phlebotomy for hyperviscosity symptoms, and consideration of heart-lung transplantation for refractory cases.

clues.text: A 2-day-old newborn develops progressive cyanosis shortly after birth. Despite supplemental oxygen, oxygen saturation remains persistently low
The infant initially appeared well but developed increasing difficulty feeding and tachypnea during the first 48 hours of life
Physical examination reveals central cyanosis and a single prominent second heart sound. A holosystolic murmur is heard along the lower left sternal border
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates persistent hypoxemia. CBC is otherwise unremarkable
Chest radiography demonstrates a relatively small right ventricle and decreased pulmonary vascular markings. ECG reveals left axis deviation
Echocardiography demonstrates no direct blood flow between the right atrium and right ventricle

1.description: Tricuspid Atresia is a congenital cardiac malformation in which the tricuspid valve fails to develop, preventing blood flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle. Survival requires an atrial septal defect or patent foramen ovale to allow blood to reach the left side of the heart, and often a ventricular septal defect or patent ductus arteriosus to provide pulmonary blood flow. Patients typically present shortly after birth with cyanosis and hypoxemia. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating absence of the tricuspid valve and a hypoplastic right ventricle.
1.management: Definitive treatment involves staged surgical palliation, often culminating in a Fontan procedure.

clues.text: A 5-day-old newborn is brought to the emergency department because of progressive cyanosis and difficulty feeding. Parents report rapid breathing that has worsened since birth
The infant was born at term without complications but has remained persistently cyanotic despite oxygen therapy
Physical examination reveals central cyanosis, tachypnea, and a fixed split second heart sound. A mild systolic flow murmur is present
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates persistent hypoxemia despite administration of 100% oxygen

clues.detail: CXR reveals cardiomegaly and a "snowman" appearance formed by the dialted vertical vein, brachiocephalic vein, and SVC forming the head and an enlarged right atrium forming the body

clues.text: Echocardiography demonstrates absence of any direct pulmonary venous connection to the left atrium

1.description: Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection is a cyanotic congenital heart defect in which none of the pulmonary veins connect normally to the left atrium. Instead, oxygenated pulmonary venous blood returns to the systemic venous circulation, resulting in complete mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood. Survival requires an atrial septal defect or patent foramen ovale to allow blood to reach the left side of the heart. Patients typically present with cyanosis, tachypnea, feeding difficulties, and hypoxemia. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis. The supracardiac form classically produces a snowman appearance on chest radiography.
1.management: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) requires urgent surgical repair, often within the neonatal period. Surgery involves redirecting the pulmonary veins to drain into the left atrium by creating an anastomosis between the pulmonary venous confluence and the left atrium, ligating the anomalous connection, and closing the associated atrial septal defect. Obstructed TAPVR is a surgical emergency.

clues.text: A 19-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and intermittent episodes of palpitations. She reports several episodes of near-syncope during physical activity over the past year
Review of prenatal records reveals maternal treatment with lithium during early pregnancy
Physical examination reveals mild cyanosis, elevated jugular venous pressure, and a holosystolic murmur heard best along the left lower sternal border
CBC demonstrates mild erythrocytosis. Electrolytes and cardiac biomarkers are normal
Echocardiography reveals severe tricuspid regurgitation and marked enlargement of the right atrium
Echocardiography further reveals The septal leaflet of the tricuspid valve is displaced toward the cardiac apex

1.description: Ebstein's Anomaly is a rare congenital heart defect characterized by downward displacement of the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle. This creates an “atrialized” portion of the right ventricle and often results in severe tricuspid regurgitation. Patients may present with cyanosis, exertional dyspnea, right-sided heart failure, or supraventricular arrhythmias and WPW Syndrome. The condition is classically associated with maternal lithium exposure during pregnancy. Right atrial enlargement is common, and many patients have an associated atrial septal defect or patent foramen ovale that permits right-to-left shunting. Echocardiography establishes the diagnosis.
1.management: Management ranges from observation to surgical repair depending on severity.

clues.text: 16-year-old boy presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance. He occasionally experiences lightheadedness during competitive sports but has never lost consciousness
A heart murmur was first detected during childhood, but he has never undergone cardiac intervention
Physical examination reveals a harsh systolic ejection murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border. A palpable right ventricular heave is present
CBC, electrolytes, and cardiac biomarkers are normal

clues.detail: ECG reveals RAD, dominant R wave in V1, dominant S wave in V6 (poor R wave progression), and ST depression and T-wave inversion in V1-4

clues.text: Doppler echocardiography demonstrates a doming pulmonary valve with a markedly elevated transvalvular systolic gradient

1.description: Pulmonary Stenosis is most commonly a congenital valvular abnormality that causes obstruction of blood flow from the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery. Progressive obstruction leads to right ventricular pressure overload and hypertrophy. Mild disease may remain asymptomatic, whereas severe stenosis can cause exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, or syncope. Physical examination classically reveals a systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border and a right ventricular heave. Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and quantifies severity by measuring transvalvular gradients. It is ccommonly associated with Noonan Syndrome.
1.management: Severe disease is often treated with balloon valvuloplasty.

clues.text: A 12-hour-old newborn develops profound cyanosis shortly after birth. Despite supplemental oxygen, oxygen saturation remains critically low
The infant initially appeared stable after delivery but deteriorated rapidly during the first day of life
Physical examination reveals marked central cyanosis and tachypnea. No significant systolic ejection murmur is appreciated
Echocardiography demonstrates a hypoplastic pulmonary outflow tract and a patent ductus arteriosus supplying pulmonary blood flow
No antegrade flow can be demonstrated across the pulmonary valve on Doppler imaging
Echocardiography demonstrates complete absence of a functional pulmonary valve opening with no direct blood flow from the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery

1.description: Pulmonary Atresia is a severe cyanotic congenital heart defect in which the pulmonary valve fails to form properly, preventing blood from flowing from the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery. Pulmonary blood flow therefore depends on a patent ductus arteriosus and other intracardiac shunts. Newborns typically present within hours of birth with profound cyanosis and hypoxemia that respond poorly to oxygen therapy. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating absent antegrade pulmonary flow. Immediate administration of Alprostadil is lifesaving.
1.management: Definitive treatment requires staged surgical or catheter-based intervention.

clues.text: A 27-year-old man presents with sharp central chest pain that began two days ago. The pain worsens with deep inspiration and coughing and has become increasingly uncomfortable when lying flat
He reports a febrile upper respiratory tract infection approximately one week before symptom onset
Physical examination reveals that the chest pain improves significantly when the patient sits up and leans forward. Cardiac auscultation identifies a leathery sound along the left sternal border
CRP and ESR are elevated. High-sensitivity troponin is mildly elevated

clues.detail: ECG demonstrates widespread ST elevation and PR depression with reciprocal ST depression and PR elevation in V1 and aVR

clues.text: Echocardiography demonstrates a small circumferential pericardial effusion

1.description: Acute Pericarditis is most commonly caused by viral infection but may also result from autoimmune disease, malignancy, uremia, myocardial infarction, or medication exposure. Patients classically present with pleuritic chest pain that worsens when supine and improves when sitting forward. Physical examination may reveal a pericardial friction rub, one of the most specific findings. Inflammatory markers are typically elevated, and mild troponin elevation may occur. Electrocardiography classically demonstrates diffuse ST-segment elevation with PR-segment depression. Echocardiography is performed to evaluate for associated pericardial effusion and cardiac tamponade.
1.management: Acute pericarditis is primarily managed with NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen or aspirin) combined with colchicine, which reduces recurrence risk. Activity restriction is recommended until symptom resolution. Corticosteroids are reserved for contraindications to first-line therapy or refractory cases, used at low doses. Treatment of underlying etiology is essential. Hospitalization is indicated for high-risk features.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with progressive shortness of breath and fatigue over several weeks. She reports increasing exercise intolerance but minimal chest pain
She has metastatic breast cancer and recently completed a course of radiation therapy
Physical examination reveals tachycardia and distant heart sounds. Lung examination is clear
CBC demonstrates mild anemia. Cardiac biomarkers are normal

clues.detail: CXR shows increased pulmonary vasculature markings and cardiac silhouetting with an increased cardiothoracic ratio. A "globular" shaped heart is noted

clues.text: Echocardiography demonstrates a large echo-free space circumferentially surrounding the heart within the pericardial sac

1.description: Pericardial Effusion occurs when fluid accumulates between the visceral and parietal layers of the pericardium. Causes include malignancy, infection, autoimmune disease, hypothyroidism, uremia, trauma, and post-cardiac injury syndromes. Small effusions may be asymptomatic, whereas larger effusions can cause dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, and hemodynamic compromise. Physical examination may reveal distant heart sounds and tachycardia. Chest radiography may demonstrate a globular “water-bottle” cardiac silhouette when the effusion is large. Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and determines size, location, and whether tamponade physiology is present.
1.management: Management depends on the underlying cause and hemodynamic significance.

clues.text: A 63-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with rapidly worsening shortness of breath, profound fatigue, and lightheadedness.
Over the past several hours she has become increasingly weak and unable to walk short distances
Physical examination reveals tachycardia, hypotension, elevated jugular venous pressure, and markedly diminished heart sounds
Serum lactate is elevated. Cardiac biomarkers are normal
During inspiration, systolic blood pressure falls by 16 mmHg
Echocardiography demonstrates a large circumferential pericardial effusion

1.description: Cardiac Tamponade occurs when pericardial pressure rises sufficiently to impair cardiac filling, reducing stroke volume and cardiac output. Causes include malignancy, trauma, uremia, infection, and progression of a pericardial effusion. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachycardia, hypotension, and signs of obstructive shock. Physical examination classically demonstrates elevated jugular venous pressure, muffled heart sounds, and hypotension (Beck triad). Echocardiography is the diagnostic test of choice and may reveal right atrial or right ventricular diastolic collapse.
1.management: Emergent pericardiocentesis is the definitive treatment and can be lifesaving.

clues.text: A 29-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance. She reports becoming unusually fatigued during activities that were previously easy
She has been told since childhood that she has a heart murmur but has never undergone cardiac evaluation
Physical examination reveals a systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border and a widely split second heart sound that does not vary with respiration
CBC and metabolic studies are normal. BNP is mildly elevated
Echocardiography demonstrates enlargement of the right atrium and right ventricle with evidence of increased pulmonary blood flow
Color Doppler echocardiography demonstrates left-to-right shunting through a defect in the interatrial septum

1.description: Atrial Septal Defect is a common congenital heart defect in which blood flows from the higher-pressure left atrium to the right atrium. There are 3 types: sinus venosus defect, secundum ASD, and primum ASD. Chronic left-to-right shunting results in increased pulmonary blood flow and progressive right atrial and right ventricular volume overload. Many patients remain asymptomatic until adulthood, when they develop exertional dyspnea, fatigue, atrial arrhythmias, or pulmonary hypertension. Physical examination classically reveals a fixed split S2 and a systolic flow murmur caused by increased flow across the pulmonary valve. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis.
1.management: Significant defects are often closed to prevent long-term complications such as atrial fibrillation and Eisenmenger syndrome.

clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents after experiencing a transient episode of right arm weakness and expressive aphasia that resolved completely within 30 minutes.
She recently completed a long-haul international flight and reports mild calf discomfort several days before the neurologic event
Neurologic examination is normal. Cardiovascular examination reveals no murmurs, and there are no signs of structural heart disease
Routine laboratory testing, thrombophilia screening, and vascular imaging are unrevealing
Brain MRI confirms a small embolic infarction. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrates normal ventricular structure and function
During an agitated saline bubble study, microbubbles appear in the left atrium within three cardiac cycles after injection and become more prominent during a Valsalva maneuver

1.description: Patent Foramen Ovale is a common congenital cardiac finding resulting from failure of the foramen ovale to seal completely after birth. Most individuals remain asymptomatic throughout life. However, transient increases in right atrial pressure may permit right-to-left shunting, allowing venous thrombi to bypass the pulmonary circulation and enter the systemic arterial system. This mechanism can result in paradoxical embolism and cryptogenic stroke. Diagnosis is typically established using contrast echocardiography with an agitated saline (“bubble”) study.
1.management: Management depends on clinical context and may include antithrombotic therapy or transcatheter closure in selected patients with cryptogenic stroke.

clues.text: A 4-month-old infant is brought to clinic because of poor weight gain, rapid breathing during feeds, and excessive sweating while feeding.
A heart murmur was first detected during a newborn examination, but the infant initially appeared otherwise healthy
Physical examination reveals tachypnea, mild hepatomegaly, and a harsh pansystolic murmur heard best at the left lower sternal border
CBC and metabolic studies are normal. BNP is mildly elevated.
Chest radiography demonstrates cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography reveals left atrial and left ventricular enlargement
Color Doppler echocardiography demonstrates systolic left-to-right blood flow

1.description: Ventricular Septal Defect is the most common congenital heart defect and results from incomplete formation of the interventricular septum. Because left ventricular pressure exceeds right ventricular pressure, blood initially shunts from left to right, producing increased pulmonary blood flow and volume overload of the left heart. Small defects may be asymptomatic and close spontaneously, whereas larger defects can cause heart failure, poor feeding, growth failure, and pulmonary hypertension. Physical examination classically reveals a harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis. Untreated large defects may eventually progress to Eisenmenger Syndrome. There are several types of VSD: Perimembranous, muscular, inlet, and outlet.
1.management: Small, asymptomatic VSDs often close spontaneously and require only monitoring. Large, hemodynamically significant VSDs causing heart failure or failure to thrive require surgical patch closure, typically by 3–6 months of age to prevent irreversible pulmonary vascular disease. Medical therapy (diuretics, nutritional support) bridges to surgery.

clues.text: A 6-month-old infant is brought to clinic because of poor weight gain and increasing shortness of breath during feeding. Parents report frequent sweating and fatigue while feeding
The infant was born prematurely at 29 weeks gestation and required a prolonged neonatal intensive care stay
Physical examination reveals bounding peripheral pulses and a continuous murmur heard beneath the left clavicle
CBC and metabolic studies are normal. BNP is mildly elevated
Chest radiography demonstrates cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography reveals left atrial and left ventricular enlargement
Color Doppler echocardiography demonstrates continuous blood flow from the descending aorta into the pulmonary artery throughout both systole and diastole

1.description: Patent Ductus Arteriosus occurs when the fetal ductus arteriosus fails to close after birth. Because aortic pressure exceeds pulmonary artery pressure, blood flows continuously from the aorta into the pulmonary circulation, producing a left-to-right shunt. Small PDAs may be asymptomatic, whereas larger lesions can cause heart failure, poor feeding, and failure to thrive. Physical examination classically reveals a continuous “machinery” murmur and bounding pulses caused by a widened pulse pressure. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis. Long-standing untreated PDA may progress to pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger syndrome.
1.management: Closure may occur pharmacologically in premature infants or through transcatheter or surgical intervention.

clues.text: A 17-year-old male presents with exertional headaches, occasional epistaxis, and fatigue in his legs while playing soccer.
He was noted to have elevated blood pressure during a routine sports physical, despite being otherwise healthy
Blood pressure is 165/90 mmHg in the arms and 105/70 mmHg in the legs. Femoral pulses are delayed compared with radial pulses
CBC, renal function, thyroid studies, and electrolytes are normal

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates inferior rib notching along multiple ribs and a "figure 3" sign

clues.text: CT angiography demonstrates a focal narrowing of the aorta just distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery

1.description: Coarctation of the Aorta is a congenital vascular abnormality characterized by narrowing of the aortic arch, most commonly at the juxtaductal region distal to the left subclavian artery. The obstruction increases afterload, causing upper extremity hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy while reducing perfusion to the lower extremities. Patients may present with headaches, epistaxis, leg claudication, or hypertension discovered during routine examination. Physical examination classically reveals delayed femoral pulses and a blood pressure gradient between the arms and legs. Imaging confirms the diagnosis. Associated conditions include bicuspid aortic valve and Turner Syndrome.
1.management: Surgical repair or catheter-based balloon angioplasty/stenting, depending on age and anatomy. Neonates with critical coarctation require prostaglandin E1 to maintain ductal patency before surgical intervention (end-to-end anastomosis or extended resection). Older children and adults may undergo balloon angioplasty with stent placement. Lifelong blood pressure monitoring is essential.

clues.text: A 3-month-old infant is brought to clinic because of poor feeding, diaphoresis during feeds, and failure to gain weight. Parents report rapid breathing that has progressively worsened over the past several weeks
Physical examination reveals tachypnea, hepatomegaly, and a systolic murmur along the lower left sternal border. A prominent right ventricular impulse is also noted
The infant has characteristic facial features and was diagnosed with a chromosomal disorder shortly after birth
BNP is elevated. CBC and metabolic studies are otherwise unremarkable
Chest radiography demonstrates cardiomegaly and marked pulmonary vascular congestion
Echocardiography demonstrates a common atrioventricular valve bridging a large defect involving both the atrial and ventricular septa

1.description: Atrioventricular Septal Defect is a congenital heart defect caused by failure of the endocardial cushions to fuse during embryologic development. The defect consists of varying degrees of atrial septal defects, ventricular septal defects, and abnormalities of the atrioventricular valves. Large left-to-right shunts result in pulmonary overcirculation, heart failure, and pulmonary hypertension early in life. Patients commonly present with tachypnea, poor feeding, diaphoresis, and failure to thrive. AVSD is strongly associated with Down Syndrome. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis. Components are primum ASD, inlet VSD, and common AV valve. Types are complete, partial, unbalanced.
1.management: Surgical repair during infancy.

clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe headache, blurred vision, and progressive confusion over the past 24 hours.
He has a long history of poorly controlled hypertension
Blood pressure on arrival is 245/145 mmHg.
Fundoscopic examination reveals retinal hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, and papilledema
Serum creatinine is markedly elevated, and urinalysis demonstrates proteinuria and microscopic hematuria.

clues.detail: Renal biopsy demonstrates concentric laminated thickening of arteriolar walls with an "onion-skin" appearance and marked luminal narrowing

1.description: Hyperplastic Arteriolosclerosis is a vascular lesion classically associated with malignant hypertension. Severe elevations in blood pressure produce endothelial injury and smooth muscle hyperplasia within small arteries and arterioles. Histologically, affected vessels demonstrate concentric laminated (“onion-skin”) thickening of the vessel wall with progressive luminal narrowing. Patients often present with hypertensive emergency, including encephalopathy, retinal hemorrhages, papilledema, and acute kidney injury. Renal involvement may lead to rapidly progressive renal failure. Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis is frequently accompanied by fibrinoid necrosis in the most severe cases.
1.management: Treatment requires urgent but controlled blood pressure reduction to prevent irreversible end-organ damage.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man is referred to nephrology after routine bloodwork demonstrates slowly worsening kidney function over several years.
He feels well and denies urinary symptoms
He has a 20-year history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, both of which have been moderately controlled
Blood pressure is persistently elevated at 155/90 mmHg. Fundoscopic examination demonstrates mild arteriolar narrowing but no papilledema
Serum creatinine is mildly elevated, and urinalysis demonstrates low-grade proteinuria without active urinary sediment

clues.detail: Renal biopsy demonstrates homogenous pink hyaline thickening of arteriolar walls with narrowing of the vascular lumen

1.description: Hyaline Arteriolosclerosis is a small-vessel lesion most commonly associated with long-standing hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Chronic endothelial injury permits leakage of plasma proteins into the vessel wall while stimulating extracellular matrix deposition by smooth muscle cells. Histologically, affected arterioles demonstrate homogeneous eosinophilic (hyaline) thickening with progressive luminal narrowing. These vascular changes reduce tissue perfusion and contribute to chronic ischemic injury, particularly in the kidneys. Hyaline arteriolosclerosis is a hallmark of benign nephrosclerosis and diabetic microvascular disease.
1.management: Management focuses on controlling blood pressure, glucose levels, and other cardiovascular risk factors.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents with progressively worsening calf pain when walking several blocks. The pain consistently resolves after several minutes of rest
He has a 40-pack-year smoking history, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia
Physical examination reveals diminished dorsalis pedis pulses and a cool lower extremity with delayed capillary refill
CT angiography demonstrates multiple eccentric arterial plaques causing segmental luminal narrowing within the superficial femoral artery
Lipid studies demonstrate elevated LDL cholesterol and reduced HDL cholesterol
Histologic examination demonstrates an intimal plaque composed of a fibrous cap overlying a lipid-rich necrotic core containing cholesterol clefts and foam cells

1.description: Atherosclerosis is a progressive inflammatory disorder of medium and large arteries driven by endothelial injury, lipid accumulation, and chronic inflammation. LDL cholesterol enters the arterial intima where it becomes oxidized and stimulates recruitment of macrophages that ingest lipid and form foam cells. Over time, smooth muscle proliferation and extracellular matrix deposition create a fibrous cap over a lipid-rich necrotic core. Plaque growth causes arterial stenosis, while plaque rupture can precipitate thrombosis and acute ischemic events. Atherosclerosis is the underlying cause of most cases of coronary artery disease, ischemic stroke, peripheral artery disease, and abdominal aortic aneurysm.
1.management: Management centers on lifestyle modification (heart-healthy diet, exercise, smoking cessation, weight management) and aggressive risk factor control, primarily LDL-C lowering with high-intensity statins (≥50% reduction), adding ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors if targets are unmet. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin), blood pressure control, and glycemic management (including SGLT-2 inhibitors/GLP-1 agonists in diabetics) are essential adjuncts. Revascularization is reserved for symptomatic or high-risk disease.

clues.text: A 68-year-old woman presents with progressive shortness of breath and a nonproductive cough that has worsened over several weeks.
She has a history of metastatic breast cancer and recently noted increasing fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance
Physical examination reveals decreased breath sounds and reduced tactile fremitus over the lower half of the left hemithorax. Percussion is dull in the same region
CBC demonstrates mild normocytic anemia. Serum chemistry studies are otherwise unremarkable

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates blunting of the left costophrenic angle and a meniscus shaped opacity

clues.text: Thoracentesis reveals pleural fluid with (pleural fluid protein / serum protein) > 0.5

1.description: Pleural Effusion is the accumulation of excess fluid between the visceral and parietal pleura. Patients commonly present with dyspnea, cough, and pleuritic chest discomfort. Physical examination classically reveals dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds, and reduced tactile fremitus over the affected area. Chest radiography may demonstrate blunting of the costophrenic angle and a meniscus sign, while ultrasound improves detection and guides thoracentesis. Diagnostic pleural fluid analysis distinguishes transudative from exudative effusions using Light’s criteria. Common causes include heart failure, malignancy, pneumonia, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, and pulmonary embolism. An effusion is exudative if pleural protein/serum protein > 0.5 or pleural LDH/serum LDH > 0.6 or pleural fluid LDH > 2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH.
1.management: Treatment of the underlying cause and drainage when clinically indicated.

clues.text: A 65-year-old woman develops progressive shortness of breath and a dry cough two days after undergoing elective abdominal surgery
She reports significant postoperative pain and has been reluctant to take deep breaths or use her incentive spirometer
Physical examination reveals mild tachypnea and decreased breath sounds at the right lung base. Oxygen saturation is mildly reduced
CBC demonstrates a normal white blood cell count, and inflammatory markers are not significantly elevated

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates ipsilateral mediastinal shift and silhouetting of the right heart border. The second image shows a F/U CXR demonstrating resolution

clues.text: Symptoms improve rapidly with aggressive pulmonary toilet and incentive spirometry

1.description: Atelectasis is a condition characterized by incomplete expansion or collapse of alveoli, resulting in reduced lung volume and impaired gas exchange. It commonly occurs in the postoperative period due to shallow breathing, mucus plugging, pain-limited inspiration, and prolonged immobilization. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, and mild hypoxemia. Laboratory findings are often normal unless a secondary infection develops. Chest radiography demonstrates signs of volume loss, including elevated hemidiaphragm, fissure displacement, and ipsilateral mediastinal shift.
1.management: Lung re-expansion through incentive spirometry, early mobilization, adequate analgesia, chest physiotherapy, and treatment of underlying airway obstruction when present.

clues.text: A 17-year-old girl presents with recurrent episodes of shortness of breath, chest tightness, and cough that occur several times per month, particularly at night
During an episode, physical examination reveals diffuse expiratory wheezing and prolonged expiration. Between episodes, examination is normal
CBC demonstrates mild eosinophilia, and serum IgE levels are elevated
She reports that symptoms are often triggered by exercise, exposure to cats, and seasonal pollen

clues.detail: Flow-volume curve shows a scooped out appearance

clues.text: Following administration of inhaled albuterol, FEV₁ increases by 15% and 350 mL from baseline

1.description: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by variable and reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and episodic respiratory symptoms. Patients typically experience wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, and cough that vary over time and are often triggered by allergens, viral infections, exercise, or cold air. Laboratory findings may reveal eosinophilia and elevated IgE levels in allergic asthma. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates airflow obstruction with a reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio, while bronchodilator testing shows significant reversibility (An increase in FEV1 or FVC of >12% AND >200 mL from baseline is the standard ATS/ERS guideline).
1.management: Trigger avoidance, inhaled corticosteroids, bronchodilators, and biologic therapies for severe disease. Severe exacerbations can result in respiratory failure if untreated.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents with a productive cough that has gradually worsened over several years
He reports bringing up large amounts of sputum most mornings and becomes short of breath when walking uphill
He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 40 years and continues to smoke

Physical examination reveals diffuse wheezing, scattered rhonchi, and mild peripheral edema. Oxygen saturation is 90% on room air. CBC demonstrates a hemoglobin concentration of 188 g/L and hematocrit of 57%
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates an obstructive pattern with a reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio. Chest radiography shows increased bronchovascular markings without focal consolidation

1.description: Chronic Bronchitis is a form of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease characterized by chronic inflammation of the bronchi, mucus gland hyperplasia, and excessive mucus production. The disease is strongly associated with long-term cigarette smoking. Patients present with chronic productive cough, dyspnea, wheezing, and recurrent respiratory infections. Laboratory findings may demonstrate secondary polycythemia due to chronic hypoxemia. Pulmonary function testing reveals persistent airflow obstruction with a reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio. The clinical diagnosis requires productive cough for at least three months per year in two consecutive years.
1.management: Smoking cessation, bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, and supplemental oxygen when indicated.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath over several years
He reports difficulty keeping up with his friends on walks but has little cough and rarely produces sputum
He has a 50-pack-year smoking history
Physical examination reveals a thin man with pursed-lip breathing, prolonged expiration, hyperresonance at the lung bases during percussion, and decreased breath sounds bilaterally
CBC is normal without polycythemia. Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates mild hypoxemia with near-normal carbon dioxide levels
High-resolution CT demonstrates multiple bullae and permanent enlargement of airspaces distal to the terminal bronchioles

1.description: Emphysema is a form of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease characterized by irreversible destruction of alveolar walls and enlargement of distal airspaces. Cigarette smoking is the most common cause, although severe disease at a young age should prompt evaluation for Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency. Patients typically present with progressive dyspnea, decreased exercise tolerance, and minimal sputum production. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates airflow obstruction, while DLCO is reduced due to loss of alveolar surface area. Imaging reveals hyperinflation and bullous changes.
1.management: Smoking cessation, bronchodilators (LAMA ± LABA), pulmonary rehabilitation, and vaccinations. ICS is added for frequent exacerbations with elevated eosinophils. Severe hyperinflation may warrant lung volume reduction (surgery or bronchoscopic valves/coils). Long-term oxygen is used for chronic hypoxemia. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency requires augmentation therapy.

clues.text: A 66-year-old man presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath over the past five years. He now becomes breathless after walking one block and has had several episodes of worsening respiratory symptoms requiring antibiotics
He has smoked 1.5 packs of cigarettes daily for more than 40 years
Physical examination reveals prolonged expiration, diffuse wheezing, decreased breath sounds, and mild use of accessory respiratory muscles
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates mild hypoxemia. CBC reveals secondary polycythemia

clues.detail: Flow-volume curve shows a scooped out appearance

clues.text: Following administration of a bronchodilator, airflow obstruction persists and the post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC ratio remains below 0.70

1.description: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease is a chronic respiratory disorder characterized by persistent, usually progressive airflow obstruction resulting from airway inflammation, emphysema, chronic bronchitis, or a combination of these processes. Cigarette smoking is the most important risk factor. Patients typically present with dyspnea, chronic cough, sputum production, wheezing, and recurrent exacerbations. Laboratory findings may demonstrate hypoxemia, hypercapnia, or secondary polycythemia in advanced disease. Pulmonary function testing confirms the diagnosis by demonstrating persistent airflow obstruction with a post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC ratio below 0.70.
1.management: Smoking cessation, bronchodilators (LAMA ± LABA), pulmonary rehabilitation, and vaccinations. ICS is added for frequent exacerbations with elevated eosinophils. Severe hyperinflation may warrant lung volume reduction (surgery or bronchoscopic valves/coils). Long-term oxygen is used for chronic hypoxemia. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency requires augmentation therapy.

clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with a chronic productive cough that has progressively worsened over several years
She has required hospitalization for recurrent lower respiratory tract infections on several occasions over the past decade
Physical examination reveals coarse inspiratory crackles at both lung bases and digital clubbing
Sputum cultures repeatedly grow Pseudomonas aeruginosa. CBC demonstrates mild neutrophilic leukocytosis during exacerbations

clues.detail: High-resolution CT of the chest reveals dilated bronchi extending to the lung periphery and a "signet-ring sign, where the bronchial lumen is larger than the accompanying pulmonary artery

clues.text: She reports producing large amounts of thick sputum every day

1.description: Bronchiectasis is a chronic respiratory disorder characterized by irreversible dilation and destruction of the bronchial walls due to recurrent infection, inflammation, or impaired mucociliary clearance. Common causes include Cystic Fibrosis, primary ciliary dyskinesia, immunodeficiency, chronic aspiration, and prior severe pulmonary infections. Patients typically present with chronic productive cough, recurrent respiratory infections, hemoptysis, and large-volume purulent sputum production. Physical examination may reveal crackles and digital clubbing. Sputum cultures often identify chronic bacterial colonization, particularly Pseudomonas. High-resolution CT is the diagnostic test of choice and demonstrates bronchial dilation, lack of tapering, and the characteristic signet-ring sign.
1.management: Bronchiectasis management centers on airway clearance techniques, treating underlying causes, and long-term antibiotics for frequent exacerbators. Vaccinations, pulmonary rehabilitation, and mucoactive agents (e.g., hypertonic saline) support care. Macrolides reduce exacerbations in high-risk patients. Pseudomonas eradication is pursued when newly detected. Surgery is reserved for localized refractory disease.

clues.text: A 69-year-old man presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath over the past three years. He now becomes breathless after climbing a single flight of stairs and has developed a persistent dry cough
He is a retired accountant with no significant occupational exposures or PMHx
Physical examination reveals fine bibasilar inspiratory crackles and digital clubbing. No wheezing is present
CBC, renal function, and liver enzymes are normal. Autoimmune serologies including ANA, RF, and anti-CCP antibodies are negative
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced total lung capacity and a markedly decreased diffusion capacity (DLCO)

clues.detail: Axial lung window CT shows diffuse reticular infiltration of both lungs associated with honeycombing, subpleural cysts and areas of air trapping

1.description: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis is a chronic, progressive interstitial lung disease characterized by irreversible pulmonary fibrosis and declining lung function. It typically affects older adults and presents with exertional dyspnea, dry cough, bibasilar inspiratory crackles, and digital clubbing. Laboratory studies are generally nonspecific and are often used to exclude connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced lung volumes and decreased DLCO. High-resolution CT classically reveals a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern with basilar, subpleural fibrosis, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis.
1.management: Antifibrotic agents such as Nintedanib and Pirfenidone, with lung transplantation considered in selected patients. Prednisone and azathioprine (or generally, immunosuppressants) should never be used. They have been shown to worsen survival.

clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and fatigue that have worsened over the past year. She now becomes short of breath after climbing a single flight of stairs
She has no smoking history and no known lung disease
Physical examination reveals a prominent parasternal heave, elevated jugular venous pressure, and a loud pulmonic component of the second heart sound
CBC, renal function, liver enzymes, and thyroid studies are normal. BNP is mildly elevated

Echocardiography demonstrates right ventricular hypertrophy and elevated estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure. Pulmonary function testing and CT imaging show no significant parenchymal lung disease
Right-heart catheterization demonstrates a mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg, pulmonary artery wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg, and elevated pulmonary vascular resistance

1.description: Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension is a progressive disorder characterized by remodeling and narrowing of the pulmonary arterioles, resulting in increased pulmonary vascular resistance and right ventricular pressure overload. Patients typically present with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, presyncope, or syncope. Physical examination may reveal a loud P2, parasternal heave, elevated jugular venous pressure, and signs of right-sided heart failure. Laboratory findings are often nonspecific but BNP may be elevated. Echocardiography is the primary screening test, while right-heart catheterization establishes the diagnosis by demonstrating precapillary pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Treatment may include euvolemia maintenance, endothelin receptor antagonists, phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, prostacyclin analogues, and lung transplantation in advanced disease.

clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents with progressive shortness of breath over the past year. Symptoms are most noticeable when lying flat, and he frequently awakens from sleep feeling as though he cannot catch his breath
He reports increasing fatigue, morning headaches, and daytime somnolence despite sleeping 8 hours per night
Physical examination demonstrates paradoxical abdominal movement when supine. Neurologic examination reveals diffuse muscle wasting in the hands and visible muscle twitching in the forearms
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates chronic compensated hypercapnia. Serum bicarbonate is elevated
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with preserved FEV₁/FVC ratio. Forced vital capacity decreases substantially when measured in the supine position
Electromyography demonstrates widespread active and chronic denervation involving bulbar, cervical, thoracic, and lumbosacral muscle groups

1.description: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by degeneration of upper and lower motor neurons, ultimately leading to respiratory failure. Respiratory manifestations often include exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, morning headaches, sleep disruption, and symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation resulting from diaphragmatic weakness. Pulmonary function testing typically demonstrates a restrictive pattern with preserved FEV₁/FVC ratio and a significant decline in forced vital capacity when supine. Arterial blood gases may reveal chronic hypercapnia. Electromyography demonstrates widespread denervation without sensory involvement. Noninvasive ventilation improves quality of life and survival, while respiratory failure remains the leading cause of death in most patients.
1.management: ALS management centers on multidisciplinary care, riluzole (modest survival benefit), and supportive therapies. Edaravone and tofersen (for SOD1 variants) are additional FDA-approved options. Non-invasive ventilation prolongs survival. Gastrostomy supports nutrition. Symptom management addresses sialorrhea, spasticity, pseudobulbar affect, and pain. Palliative care is integral throughout.

clues.text: A 45-year-old woman presents with persistent nasal congestion, facial pressure, and reduced sense of smell that have gradually worsened over the past several months
She has a history of asthma and reports frequent episodes of nasal symptoms despite multiple courses of antibiotics
Physical examination reveals mucosal edema, thick nasal secretions, and tenderness over the maxillary sinuses
CBC demonstrates mild peripheral eosinophilia. Total serum IgE is mildly elevated
CT imaging of the sinuses demonstrates diffuse mucosal thickening and opacification involving multiple paranasal sinuses
Nasal endoscopy reveals bilateral nasal polyps arising from the middle meatus

1.description: Chronic Rhinosinusitis is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the sinonasal mucosa characterized by nasal obstruction, facial pressure, nasal discharge, and reduced olfaction persisting for more than 12 weeks. The condition may occur with or without nasal polyps and is frequently associated with asthma, allergic disease, and eosinophilic inflammation. Laboratory findings may demonstrate eosinophilia or elevated IgE levels. Diagnosis requires both compatible symptoms and objective evidence of inflammation on nasal endoscopy or CT imaging.
1.management: Treatment includes intranasal corticosteroids, saline irrigation, management of underlying inflammatory disease, biologic therapy in selected patients, and endoscopic sinus surgery for refractory cases.

clues.text: A 17-year-old boy presents with increasing daytime fatigue, morning headaches, and worsening shortness of breath when lying flat. His parents report that he has become increasingly sleepy during school
He has been wheelchair-dependent since age 12
Physical examination reveals marked proximal muscle wasting, scoliosis, weak cough, and paradoxical abdominal movement when supine
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates chronic compensated hypercapnia. Serum bicarbonate is elevated

Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a severe restrictive pattern with preserved FEV₁/FVC ratio. Forced vital capacity is significantly reduced and declines further when measured in the supine position
Genetic testing identifies an out-of-frame deletion in the dystrophin gene, and historical records note markedly elevated creatine kinase levels during childhood

1.description: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy is an X-linked recessive neuromuscular disorder caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene, leading to progressive skeletal, cardiac, and respiratory muscle degeneration. Respiratory complications are a major cause of morbidity and mortality and result from diaphragmatic weakness, impaired cough, scoliosis, and nocturnal hypoventilation. Patients often develop morning headaches, daytime somnolence, orthopnea, recurrent respiratory infections, and chronic hypercapnia. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with declining forced vital capacity. Diagnosis is established through genetic testing and elevated creatine kinase levels.
1.management: Airway clearance therapies, lung-volume recruitment, noninvasive ventilation, corticosteroids, and multidisciplinary care.

clues.text: A 4-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department with three days of cough and increasing difficulty feeding.
The infant attends daycare, and several children recently developed upper respiratory symptoms
Physical examination reveals tachypnea, nasal flaring, subcostal retractions, and diffuse bilateral wheezing with scattered crackles
CBC demonstrates a normal white blood cell count. Blood cultures remain negative
Chest radiography demonstrates hyperinflation and peribronchial thickening without focal lobar consolidation
A nasopharyngeal PCR test identifies Respiratory Syncytial Virus infection

1.description: Bronchiolitis is the most common lower respiratory tract infection in infants and is most frequently caused by Respiratory Syncytial Virus. The disease results from inflammation, edema, and mucus plugging of the small airways. Patients typically present with rhinorrhea followed by cough, wheezing, tachypnea, respiratory distress, and feeding difficulties. Laboratory studies are usually nonspecific and are not routinely required. Chest radiographs may demonstrate hyperinflation and peribronchial thickening. Diagnosis is primarily clinical.
1.management: Supportive and includes hydration, nasal suctioning, and oxygen therapy when indicated. Routine antibiotics, corticosteroids, and bronchodilators are generally not recommended.

clues.text: A 39-year-old man presents with progressively worsening shortness of breath and reduced exercise tolerance over the past several years.
He has smoked only occasionally in the past and has no significant occupational exposures. His father died of liver disease at a relatively young age
Physical examination reveals decreased breath sounds, prolonged expiration, and mild hyperinflation of the chest
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates airflow obstruction and a reduced DLCO. Liver enzymes are mildly elevated
CT imaging demonstrates diffuse panacinar emphysema with greatest involvement of the lower lobes
Serum alpha-1 antitrypsin concentration is markedly reduced, and genotyping identifies a PiZZ phenotype

1.description: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency is an autosomal codominant disorder caused by mutations in the SERPINA1 gene, resulting in reduced levels or dysfunctional alpha-1 antitrypsin. Deficiency of this antiprotease permits unchecked neutrophil elastase activity, leading to progressive alveolar destruction and emphysema. Patients typically develop early-onset COPD, particularly panacinar emphysema involving the lower lobes, often with minimal smoking exposure. Liver disease may also occur because abnormal alpha-1 antitrypsin accumulates within hepatocytes. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates obstructive physiology and reduced DLCO. Diagnosis is confirmed by low serum alpha-1 antitrypsin levels and genotype testing.
1.management: A1AT deficiency management centers on smoking cessation, standard COPD therapy, and IV augmentation therapy (weekly pooled human AAT) for those with established emphysema and severely reduced AAT levels. Liver disease requires alcohol avoidance and transplantation for advanced cases. Surveillance includes serial PFTs and liver monitoring. Family screening is recommended.

clues.text: A 54-year-old man is admitted to the intensive care unit with severe community-acquired pneumonia.
Three days later, he develops rapidly worsening shortness of breath and increasing oxygen requirements despite appropriate antibiotic therapy
He is now requiring high-flow oxygen and has become increasingly tachypneic. Blood cultures remain positive for a gram-negative organism
Physical examination reveals diffuse bilateral crackles and marked respiratory distress. No jugular venous distension or peripheral edema is present

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates diffuse bilateral pulmonary opacities involving multiple lung fields

clues.text: Histologic examination demonstrates diffuse alveolar damage with eosinophilic hyaline membranes lining the alveolar walls

1.description: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome is a severe form of acute lung injury caused by diffuse inflammation and increased permeability of the alveolar-capillary barrier. Common triggers include sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, trauma, pancreatitis, and massive transfusion. Protein-rich fluid leaks into alveoli, causing noncardiogenic pulmonary edema and profound hypoxemia. Patients develop acute respiratory failure with bilateral pulmonary infiltrates and reduced lung compliance. Echocardiography demonstrates no evidence of left-sided heart failure. Histologically, ARDS is characterized by diffuse alveolar damage and hyaline membrane formation.
1.management: Primarily supportive, including lung-protective mechanical ventilation and treatment of the underlying cause.

clues.text: A 52-year-old man presents with excessive daytime sleepiness, difficulty concentrating at work, and frequent morning headaches. He reports feeling unrefreshed despite sleeping 8–9 hours each night
His spouse reports loud nightly snoring
Physical examination reveals obesity, a moderately large neck circumference, and elevated blood pressure
CBC demonstrates a mildly elevated hemoglobin concentration. Thyroid function studies are normal
Overnight pulse oximetry demonstrates recurrent episodes of oxygen desaturation during sleep
Polysomnography demonstrates repeated episodes of airflow cessation despite persistent respiratory effort

1.description: Obstructive Sleep Apnea is a common sleep-related breathing disorder characterized by recurrent collapse of the upper airway during sleep. These episodes cause intermittent hypoxemia, hypercapnia, and repeated arousals that disrupt normal sleep architecture. Risk factors include obesity, male sex, increasing age, craniofacial abnormalities, and large neck circumference. Patients typically present with loud snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness, and morning headaches. Diagnosis is established by polysomnography, which demonstrates repeated obstructive apneas despite ongoing respiratory effort. Untreated OSA increases the risk of hypertension, atrial fibrillation, stroke, pulmonary hypertension, and cardiovascular disease.
1.management: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is the first-line treatment. Weight loss, exercise, alcohol avoidance, are recommended. Oral appliances used for those who cannot tolerate PAP and uvulopalatopharyngoplasty, maxillomandibular advancement, and hypoglossal nerve stimulation is more moderate to severe cases.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with excessive daytime sleepiness, frequent nighttime awakenings, and nonrestorative sleep
He has a history of severe systolic heart failure with an ejection fraction of 25%
Physical examination demonstrates elevated jugular venous pressure and bilateral lower extremity edema. Body mass index is normal
BNP is markedly elevated. CBC and thyroid studies are normal
Overnight monitoring demonstrates recurrent episodes of oxygen desaturation associated with cyclic fluctuations in breathing pattern
Polysomnography demonstrates repeated episodes of absent airflow accompanied by complete absence of respiratory effort

1.description: Central Sleep Apnea is a sleep-related breathing disorder characterized by transient loss of central respiratory drive during sleep. Unlike obstructive sleep apnea, airflow ceases because the brain temporarily fails to initiate breathing rather than because of upper airway obstruction. Common causes include heart failure, stroke, high-altitude exposure, neurologic disease, and chronic opioid use. Patients present with fragmented sleep, excessive daytime sleepiness, and nocturnal awakenings. Polysomnography demonstrates recurrent apneas without respiratory effort. A characteristic breathing pattern known as Cheyne-Stokes respiration may occur in patients with heart failure.
1.management: Central sleep apnea management prioritizes treating the underlying cause (e.g., optimizing heart failure therapy, reducing opioids), followed by CPAP, adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV) (avoid in HFrEF with LVEF ≤45%), nocturnal oxygen, phrenic nerve stimulation, or acetazolamide as adjuncts

clues.text: A 15-year-old boy presents because of a progressively prominent chest wall deformity that has become more noticeable during his adolescent growth spurt. He is otherwise healthy but feels self-conscious during sports and swimming
The deformity first became noticeable during early childhood but has worsened significantly over the past two years
Physical examination reveals a symmetric forward projection of the sternum. Lung and cardiac examinations are otherwise unremarkable
Routine lab tests are unremarkable

clues.detail: Chest CT shows protrusion of the sternal body and xiphisternum. No suspicious osseous or retrosternal lesion

clues.text: Inspection reveals a characteristic ‘pigeon chest’ deformity

1.description: Pectus Carinatum is a congenital chest wall deformity characterized by outward displacement of the sternum, often becoming more prominent during rapid adolescent growth. Most patients are asymptomatic, although some experience cosmetic concerns, exercise intolerance, or chest discomfort. Physical examination reveals a protruding sternum, commonly described as a “pigeon chest.” The condition may occur in isolation or be associated with connective tissue disorders such as Marfan Syndrome and Noonan Syndrome. Diagnosis is clinical, although imaging may be used to assess severity.
1.management: External bracing during adolescence and surgery for severe cases.

clues.text: A 15-year-old boy presents to his family physician because he becomes more fatigued than his peers during soccer games and feels short of breath with intense exercise
His symptoms have become more noticeable over the past year during a period of rapid growth. He has no relevant PMHx.
Cardiopulmonary examination is largely unremarkable, but inspection of the chest reveals a symmetric depression of the lower anterior chest wall centered around the sternum
Complete blood count, electrolyte panel, and cardiac biomarkers are all within normal limits

clues.detail: Chest CT shows posterior displacement of the sternum with mild compression of the right ventricle

clues.text: Further assessment reveals mild scoliosis and an arm-span-to-height ratio greater than expected for age.

1.description: Pectus excavatum is the most common congenital chest wall deformity, characterized by posterior displacement of the sternum due to abnormal costal cartilage growth. It is more common in males and may occur with connective tissue disorders such as Marfan syndrome. Diagnosis begins with physical examination and is confirmed with CT measurement of the Haller index. Most cases are mild, but severe deformities can cause exercise intolerance, cardiopulmonary compression, psychosocial distress, mitral valve prolapse, and arrhythmias.
1.management: Mild cases require reassurance and observation. Symptomatic or severe deformities causing cardiopulmonary compromise or significant psychosocial burden may warrant surgical correction.

clues.text: A 16 year old female presents to her family physician due to shortness of breath on exertion and recent development of intermittent upper back discomfort
Her parents report that she experienced a rapid growth spurt over the past two years, and she has no history of trauma or chronic illness
On inspection, one shoulder sits higher than the other, and forward bending reveals asymmetry of the thoracic back with an exaggerated posterior curvature
CBC, inflammatory markers, and metabolic studies are within normal limits.
Standing spinal radiographs demonstrate a lateral curvature of the thoracic spine with vertebral rotation and increased thoracic sagittal curvature
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive ventilatory defect with reduced total lung capacity

1.description: Kyphoscoliosis is a spinal deformity characterized by combined scoliosis and excessive kyphosis, producing abnormal curvature in both the coronal and sagittal planes. It most commonly develops during adolescence and may be idiopathic, congenital, or associated with neuromuscular disorders. Diagnosis begins with physical examination and is confirmed using standing radiographs to assess spinal curvature. Severe disease may cause chronic pain, restrictive lung disease, pulmonary hypertension, and respiratory failure.
1.management: Monitor mild curves with serial examinations and radiographs. Progressive deformities may require bracing, while severe curvature, respiratory compromise, or pain warrants surgery.

clues.text: A 47-year-old woman presents to her family physician because she has had increasing difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects onto high shelves over the past several months
She reports progressive fatigue and has recently noticed that routine daily activities, such as rising from a chair, require more effort than before
Neurologic examination demonstrates symmetric proximal muscle weakness involving the shoulder and hip girdles, while sensation and reflexes are preserved
Serum creatine kinase, aldolase, AST, and ALT are elevated
MRI of the thighs demonstrates bilateral symmetric muscle edema with areas of inflammation involving proximal muscle groups
Muscle biopsy reveals endomysial inflammatory infiltrates surrounding and invading non-necrotic muscle fibers

1.description: Inflammatory myositis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by immune-mediated skeletal muscle inflammation causing progressive proximal muscle weakness. It most commonly affects middle-aged adults and may occur with other autoimmune diseases or malignancy. Diagnosis is suggested by symmetric proximal weakness and elevated muscle enzymes, then supported by MRI, autoantibody testing, electromyography, and muscle biopsy. Management involves corticosteroids and immunosuppressive therapy. Complications include dysphagia, interstitial lung disease, respiratory muscle weakness, disability, and increased malignancy risk.
1.management: High-dose corticosteroids followed by steroid-sparing immunosuppressive therapy. Monitor muscle strength, creatine kinase levels, swallowing function, pulmonary involvement, and malignancy risk.

clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents to her family physician with several months of progressive shortness of breath, dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance
Respiratory examination reveals fine inspiratory crackles at both lung bases without wheezing or signs of airway obstruction
She notes that her symptoms tend to worsen on certain days and improve when she spends extended periods away from home.
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced diffusion capacity, while routine bloodwork is largely unremarkable
High-resolution CT demonstrates diffuse centrilobular ground-glass nodules with patchy areas of air trapping, creating a mosaic attenuation pattern
Further history reveals that she keeps several pet birds inside her home

1.description: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is an immune-mediated interstitial lung disease caused by repeated inhalation of organic antigens, leading to alveolar and bronchiolar inflammation. Common exposures include birds, mold, hay, and agricultural dusts. Patients present with cough, dyspnea, and fatigue that often correlate with exposure. Diagnosis relies on exposure history, pulmonary function testing, and high-resolution CT, sometimes supplemented by bronchoscopy or biopsy.  Chronic disease may progress to pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure, and pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Identify and eliminate the offending antigen. Corticosteroids may accelerate recovery in symptomatic disease. Monitor lung function and progression to chronic fibrotic lung disease.

clues.text: A 24-year-old man presents to his family physician with gradually worsening lower back pain and stiffness that has persisted for over a year
He reports that his symptoms are worst in the morning, improve with activity, and occasionally wake him during the second half of the night
Examination reveals reduced lumbar spine flexion and decreased chest expansion with deep inspiration
Inflammatory markers are elevated, rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative, and HLA-B27 testing is positive
Pelvic radiographs demonstrate bilateral sacroiliac joint sclerosis and erosive changes with progressive joint space narrowing
Spinal imaging later demonstrates thin vertical bony bridges connecting adjacent vertebral bodies, producing a "bamboo spine" appearance

1.description: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory spondyloarthropathy affecting the axial skeleton, particularly the sacroiliac joints and spine. It most commonly occurs in young men and is strongly associated with HLA-B27. Patients develop inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness, and reduced spinal mobility. Diagnosis is based on characteristic clinical features and imaging evidence of sacroiliitis. Complications include spinal fusion, uveitis, osteoporosis, fractures, and restrictive lung disease.
1.management: Encourage regular exercise and physiotherapy. NSAIDs are first-line therapy. Persistent active disease may require TNF-α or IL-17 inhibitors with ongoing monitoring.

clues.text: A 56-year-old man presents to his family physician with gradually worsening shortness of breath and a chronic nonproductive cough over several years
He has worked for decades in an industry involving frequent exposure to airborne dust despite intermittent use of respiratory protection
Respiratory examination reveals scattered inspiratory crackles, while the remainder of the examination is largely unremarkable
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive ventilatory defect with reduced diffusion capacity. Routine bloodwork is otherwise unremarkable

clues.detail: Coronal CT shows extensive areas of bilateral perihilar and apical consolidation and fibrosis with architectural distortion and apical volume loss. Numerous calcified mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes are seen. Mosaic attenuation and widespread interlobular septal thickening involving both lung fields

clues.text: Rheumatoid factor and ANA lab results are negative.

1.description: Silicosis is a pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation of crystalline silica particles, leading to chronic pulmonary inflammation and progressive fibrotic lung disease. It commonly affects miners, quarry workers, sandblasters, and construction workers with prolonged occupational exposure. Patients present with progressive dyspnea and cough. Diagnosis relies on exposure history, pulmonary function testing, and characteristic upper-lobe nodules with eggshell lymph node calcification on imaging. Complications include progressive massive fibrosis, respiratory failure, tuberculosis, lung cancer, and pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Eliminate further silica exposure and provide supportive care. Monitor pulmonary function regularly, vaccinate appropriately, and screen for tuberculosis and progressive fibrosis.

clues.text: A 34-year-old woman presents to her family physician with several months of fatigue, dry cough, and progressive shortness of breath on exertion.
She has otherwise been healthy but recently developed painful red bumps on the fronts of her legs
Examination reveals tender erythematous nodules over both shins and mild cervical lymphadenopathy
Serum calcium and angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) levels are elevated

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates upper zone reticulonodular opacities with mediastinal and hilar nodal enlargment

clues.text: Biopsy of an affected lymph node reveals noncaseating granulomas in the absence of identifiable infectious organisms

1.description: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation in affected tissues. It most commonly affects young and middle-aged adults and frequently involves the lungs, lymph nodes, skin, and eyes. Patients may present with cough, dyspnea, fatigue, erythema nodosum, or uveitis. Diagnosis is based on compatible clinical and radiographic findings with histologic confirmation and exclusion of alternative causes. Complications include pulmonary fibrosis, hypercalcemia, vision loss, cardiac involvement, and pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Asymptomatic disease may only require observation. Symptomatic organ involvement is treated with corticosteroids, with immunosuppressive agents reserved for refractory cases.

clues.text: A 38-year-old man presents to his family physician with a persistent cough, progressive fatigue, and unintentional weight loss over the past two months.
He also reports occasinal night sweats
Examination reveals a thin, chronically ill-appearing patient with low-grade fever and scattered crackles over the upper lung fields.
Complete blood count demonstrates mild anemia of chronic disease, and inflammatory markers are elevated.

clues.detail: CXR shows bilateral airspace consolidation and pleural effusions. A widened paratracheal stripe is seen too

clues.text: Sputum microscopy demonstrates acid-fast bacilli

1.description: Tuberculosis is a chronic granulomatous infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and transmitted through airborne droplets. Risk factors include exposure to infected individuals, immunosuppression, crowded living conditions, and residence in endemic regions. Patients commonly present with chronic cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Diagnosis involves tuberculin skin testing or interferon-gamma release assays, chest imaging, and microbiologic confirmation. Treatment requires prolonged multidrug therapy. Complications include hemoptysis, cavitary lung destruction (caseating granulomas), miliary dissemination, meningitis, and vertebral osteomyelitis.
1.management: Treat active disease with multidrug therapy, typically including isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Monitor adherence, drug toxicity, and contacts.

clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents to her family physician with several months of progressive shortness of breath, dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance
She reports that her symptoms seem to worsen on weekends and improve when she spends several days away from home
Respiratory examination reveals fine inspiratory crackles bilaterally without wheezing or signs of airflow obstruction
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced diffusion capacity. Routine bloodwork is otherwise unremarkable
High-resolution CT demonstrates diffuse centrilobular ground-glass nodules with patchy mosaic attenuation and areas of air trapping
Further history reveals that she has bred and cared for multiple pigeons in an indoor aviary for many years

1.description: Bird-fancier's lung is a subtype of hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by repeated inhalation of avian proteins found in bird feathers, droppings, and serum.
