It most commonly affects individuals with prolonged exposure to pet birds, pigeons, or aviaries. Patients develop cough, dyspnea, and fatigue that correlate with antigen exposure. Diagnosis relies on exposure history, pulmonary function testing, and characteristic high-resolution CT findings. Management focuses on antigen avoidance and corticosteroids. Chronic exposure may result in irreversible pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure, and pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Eliminate bird antigen exposure and consider corticosteroids for symptomatic disease. Monitor pulmonary function and imaging for progression to chronic fibrotic lung disease.

clues.text: A 63-year-old woman presents to her family physician with gradually worsening shortness of breath and a persistent dry cough over the past year
She has a long-standing history of inflammatory joint disease affecting her hands and reports a remote smoking history
Respiratory examination reveals fine bibasilar inspiratory crackles. Examination of the hands demonstrates ulnar deviation and deformities of several finger joints

Inflammatory markers are elevated, and serologic testing is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced diffusion capacity
High-resolution CT demonstrates peripheral, basilar-predominant reticular opacities with traction bronchiectasis and honeycombing
Histopathology demonstrates a usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern in a patient with established autoimmune inflammatory arthritis

1.description: Rheumatoid arthritis–associated interstitial lung disease is an extra-articular manifestation of rheumatoid arthritis characterized by chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the pulmonary interstitium. It occurs most commonly in older patients with long-standing seropositive disease, particularly smokers. Patients develop progressive dyspnea and nonproductive cough. Diagnosis is suggested by restrictive pulmonary function tests and confirmed with high-resolution CT, often demonstrating a UIP pattern. Complications include pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure, pulmonary hypertension, and increased mortality.
1.management: Treat underlying rheumatoid arthritis and pulmonary inflammation with immunomodulatory therapy. Monitor lung function regularly and provide supportive care for progressive fibrotic disease.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents to his family physician with progressively worsening shortness of breath and reduced exercise tolerance over several years
He worked for decades in shipbuilding and industrial construction before retiring
Respiratory examination reveals fine end-inspiratory crackles at both lung bases and digital clubbing
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive ventilatory defect with reduced diffusion capacity. Routine bloodwork is otherwise unremarkable

clues.detail: UIP pattern characterized by subreticular opacity, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis worse in the bases. Calcified pleural plaques are seen. There is no pulmonary nodule or mass or lobar collapse

clues.text: Histopathology demonstrates golden-brown, beaded rods with an iron-containing protein coating within the lung parenchyma

1.description: Asbestosis is a chronic fibrotic interstitial lung disease caused by prolonged inhalation of asbestos fibers. It most commonly affects workers in shipbuilding, construction, insulation, and manufacturing industries after a long latency period. Patients develop progressive dyspnea, cough, and restrictive lung disease. Diagnosis relies on occupational exposure history, pulmonary function testing, and imaging demonstrating basilar fibrosis and pleural plaques. Management is supportive and focuses on exposure cessation. Complications include respiratory failure, lung cancer, malignant mesothelioma, and pulmonary hypertension.
1.management: Eliminate further asbestos exposure and optimize pulmonary health through smoking cessation, vaccination, and supportive care. Monitor for malignancy and progressive respiratory impairment

clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents to her family physician with gradually worsening shortness of breath and a persistent dry cough over the past year
She reports several years of episodic finger discoloration and pain when exposed to cold temperatures, along with progressive tightening of the skin on her hands
Respiratory examination reveals fine bibasilar inspiratory crackles. Examination of the hands demonstrates taut, shiny skin with reduced finger mobility
Antinuclear antibody testing is positive, and anti-topoisomerase I (anti-Scl-70) antibodies are detected. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive pattern with reduced diffusion capacity
High-resolution CT demonstrates bilateral basilar-predominant ground-glass opacities, reticulation, and traction bronchiectasis
Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates dilated capillary loops with capillary dropout in a patient with Raynaud phenomenon and skin thickening

1.description: Systemic sclerosis–associated interstitial lung disease is a pulmonary manifestation of systemic sclerosis characterized by immune-mediated inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the lung interstitium. It occurs most commonly in patients with diffuse cutaneous disease and anti-Scl-70 antibodies. Patients present with exertional dyspnea, dry cough, Raynaud phenomenon, and skin thickening. Diagnosis relies on autoantibody testing, pulmonary function testing, and high-resolution CT. Management includes immunosuppressive therapy and monitoring. Complications include pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure, pulmonary hypertension, and increased mortality.
1.management: Treat progressive lung disease with immunomodulatory therapy and monitor pulmonary function regularly. Manage systemic sclerosis manifestations and screen for pulmonary hypertension

clues.text: A 71-year-old man presents to his family physician with progressively worsening shortness of breath and a nonproductive cough over the past several months
He has a history of atrial fibrillation and has not had any recent infectious symptoms, travel, or environmental exposures
Respiratory examination reveals fine bilateral inspiratory crackles, while cardiac examination demonstrates an irregularly irregular rhythm
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive ventilatory defect with reduced diffusion capacity. Infectious workup is negative
High-resolution CT demonstrates bilateral patchy ground-glass opacities and areas of interstitial infiltrates, predominantly in the peripheral lung fields
Bronchoalveolar lavage and lung biopsy demonstrate lipid-laden foamy macrophages within the alveolar spaces

1.description: Amiodarone-induced pneumonitis is a drug-related interstitial lung disease caused by pulmonary toxicity from chronic amiodarone exposure. Risk increases with higher cumulative doses, advanced age, and prolonged therapy. Patients develop progressive dyspnea, nonproductive cough, and restrictive lung disease. Diagnosis relies on medication history, exclusion of infection and heart failure, pulmonary function testing, and characteristic imaging findings. Management involves discontinuing amiodarone and often initiating corticosteroids. Complications include pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory failure, and death if unrecognized.
1.management: Immediately discontinue amiodarone and exclude alternative causes of pulmonary symptoms. Corticosteroids are commonly used for significant disease, with close respiratory follow-up.

clues.text: A 68-year-old woman presents to her family physician with progressively worsening shortness of breath and a persistent dry cough over several weeks
She has experienced recurrent urinary tract infections and has been taking a daily medication for prophylaxis over the past several months
Respiratory examination reveals bilateral fine inspiratory crackles without evidence of volume overload or focal consolidation
Pulmonary function testing demonstrates a restrictive ventilatory defect with reduced diffusion capacity. Mild peripheral eosinophilia is present
High-resolution CT demonstrates bilateral ground-glass opacities and patchy interstitial infiltrates
Review of her medication list reveals prolonged nitrofurantoin use for recurrent urinary tract infection prophylaxis

1.description: Nitrofurantoin-induced pneumonitis is a drug-induced lung disease resulting from hypersensitivity reactions or chronic pulmonary toxicity associated with nitrofurantoin exposure. It occurs most commonly in older women receiving long-term therapy for recurrent urinary tract infection prophylaxis. Patients develop dyspnea, cough, and restrictive lung disease, sometimes accompanied by eosinophilia. Diagnosis relies on medication history, exclusion of alternative causes, pulmonary function testing, and characteristic imaging findings. Complications include pulmonary fibrosis, chronic respiratory impairment, and respiratory failure.
1.management: Immediately discontinue nitrofurantoin and monitor for clinical improvement. Corticosteroids may be considered in severe cases. Follow pulmonary function and imaging resolution.

clues.text: A 19-year-old university student presents to his family physician because he repeatedly falls asleep during lectures despite getting what he believes is an adequate amount of sleep each night
He reports that these episodes have become more frequent over the past year and are beginning to interfere with his academic performance and social life
Physical and neurologic examinations are unremarkable, but he describes several episodes of sudden muscle weakness triggered by laughter
Routine bloodwork, including thyroid function testing and metabolic studies, is normal. Overnight polysomnography demonstrates no alternative explanation for excessive daytime sleepiness
Brain MRI is unremarkable without structural abnormalities
Multiple sleep latency testing demonstrates a mean sleep latency of less than 8 minutes with two or more sleep-onset REM periods

1.description: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurologic sleep disorder caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin)-producing neurons, resulting in dysregulation of sleep-wake cycles. It typically begins during adolescence or early adulthood and may be associated with specific HLA haplotypes. Patients develop excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations. Diagnosis is established with polysomnography followed by multiple sleep latency testing. Complications include accidents, academic difficulties, and impaired quality of life.
1.management: Treat excessive daytime sleepiness with wake-promoting agents and encourage scheduled naps. Cataplexy may require REM-suppressing medications and ongoing sleep medicine follow-up.

clues.text: A 23-year-old woman presents to her family physician because she feels excessively sleepy throughout the day despite regularly sleeping more than eight hours each night
She reports difficulty waking in the morning and often requires multiple alarms, but denies shift work, substance use, or significant psychosocial stressors
Physical and neurologic examinations are unremarkable. She does not report episodes of sudden muscle weakness, vivid hallucinations, or sleep paralysis

Routine bloodwork, including thyroid function studies, complete blood count, and metabolic testing, is normal. Overnight polysomnography reveals adequate sleep duration without evidence of sleep-disordered breathing
Brain MRI demonstrates no structural abnormalities
Multiple sleep latency testing demonstrates a mean sleep latency of less than 8 minutes without sleep-onset REM periods

1.description: Idiopathic hypersomnolence is a central disorder of hypersomnolence characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness despite adequate or prolonged sleep duration. It typically affects adolescents and young adults and is diagnosed after excluding secondary causes such as sleep deprivation, medications, psychiatric illness, and sleep-disordered breathing. Patients experience persistent sleepiness, prolonged nocturnal sleep, and severe sleep inertia. Diagnosis relies on polysomnography and multiple sleep latency testing. Complications include impaired academic performance, occupational difficulties, and reduced quality of life.
1.management: Optimize sleep hygiene and treat excessive daytime sleepiness with wake-promoting medications. Monitor functional impairment and reassess for alternative sleep disorders if symptoms evolve.

clues.text: A full-term newborn develops respiratory distress and repeated episodes of coughing and cyanosis during the first attempted feeding shortly after birth
The pregnancy was complicated by excessive amniotic fluid accumulation, and prenatal ultrasounds did not identify a clear cause
The infant demonstrates excessive oral secretions and develops choking, coughing, and oxygen desaturation whenever feeding is attempted
Routine neonatal laboratory investigations are unremarkable. Blood gas analysis demonstrates hypoxemia during feeding episodes
A radiograph obtained after attempted passage of a nasogastric tube demonstrates the tube coiled within the upper mediastinum, with air present throughout the stomach and intestines
Further evaluation reveals the most common congenital pattern: proximal esophageal atresia with a distal communication between the trachea and esophagus

1.description: Tracheoesophageal fistula is a congenital foregut malformation characterized by an abnormal connection between the trachea and esophagus, most commonly occurring with esophageal atresia. It typically presents in the neonatal period with choking, coughing, cyanosis during feeds, and excessive secretions. Polyhydramnios is a common prenatal clue. Diagnosis is suggested by inability to pass a nasogastric tube and confirmed radiographically. Complications include aspiration pneumonia, feeding difficulties, gastroesophageal reflux, tracheomalacia, and recurrent respiratory infections.
1.management: Prevent aspiration with feeding cessation, suctioning, and supportive care. Definitive treatment is surgical repair, followed by monitoring for reflux and respiratory complications.

clues.text: A full-term newborn develops severe respiratory distress within minutes of birth, requiring supplemental oxygen and ventilatory support
Prenatal ultrasound demonstrated excess amniotic fluid and difficulty visualizing normal abdominal structures in their expected location
Examination reveals marked respiratory distress with decreased breath sounds over the left hemithorax and a scaphoid-appearing abdomen
Arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates hypoxemia and respiratory acidosis despite escalating respiratory support
Chest radiography demonstrates multiple air-filled bowel loops occupying the left hemithorax with displacement of the mediastinum to the right
The defect most commonly occurs through the posterolateral portion of the diaphragm and is associated with pulmonary hypoplasia

1.description: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia is a developmental defect of the diaphragm that permits abdominal viscera to herniate into the thoracic cavity, impairing normal lung development. It most commonly results from a left-sided posterolateral (Bochdalek) defect. Newborns present with respiratory distress, decreased breath sounds, and a scaphoid abdomen. Diagnosis is suggested prenatally by ultrasound and confirmed with postnatal imaging.Complications include pulmonary hypoplasia, persistent pulmonary hypertension, respiratory failure, and long-term feeding difficulties.
1.management: Prioritize respiratory stabilization and management of pulmonary hypertension. Avoid bag-mask ventilation when possible. Definitive treatment involves surgical repair after cardiopulmonary stabilization

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to his family physician with intermittent episodes of coughing up blood over the past several months
He was treated for a chronic pulmonary infection many years ago and has otherwise been well since completing therapy
Respiratory examination is largely unremarkable aside from occasional crackles over the upper lung fields. He is not febrile and appears clinically stable
Complete blood count and inflammatory markers are within normal limits. Sputum cultures are negative for bacterial pathogens
Chest CT demonstrates a rounded soft-tissue mass within a pre-existing upper lobe cavity, separated from the cavity wall by a crescent of air
Repeat imaging obtained in a different patient position demonstrates movement of the intracavitary mass within the cavity

1.description: Pulmonary mycetoma, most commonly an aspergilloma, is a fungal ball formed by colonization of a pre-existing lung cavity, often left by prior tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, or other cavitary lung diseases. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with hemoptysis, which can be life-threatening. Diagnosis is suggested by characteristic imaging showing an intracavitary mass with an air crescent and confirmed through microbiologic or serologic evaluation. Complications include massive hemoptysis, recurrent bleeding, and rarely invasive fungal infection.
1.management: Monitor asymptomatic patients. Significant or recurrent hemoptysis may require surgical resection or bronchial artery embolization. Consider antifungal therapy in selected cases.

clues.text: A 46-year-old man presents to his family physician with several months of fatigue, persistent sinus congestion, and a chronic cough occasionally streaked with blood
He has required multiple courses of antibiotics for recurrent "sinus infections" over the past year but has experienced little improvement
Examination reveals crusting and ulceration of the nasal mucosa, while chest auscultation demonstrates scattered crackles bilaterally
Urinalysis demonstrates hematuria with red blood cell casts, inflammatory markers are elevated, and serum creatinine is increased

clues.detail: Chest CT demonstrates pulmonary nodules and an area of central cavitation

clues.text: Serologic testing reveals positivity for proteinase-3 antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (PR3-ANCA, c-ANCA)

1.description: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is an ANCA-associated necrotizing small-vessel vasculitis characterized by granulomatous inflammation involving the upper respiratory tract, lungs, and kidneys. It typically affects middle-aged adults and presents with chronic sinus disease, pulmonary symptoms, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Diagnosis is suggested by compatible clinical findings, PR3-ANCA positivity, and tissue biopsy demonstrating necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis. Management involves glucocorticoids with immunosuppressive therapy. Complications include diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, renal failure, subglottic stenosis, and permanent organ damage.
1.management: Induce remission with glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide. Transition to maintenance immunosuppression and monitor closely for renal and pulmonary complications.

clues.text: A 39-year-old woman presents to her family physician with progressively worsening shortness of breath, chronic cough, and increasing fatigue over several months
She has a long-standing history of adult-onset asthma and recurrent episodes of nasal congestion requiring treatment with inhaled and oral medications
Respiratory examination reveals diffuse expiratory wheezing, and neurologic examination demonstrates decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of her right foot
Complete blood count demonstrates marked peripheral eosinophilia, and inflammatory markers are elevated
Chest CT demonstrates transient, patchy bilateral pulmonary infiltrates that do not conform to a single lobar distribution
Biopsy demonstrates eosinophil-rich necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis involving small- to medium-sized vessels

1.description: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis is an ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis characterized by asthma, eosinophilia, and eosinophil-rich granulomatous inflammation. It typically develops in adults with long-standing asthma and chronic rhinosinusitis. Patients may present with pulmonary infiltrates, peripheral neuropathy, skin lesions, or systemic symptoms. Diagnosis relies on clinical features, eosinophilia, ANCA testing, imaging, and biopsy findings. Management includes glucocorticoids and immunosuppressive therapy for severe disease. Complications include mononeuritis multiplex, myocarditis, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, and end-organ damage.
1.management: Treat with systemic glucocorticoids and add immunosuppressive or biologic therapy for organ-threatening disease. Monitor eosinophil counts and cardiac involvement closely.

1.diagnosis: Goodpasture's Syndrome (antiglomerular basement membrane antibody disease [anti-GBM])

clues.text: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath, fatigue, and several episodes of coughing up blood over the past week
He recently recovered from a viral respiratory illness and has a history of cigarette smoking but was otherwise healthy before symptom onset
Examination reveals bibasilar crackles and mild lower extremity edema. He appears pale and mildly tachypneic
Hemoglobin is decreased, serum creatinine is elevated, and urinalysis demonstrates hematuria with red blood cell casts

clues.detail: Chest CT shows patchy regions of ground glass opacity throughout the lungs. No interlobular septal thickening. No pleural effusion

clues.text: Kidney biopsy with immunofluorescence demonstrates smooth linear deposition of IgG along the glomerular basement membrane

1.description: Goodpasture syndrome is an autoimmune disorder caused by antibodies directed against type IV collagen within glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. It most commonly affects young adults and presents with pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Patients develop hemoptysis, dyspnea, hematuria, and acute kidney injury. Diagnosis relies on anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies and biopsy demonstrating linear IgG deposition. Management includes plasmapheresis, glucocorticoids, and immunosuppression. Complications include diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, respiratory failure, end-stage kidney disease, and death.
1.management: Initiate urgent plasmapheresis with high-dose glucocorticoids and immunosuppression. Monitor pulmonary hemorrhage and renal function closely to prevent irreversible organ damage.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a sudden episode of large-volume hematemesis associated with lightheadedness and weakness
He reports no prior history of peptic ulcer disease, chronic liver disease, or significant gastrointestinal symptoms before this event
Examination reveals tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, and signs of acute blood loss without abdominal tenderness or peritoneal findings
Hemoglobin is decreased, while coagulation studies and liver function tests are within normal limits
Upper endoscopy demonstrates active arterial bleeding from a minute mucosal defect in the proximal stomach, with otherwise normal surrounding mucosa
Endoscopic evaluation identifies a large-caliber submucosal artery that protrudes through a tiny erosion, most commonly along the lesser curvature of the stomach near the gastroesophageal junction

1.description: A Dieulafoy lesion is a vascular anomaly in which an abnormally large-caliber submucosal artery erodes through a small mucosal defect, causing potentially massive gastrointestinal bleeding. It most commonly occurs in the proximal stomach along the lesser curvature but can arise elsewhere in the gastrointestinal tract. Patients present with recurrent or sudden severe upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Diagnosis is established by endoscopy. Management is primarily endoscopic hemostasis. Complications include recurrent bleeding, hemorrhagic shock, transfusion requirements, and death.
1.management: Stabilize hemodynamics and perform urgent endoscopic hemostasis using clips, thermal coagulation, or injection therapy. Refractory bleeding may require angiographic or surgical intervention.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath, pleuritic chest pain, and persistent fever despite completing a course of antibiotics for pneumonia
Two weeks earlier, he was treated for a community-acquired lower respiratory tract infection but reports progressive symptoms rather than improvement
Examination reveals reduced chest expansion on the right side, dullness to percussion, and markedly decreased breath sounds over the right lung base
Leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers are present. Thoracentesis yields cloudy fluid with low pH, low glucose, and elevated lactate dehydrogenase

clues.detail: Chest CT demonstrates a large pleural collection with thickened surrounding pleura (split pleura sign) and multiple gas bubbles. The adjacent lung is compressed and collapsed

clues.text: Gram stain and culture of pleural fluid demonstrate bacterial infection within the pleural space

1.description: Empyema is the accumulation of purulent fluid within the pleural space, most commonly arising as a complication of bacterial pneumonia. Risk factors include pneumonia, thoracic surgery, chest trauma, and aspiration. Patients typically present with fever, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, and persistent infection despite antibiotic therapy. Diagnosis relies on imaging and thoracentesis demonstrating infected exudative pleural fluid. Management requires antimicrobial therapy and pleural drainage. Complications include fibrothorax, trapped lung, sepsis, respiratory failure, and prolonged hospitalization.
1.management: systemic antibiotics and pleural drainage via chest tube (thoracostomy), with intrapleural tPA plus DNase for loculated collections or VATS decortication if medical therapy fails.

clues.text: A 64-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, productive cough, and worsening shortness of breath over the past week
She was recently diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia and has completed several days of antibiotic therapy with only partial improvement
Examination reveals decreased breath sounds, reduced tactile fremitus, and dullness to percussion over the lower portion of the left hemithorax
Leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers are present. Thoracentesis demonstrates an exudative pleural effusion without evidence of frank pus

clues.detail: Chest CT shows left lower lobe consolidation and atelectasis. Moderate left pleural effusion extending to the oblique fissure

clues.text: Pleural fluid analysis demonstrates a neutrophil-predominant exudate occurring in association with bacterial pneumonia but without microorganisms identified on Gram stain or culture

1.description: Parapneumonic effusion is a pleural effusion that develops secondary to pneumonia or another pulmonary infection. It results from inflammation of the pleural surfaces and ranges from uncomplicated sterile exudates to complicated effusions that may progress to empyema. Patients present with symptoms of pneumonia accompanied by dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Diagnosis relies on imaging and thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis. Management depends on severity and fluid characteristics. Complications include loculation, empyema, fibrothorax, and respiratory compromise.
1.management: Treat the underlying pneumonia with appropriate antibiotics. Perform thoracentesis when indicated and drain complicated effusions to prevent progression to empyema.

clues.text: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset right-sided chest pain and shortness of breath that began while studying for an exam
He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no history of lung disease or recent trauma
Examination reveals decreased breath sounds and reduced chest expansion over the left hemithorax. Oxygen saturation is mildly reduced
Complete blood count, cardiac biomarkers, and inflammatory markers are within normal limits. Arterial blood gas demonstrates mild hypoxemia

clues.detail: CXR reveals a visible visceral pleural edge and loss of pulmonary markings peripheral to the line. There is a slight mediastinal shift away from the compromised lung

clues.text: Thoracoscopic evaluation identifies rupture of a small apical subpleural bleb

1.description: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs when air accumulates within the pleural space without preceding trauma or known lung disease, usually due to rupture of apical subpleural blebs. It most commonly affects tall, thin adolescents and young adults, particularly males and smokers. Patients present with sudden pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. Diagnosis is confirmed by chest imaging demonstrating a pneumothorax. Management ranges from observation to needle aspiration or chest tube placement. Complications include recurrence, tension pneumothorax, and respiratory compromise.
1.management: Small stable pneumothoraces may be observed with oxygen therapy. Larger or symptomatic cases require aspiration or chest tube drainage and recurrence prevention.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset left-sided chest pain and worsening shortness of breath that developed while walking to the mailbox
He has a long history of cigarette smoking and chronic respiratory symptoms requiring daily inhaler therapy
Examination reveals tachypnea, decreased breath sounds over the left hemithorax, and increased work of breathing compared with his baseline
Arterial blood gas demonstrates hypoxemia. Routine bloodwork is otherwise unremarkable
Chest radiography demonstrates a visible pleural line with absence of peripheral lung markings and partial collapse of the left lung. Hyperinflated lungs are also noted
Review of prior imaging demonstrates severe centrilobular emphysema with large apical bullae

1.description: Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs when air accumulates in the pleural space due to an underlying lung disease, most commonly chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with emphysematous bullae. It typically affects older adults and causes more severe symptoms than primary spontaneous pneumothorax because of reduced pulmonary reserve. Diagnosis is confirmed by chest imaging demonstrating a pneumothorax and underlying lung pathology. Management generally requires pleural drainage. Complications include respiratory failure, recurrence, tension pneumothorax, and increased mortality.
1.management: Provide supplemental oxygen and perform chest tube drainage in most cases. Address the underlying lung disease and consider definitive recurrence-prevention strategies.

clues.text: A 31-year-old woman presents to her family physician with a persistent cough that began after a recent upper respiratory tract infection and has continued for nearly two weeks
She reports several days of nasal congestion, sore throat, and low-grade fever before the cough developed. She is otherwise healthy and has no history of chronic lung disease
Examination reveals scattered wheezes and rhonchi that improve with coughing. She is afebrile and not in respiratory distress
Complete blood count and inflammatory markers are within normal limits or only mildly elevated

clues.detail: Normal CXR. Some degree of hyperinflation due to mildly depressed diaphragms

clues.text: The cough persists for several weeks despite resolution of other symptoms, most commonly following a viral infection and without radiographic evidence of pneumonia.

1.description: Acute bronchitis is a self-limited inflammation of the bronchi, most commonly caused by viral respiratory infections. It affects otherwise healthy individuals and typically follows an upper respiratory tract infection. Patients present with an acute cough, often lasting several weeks, with or without sputum production. Diagnosis is clinical and supported by the absence of focal findings or radiographic evidence of pneumonia. Management is primarily supportive. Complications are uncommon but include transient bronchial hyperreactivity, unnecessary antibiotic exposure, and missed alternative diagnoses.
1.management: Provide reassurance and supportive care with hydration and symptom control. Antibiotics are generally not indicated unless a specific bacterial cause is suspected.

clues.text: A 10-year-old boy is brought to his family physician with a two-day history of sore throat, painful swallowing, and fever
Several classmates have recently been absent from school with similar symptoms
Examination reveals an erythematous pharynx with tonsillar enlargement, patchy tonsillar exudates, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy
He has no cough or rhinorrhea
Complete blood count demonstrates leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance. A rapid antigen detection test is positive
Throat culture grows a β-hemolytic organism that is bacitracin-sensitive

1.description: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis is an acute bacterial infection of the oropharynx caused by Streptococcus pyogenes. It most commonly affects school-aged children and spreads through respiratory droplets. Patients present with fever, sore throat, tonsillar exudates, and tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, typically without cough. Diagnosis is established with rapid antigen testing or throat culture. Treatment reduces symptom duration and prevents complications. Potential complications include peritonsillar abscess, acute rheumatic fever, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, and scarlet fever.
1.management: Treat with penicillin or amoxicillin to reduce transmission and prevent rheumatic fever. Provide analgesia, hydration, and return precautions for complications.

clues.text: A 72-year-old man develops worsening cough, fever, and shortness of breath while recovering from a hip fracture repair in hospital
He had been recovering well postoperatively but develops symptoms five days after admission. His medical history is significant for COPD and type 2 diabetes mellitus
Examination reveals tachypnea, fever, and crackles over the right lower lung field. Oxygen saturation is lower than previously documented during his admission
Complete blood count demonstrates neutrophilic leukocytosis, and inflammatory markers are elevated. Arterial blood gas reveals hypoxemia
Chest radiography demonstrates a new right lower lobe air-space opacity not present on admission imaging
Respiratory culture grows a multidrug-resistant gram-negative organism after pneumonia develops more than 48 hours following hospital admission

1.management: Empiric antibiotic therapy covering S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and other gram-negative bacilli for all patients with suspected HAP. MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid): Add only if risk factors for MDR pathogens are present, the unit has >10–20% MRSA prevalence, or local prevalence is unknown. Without MRSA risk: Monotherapy with piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, levofloxacin, imipenem, or meropenem is suggested. A 7-day course is recommended (strong recommendation), with adjustment based on clinical, radiologic, and laboratory response.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man in the intensive care unit develops increasing oxygen requirements and recurrent fevers while recovering from septic shock
He has been mechanically ventilated for six days following emergency abdominal surgery and had initially shown signs of clinical improvement
Examination reveals fever, tachycardia, purulent tracheal secretions, and new coarse crackles over the right lung field
Complete blood count demonstrates neutrophilic leukocytosis, inflammatory markers are elevated, and arterial blood gas analysis demonstrates worsening hypoxemia
Chest radiography demonstrates a new right lower lobe infiltrate that was not present on previous imaging obtained after intubation
Quantitative culture of lower respiratory tract secretions obtained after more than 48 hours of mechanical ventilation grows a multidrug-resistant gram-negative organism

1.description: Ventilator-associated pneumonia is a hospital-acquired lung infection that develops more than 48 hours after endotracheal intubation and initiation of mechanical ventilation. Common pathogens include Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and other multidrug-resistant gram-negative organisms. Patients develop fever, purulent respiratory secretions, worsening oxygenation, and new pulmonary infiltrates. Diagnosis combines clinical findings, imaging, and microbiologic sampling. Management requires prompt empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics followed by culture-directed therapy. Complications include respiratory failure, sepsis, prolonged mechanical ventilation, and increased mortality.
1.management: Empiric antibiotic coverage for S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and other gram-negative bacilli in all patients with suspected VAP. MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid): Only when risk factors for MDR pathogens are present, the unit has >10–20% MRSA prevalence, or local prevalence is unknown. Otherwise, cover MSSA with piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, levofloxacin, imipenem, or meropenem. Dual anti-pseudomonal therapy: Recommended empirically when MDR risk factors are present, to increase the probability that at least one agent is active against the causative pathogen. A 7-day course is recommended (strong recommendation, moderate-quality evidence), with adjustment based on clinical, radiologic, and laboratory response.

clues.text: A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever, fatigue, dry cough, and progressive shortness of breath over the past five days

1.description: COVID-19 is a viral respiratory illness caused by SARS-CoV-2, an enveloped RNA coronavirus that enters host cells through ACE2 receptors. Disease severity ranges from asymptomatic infection to severe pneumonia and acute respiratory distress syndrome. Risk factors for severe illness include advanced age, obesity, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and immunosuppression. Diagnosis is established through nucleic acid amplification testing. Management depends on disease severity and may include supportive care, antiviral therapy, and corticosteroids. Complications include respiratory failure, thrombosis, myocarditis, and long COVID.
1.management: Provide supportive care and oxygen as needed. Use antiviral therapy for eligible patients and corticosteroids for hypoxemic disease while monitoring complications.

clues.text: A 27-year-old woman presents to her family physician with the sudden onset of fever, chills, severe fatigue, headache, and diffuse muscle aches that began yesterday

Chest radiography demonstrates no focal consolidation or pleural effusion

1.description: Influenza is an acute respiratory infection caused by influenza viruses, most commonly influenza A and B. Transmission occurs through respiratory droplets and seasonal outbreaks. Patients typically develop abrupt fever, myalgias, headache, fatigue, cough, and upper respiratory symptoms. Diagnosis is usually clinical but may be confirmed with molecular testing. Management is primarily supportive, with antiviral therapy considered for high-risk or severely ill patients. Complications include viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia, myocarditis, encephalitis, and respiratory failure.
1.management: Provide supportive care and initiate antiviral therapy early in high-risk or hospitalized patients. Monitor for secondary bacterial pneumonia and respiratory complications.

clues.text: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a barking cough and noisy breathing that worsened overnight

Examination reveals inspiratory stridor, hoarseness, and suprasternal retractions. Lung auscultation is otherwise clear bilaterally
Routine laboratory investigations are unremarkable. Respiratory viral testing identifies an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus

The causative pathogen belongs to the Paramyxoviridae family

1.description: Parainfluenza virus is an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus and the leading cause of croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in young children. Infection causes inflammation and edema of the subglottic airway, producing a characteristic barking cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor. Transmission occurs through respiratory droplets and direct contact. Diagnosis is usually clinical, though imaging may demonstrate the steeple sign. Management is supportive, with corticosteroids and nebulized epinephrine used for more severe disease. Complications include respiratory distress, hypoxemia, and rarely respiratory failure.
1.management: Administer corticosteroids for all symptomatic patients. Moderate or severe disease may require nebulized epinephrine and observation until respiratory distress improves.

clues.text: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department with worsening cough, difficulty feeding, and increasing work of breathing over the past three days

Pulse oximetry demonstrates mild hypoxemia. Routine bloodwork is unremarkable, and respiratory viral testing is positive for a common pediatric respiratory virus
Chest radiography demonstrates hyperinflation with peribronchial thickening and patchy areas of subsegmental atelectasis
The causative virus induces fusion of infected respiratory epithelial cells, resulting in the formation of multinucleated syncytia

1.description: Respiratory syncytial virus is an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus and the leading cause of bronchiolitis in infants. Infection spreads through respiratory secretions and is particularly severe in premature infants and those with cardiopulmonary disease. Patients develop rhinorrhea, cough, wheezing, and respiratory distress. Diagnosis is usually clinical, though viral testing may confirm infection. Management is primarily supportive. Complications include apnea, dehydration, respiratory failure, recurrent wheezing, and hospitalization, particularly among high-risk infants.
1.management: Provide supportive care with hydration, nasal suctioning, and oxygen when needed. Severe cases may require hospitalization and respiratory support. Avoid routine antibiotics.

clues.text: A 67-year-old woman presents to her family physician with a chronic cough, progressive fatigue, and unintentional weight loss over the past year.

1.description: Lady Windermere syndrome is a form of pulmonary nontuberculous mycobacterial infection, usually caused by Mycobacterium avium complex, affecting older women without major pre-existing lung disease. It classically involves the right middle lobe and lingula, producing chronic cough, fatigue, weight loss, and recurrent respiratory infections. Diagnosis relies on characteristic imaging findings and repeated positive respiratory cultures. Management requires prolonged multidrug antimicrobial therapy. Complications include progressive bronchiectasis, chronic respiratory symptoms, hemoptysis, and irreversible structural lung damage.
1.management: Three-times-weekly macrolide-based regimen (azithromycin, rifampicin, ethambutol) for ≥12 months after sputum culture conversion, combined with airway clearance techniques. Airway clearance, nutritional support, and pulmonary rehabilitation is an essential adjunct.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever, cough, and worsening shortness of breath despite receiving broad-spectrum antibiotics for several days

Biopsy demonstrates septate hyphae with acute-angle branching invading blood vessels

1.description: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis is an opportunistic fungal infection caused by Aspergillus species, most commonly affecting patients with prolonged neutropenia, hematologic malignancies, transplantation, or significant immunosuppression. Infection begins after inhalation of spores and can invade pulmonary blood vessels, causing tissue necrosis and hemorrhage. Diagnosis relies on imaging, fungal biomarkers such as galactomannan, and microbiologic or histopathologic confirmation. Management requires prompt antifungal therapy. Complications include massive hemoptysis, disseminated infection, respiratory failure, and death.
1.management: Initiate prompt antifungal therapy, typically with voriconazole. Reverse immunosuppression when possible and monitor closely for dissemination, hemorrhage, and respiratory failure.

clues.text: A 34-year-old man presents to his family physician with two weeks of fever, dry cough, fatigue, and progressive shortness of breath

1.description: Histoplasmosis is a fungal infection caused by Histoplasma capsulatum, a dimorphic fungus found in soil contaminated with bird or bat droppings. Infection occurs through inhalation of fungal spores and most commonly causes a self-limited pulmonary illness. Risk factors include cave exploration, demolition work, and exposure to bird or bat habitats. Diagnosis relies on antigen testing, serology, culture, or tissue biopsy. Management depends on disease severity. Complications include chronic pulmonary disease, mediastinal fibrosis, and disseminated infection in immunocompromised individuals.
1.management: Mild disease may only require observation. Moderate or severe infection is treated with antifungal therapy, and disseminated disease requires prolonged treatment and follow-up.

clues.text: A 41-year-old man presents to his family physician with several weeks of cough, fever, fatigue, and unintentional weight loss
He recently returned from a hunting trip where he spent significant time clearing brush and disturbing soil near rivers and wooded areas
Respiratory examination reveals scattered crackles. In addition, several painless verrucous skin lesions with irregular raised borders are noted on his forearm
Routine bloodwork demonstrates elevated inflammatory markers. Fungal culture from a skin lesion is pending.

Microscopy demonstrates broad-based budding yeasts with thick, refractile cell walls

1.description: Blastomycosis is a fungal infection caused by Blastomyces species, dimorphic fungi found in moist soil and decaying organic matter, particularly near waterways. Infection occurs through inhalation of spores and primarily affects the lungs, although dissemination to skin, bone, and the genitourinary system is common. Diagnosis relies on culture, antigen testing, or visualization of characteristic broad-based budding yeasts. Management depends on disease severity and extent of dissemination. Complications include respiratory failure, osteomyelitis, central nervous system involvement, and disseminated disease.
1.management: Treat mild to moderate disease with itraconazole. Severe, disseminated, or central nervous system infection requires amphotericin B followed by prolonged azole therapy.

clues.text: A 14-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department with worsening cough, fever, and difficulty breathing over the past three days

clues.text: The causative pathogen belongs to the Paramyxoviridae family

1.description: Human metapneumovirus is an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus in the Paramyxoviridae family that causes upper and lower respiratory tract infections. It primarily affects infants, young children, older adults, and immunocompromised individuals. Clinical manifestations range from mild upper respiratory symptoms to bronchiolitis and pneumonia. Diagnosis is typically established by respiratory viral PCR testing. Management is supportive, including hydration and respiratory support when necessary. Complications include bronchiolitis, pneumonia, respiratory failure, hospitalization, and severe disease in vulnerable populations.
1.management: Provide supportive care with hydration, oxygen supplementation, and respiratory support as needed. No specific antiviral therapy is routinely recommended for immunocompetent patients.

clues.text: A 44-year-old man presents to his family physician with several weeks of cough, progressive shortness of breath, fatigue, and unintentional weight loss

Microscopic examination using India ink demonstrates encapsulated budding yeasts with a prominent polysaccharide capsule

1.description: Cryptococcal pneumonia is a fungal lung infection caused by Cryptococcus neoformans or Cryptococcus gattii. Infection occurs through inhalation of environmental spores and primarily affects immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with advanced HIV/AIDS, although C. gattii may infect immunocompetent hosts. Patients develop cough, dyspnea, fever, and constitutional symptoms. Diagnosis relies on antigen testing, culture, and histopathology. Management involves antifungal therapy. Complications include respiratory failure, fungemia, and dissemination to the central nervous system causing cryptococcal meningitis.
1.management: Evaluate for central nervous system involvement and initiate antifungal therapy. Severe disease requires induction treatment followed by prolonged consolidation and maintenance therapy.

clues.text: A 32-year-old woman presents to her family physician with three weeks of cough, fatigue, fever, and pleuritic chest pain that began shortly after returning from a hiking trip
She recently spent time in a desert region and recalls participating in outdoor activities during a windstorm. She has no significant past medical history

1.description: Coccidioidomycosis is a fungal infection caused by Coccidioides species, dimorphic fungi endemic to arid desert regions of the southwestern United States and parts of Central and South America. Infection occurs through inhalation of airborne arthroconidia disturbed from soil. Most infections are asymptomatic or cause a self-limited pulmonary illness. Diagnosis relies on serology, culture, or histopathology. Management depends on disease severity and host factors. Complications include chronic pulmonary disease, meningitis, osteomyelitis, and disseminated infection.
1.management: Mild disease may only require observation. Severe, chronic, or disseminated infection requires prolonged antifungal therapy and monitoring for extrapulmonary complications

clues.text: A 69-year-old man presents to his family physician with persistent cough, fever, and increasing shortness of breath despite completing two courses of antibiotics over the past month

1.description: Postobstructive pneumonia is a pulmonary infection that develops distal to a partial or complete airway obstruction, most commonly caused by bronchogenic carcinoma. Obstruction impairs airway clearance and promotes bacterial growth, resulting in recurrent or persistent pneumonia despite appropriate antibiotic therapy. Patients often present with cough, fever, dyspnea, and constitutional symptoms related to the underlying lesion. Diagnosis relies on imaging and bronchoscopy. Management requires treatment of both the infection and the obstructing cause. Complications include lung abscess, empyema, sepsis, and respiratory failure.
1.management: Treat the infection with appropriate antibiotics and relieve the airway obstruction when possible. Bronchoscopy, surgery, or oncologic therapy may be required.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents to her family physician with progressively worsening shortness of breath and a persistent dry cough over the past two months

1.description: Lymphangitic carcinomatosis is metastatic spread of malignancy through the pulmonary lymphatic system, most commonly from breast, lung, gastric, pancreatic, or prostate cancer. Tumor infiltration of lymphatic channels causes progressive interstitial edema and impaired gas exchange. Patients present with rapidly progressive dyspnea, dry cough, and constitutional symptoms. Diagnosis relies on characteristic CT findings and histopathologic confirmation. Management focuses on treatment of the underlying malignancy. Complications include severe hypoxemia, respiratory failure, reduced quality of life, and poor overall prognosis.
1.management: Treat the underlying malignancy with systemic therapy when appropriate. Provide supportive respiratory care and symptom management while monitoring for progressive hypoxemic failure.

clues.text: An 11-year old boy reports peeing more

1.description: Type 1 Diabetes is a disease caused by the autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells causing absolute insulin deficiency. C-peptide levels become low when the pancreas is making little or no insulin. Autoantibodies (GAD65) indicate an immune process attacking the insulin-producing cells of the pancreas.
1.management: Insulin therapy and glucose monitoring are the main treatments for T1D. Key investigations include HbA1c, glucose, electrolytes, renal function etc.

clues.text: A 43 year old man presents with increasing fatigue and nocturia

1.description: Type 2 Diabetes is a disease caused by insulin resistance with progressive beta-cell dysfunction. A diagnosis primarily made on blood glucose testing, unlike type-1 diabetes. Key labs include HbA1c, a long-term marker of blood glucose control, an oral-glucose tolerance test, fasting plasma glucose and random plasma glucose.
1.management: Lifestyle modification, metformin, GLP1 agonists, insulin if needed.  Key investigations include HbA1c, glucose, electrolytes, renal function etc.

clues.text: A 33 year old pregnant woman presents for routine prenatal visit at 28 weeks gestation
During her first pregnancy she delivered a 4.3kg (9.5 lb) baby vaginally without complication

1.description: Placental hormones induce insulin resistance exceeding maternal reserve, leading to gestational diabetes. Key labs include a 50g glucose test, oral glucose tolerance test. HbA1c, a long-term marker of blood glucose control, fasting plasma glucose and random plasma glucose. Electrolytes, renal function etc.
1.management: Diet, exercise, glucose monitoring, insulin if needed and postpartum screening

clues.text: A 33 year old man presents with vomiting and abdominal pain
He has a history of type-1 diabetes
His respirations are deep, rapid, and laboured (Kussmaul respirations)

1.description: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is a life-threatening acute complication of diabetes mellitus, most commonly associated with Type 1 diabetes, although it can also occur in individuals with Type 2 diabetes. DKA develops when there is a severe deficiency of insulin, preventing glucose from entering cells for energy. As a result, the body breaks down fat for fuel, producing acidic substances called ketones. The accumulation of ketones in the blood leads to metabolic acidosis, while elevated blood glucose levels cause dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.
1.management: Immediate management entails fluid resuscitation, potassium management, and IV insulin. Careful monitoring is required to prevent life-threatening complications. Provide explanation of appropriate glucose management longterm.

clues.text: A 72-year-old man with a 15-year history of Type 2 diabetes is brought to the emergency department

Blood glucose: 980 mg/dL (54.4 mmol/L)
Serum osmolality: 340 mOsm/kg
Arterial pH: 7.38
Serum bicarbonate: 23 mEq/L
Minimal ketones present
He does not have fruity breath odor, or deep Kussmaul respirations

1.description: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) is a serious, potentially life-threatening complication of diabetes mellitus that occurs most commonly in people with Type 2 diabetes. HHS develops when blood glucose levels become extremely elevated, leading to severe dehydration and increased blood osmolality. Unlike diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), there is usually enough insulin present to prevent significant ketone production and acidosis.
1.management: The primary goal of HHS management is to restore fluids, monitor electrolytes, and begin insulin therapy. Provide education on appropriate management of blood glucose longterm.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman with a 20-year history of Type 2 diabetes attends her annual diabetes follow-up appointment. Her

1.description: The patient is diagnosed with Diabetic Retinopathy, a microvascular complication of diabetes caused by chronic hyperglycemia damaging the retinal blood vessels.
1.management: Management includes optimizing blood glucose, blood pressure, and lipid control, regular ophthalmologic monitoring, and treatments such as laser photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-VEGF injections, or vitrectomy in advanced cases.

clues.text: A 19 year old university student is found with elevated serum glucose levels during annual bloodwork

1.description: Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young is an inherited form of diabetes caused by a single-gene mutation that affects insulin production.
1.management: Management depends on the subtype of MODY and may include lifestyle modifications, oral meds, or in some cases, insulin therapy. Genetic counseling and screening are also recommended as MODY follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern

clues.text: A 67-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes takes insulin before breakfast but becomes busy with errands and skips his meal.

1.description: A hypoglycemic emergency can occur when blood glucose levels fall below the normal range, depriving the brain of its primary energy source.
1.management: Immediate management includes administering 15–20 g of fast-acting carbohydrates if the patient is conscious, or intravenous dextrose or glucagon if unconscious. Long-term management focuses on identifying the cause, adjusting diabetes medications, regular blood glucose monitoring, and educating the patient on recognizing and treating early symptoms of hypoglycemia.

clues.text: A young woman presents with several months of menstrual irregularity.
She and her partner have been unsuccessfully trying to conceive.

1.management: Cabergoline or bromocriptine; surgery if refractory.

1.description: Growth hormone excess usually due to a pituitary adenoma.
1.management: Transsphenoidal surgery; medical therapy if persistent.

clues.text: A patient presents with progressive weight gain despite no major dietary changes.
Most of the weight accumulates centrally around the trunk.
She bruises more easily than before.

1.description: Pituitary ACTH-secreting tumour causing hypercortisolism.
1.management: Pituitary surgery; adjunctive medical therapy when needed.

clues.text: A 27-year-old woman presents to her family physician with excessive thirst and frequent urination that have progressively worsened over the past several months

1.description: Central diabetes insipidus is a disorder caused by deficient secretion of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the hypothalamus or posterior pituitary. Common causes include pituitary surgery, head trauma, tumors, infections, and autoimmune disease. Patients present with polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, and hypernatremia if unable to maintain water intake. Diagnosis relies on serum and urine osmolality measurements, MRI evaluation of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, and water deprivation testing. Complications include dehydration, severe hypernatremia, and neurologic dysfunction.
1.management: Treat with desmopressin replacement and ensure adequate water intake. Monitor serum sodium closely and address the underlying hypothalamic or pituitary disorder.

clues.text: A 52-year-old man presents to his family physician with excessive thirst and frequent urination that have gradually worsened over the past several months
He has bipolar disorder and has been taking the same mood-stabilizing medication for many years

Serum sodium and serum osmolality are elevated, while urine osmolality and urine specific gravity remain inappropriately low
MRI of the brain demonstrates an intact posterior pituitary bright spot with no hypothalamic or pituitary abnormalities
During water deprivation testing, administration of desmopressin produces little or no increase in urine osmolality

1.description: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is a disorder in which the kidneys are unable to respond appropriately to antidiuretic hormone (ADH). Common causes include chronic lithium therapy, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, chronic kidney disease, and inherited mutations affecting the vasopressin receptor or aquaporin channels. Patients present with polyuria, polydipsia, and hypernatremia. Diagnosis is established with water deprivation testing demonstrating failure to concentrate urine despite desmopressin administration. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause. Complications include dehydration, severe hypernatremia, and chronic urinary tract dilation.
1.management: Address the underlying cause, optimize hydration, and reduce urine output with dietary measures and selected medications. Monitor closely for dehydration and hypernatremia

clues.text: A 56-year-old man develops progressive difficulty speaking, trouble swallowing, and generalized weakness two days after being admitted to hospital
