A trident-shaped lesion involving the central pons is seen on imaging

1.description: Osmotic demyelination syndrome is a neurologic disorder caused by rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia, leading to osmotic injury and demyelination, most commonly within the pons. Risk factors include alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease, and severe chronic hyponatremia. Patients develop delayed neurologic deterioration with dysarthria, dysphagia, spasticity, and altered consciousness. Diagnosis is established through clinical history and MRI findings. Management is largely supportive. Complications include locked-in syndrome, permanent neurologic deficits, respiratory failure, and death
1.management: Prevention through careful sodium correction is critical. Provide supportive neurologic care, rehabilitation, and close monitoring for respiratory compromise and long-term deficits.

1.diagnosis: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion (SIADH)
aliases: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion

clues.text: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his family because of worsening confusion, fatigue, and several recent falls
He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and reports a persistent cough and unintentional weight loss over the past few months
Neurologic examination reveals mild confusion but no focal deficits. He appears euvolemic, with no peripheral edema, orthostatic hypotension, or signs of dehydration
Serum sodium is 118 mmol/L, serum osmolality is low, urine osmolality is inappropriately elevated, and urine sodium concentration is increased
Chest CT demonstrates a centrally located hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy
Further evaluation identifies a neuroendocrine malignancy associated with ectopic production of antidiuretic hormone

1.description: SIADH is a disorder of excessive antidiuretic hormone secretion causing impaired free-water excretion and euvolemic hyponatremia. Common causes include malignancy (especially small-cell lung cancer), pulmonary disease, central nervous system disorders, and medications. Patients present with symptoms of hyponatremia ranging from fatigue and confusion to seizures and coma. Diagnosis is based on hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine in a clinically euvolemic patient. Management targets the underlying cause. Complications include cerebral edema, seizures, and osmotic demyelination from overly rapid correction.
1.management: Treat the underlying cause and restrict fluids first; if insufficient, escalate to urea or tolvaptan as second-line therapy; and use hypertonic saline emergently for severe symptomatic hyponatremia, with careful sodium correction to avoid osmotic demyelination

clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents to her family physician for evaluation of persistently elevated blood pressure despite taking three antihypertensive medications
She has experienced increasing fatigue and intermittent muscle cramps over the past several months. There is no family history of early cardiovascular disease
Blood pressure is 172/98 mmHg. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable, with no peripheral edema or signs of heart failure
Serum potassium is decreased, bicarbonate is elevated, plasma renin activity is suppressed, and the aldosterone-to-renin ratio is markedly increased
CT of the abdomen demonstrates a unilateral 1.8-cm adrenal nodule
Confirmatory testing demonstrates persistent autonomous aldosterone secretion despite sodium loading

1.description: Primary hyperaldosteronism is a disorder of autonomous aldosterone production, most commonly caused by an aldosterone-producing adrenal adenoma or bilateral adrenal hyperplasia. Excess aldosterone promotes sodium retention, potassium wasting, and metabolic alkalosis, leading to resistant hypertension. Patients may present with hypertension, muscle weakness, or be asymptomatic. Diagnosis relies on an elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio followed by confirmatory testing and adrenal imaging. Management depends on the underlying cause. Complications include cardiovascular disease, arrhythmias, chronic kidney disease, and stroke.
1.management: Unilateral disease is typically treated surgically. Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia is managed medically with mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists and cardiovascular risk reduction.

clues.text: A 17-year-old girl presents to her family physician with chronic fatigue, intermittent muscle cramps, and episodes of lightheadedness during sports activities
She has experienced these symptoms for several years but has otherwise been healthy. There is no history of diuretic use, vomiting, or eating disorders
Examination reveals low-normal blood pressure and mild muscle weakness. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable
Serum potassium and magnesium are decreased, metabolic alkalosis is present, and urinary calcium excretion is low

clues.detail: Normal kidney US

clues.text: Genetic testing identifies a mutation affecting the thiazide-sensitive sodium-chloride cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule

1.description: Gitelman syndrome is an autosomal recessive renal tubulopathy caused by mutations in the SLC12A3 gene, resulting in defective sodium-chloride reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule. The disorder mimics chronic thiazide diuretic use and typically presents during adolescence or adulthood with fatigue, muscle cramps, salt craving, and weakness. Diagnosis is suggested by hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalciuria. Management focuses on electrolyte replacement. Complications include arrhythmias, chondrocalcinosis, chronic fatigue, and impaired quality of life.
1.management: Provide lifelong magnesium and potassium supplementation. Encourage liberal salt intake and consider potassium-sparing agents for persistent electrolyte abnormalities or symptoms.

clues.text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic because of poor growth, excessive urination, and frequent episodes of muscle weakness
His mother recalls being told she had excessive amniotic fluid during pregnancy, and he has required multiple evaluations for dehydration since infancy
Examination reveals a thin child with short stature, mild hypotension, and signs of volume depletion. No edema is present
Serum potassium is decreased, metabolic alkalosis is present, plasma renin and aldosterone levels are elevated, and urinary calcium excretion is increased
Renal ultrasonography demonstrates bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis
Genetic testing identifies a defect affecting ion transport in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, producing a physiology similar to chronic loop diuretic use

1.description: Bartter syndrome is an autosomal recessive renal tubulopathy caused by impaired sodium, potassium, and chloride reabsorption in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle. It commonly presents during infancy or childhood with polyuria, polydipsia, growth delay, dehydration, and muscle weakness. Laboratory findings include hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, elevated renin and aldosterone levels, and hypercalciuria. Diagnosis is based on clinical and biochemical features. Complications include nephrocalcinosis, chronic kidney disease, growth impairment, and cardiac arrhythmias.
1.management: Correct fluid and electrolyte losses with potassium supplementation and liberal salt intake. NSAIDs and potassium-sparing agents may reduce renal salt wasting

clues.text: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with headache, nausea, vomiting, and progressively worsening blurry vision several hours after attending a party
Examination reveals tachypnea, confusion, and markedly reduced visual acuity. He describes his vision as looking through a "snowstorm."
Arterial blood gas demonstrates a severe high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Serum osmolar gap is elevated
MRI of the brain demonstrates bilateral putaminal necrosis
After tracking down his friends, they report that he consumed a large quantity of homemade alcohol the previous evening. He was initially asymptomatic but became increasingly ill overnight

1.description: Methanol poisoning occurs after ingestion of methanol-containing substances such as industrial solvents, windshield washer fluid, or adulterated alcohol. Methanol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to formic acid, which causes metabolic acidosis and optic nerve toxicity. Patients present with visual disturbances, gastrointestinal symptoms, and altered mental status. Diagnosis relies on exposure history, elevated osmolar gap, and high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Complications include blindness, cerebral injury, respiratory failure, and death if untreated.
1.management: Administer fomepizole to inhibit alcohol dehydrogenase, correct acidosis, and perform hemodialysis when indicated. Monitor closely for visual and neurologic injury.

clues.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with nausea, vomiting, ringing in her ears, and rapid breathing after taking medication for a severe headache
Examination reveals tachypnea, diaphoresis, agitation, and mild confusion. Temperature is mildly elevated
Arterial blood gas demonstrates a mixed primary respiratory alkalosis and high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Serum salicylate concentration is elevated
Chest radiography demonstrates bilateral diffuse pulmonary infiltrates consistent with noncardiogenic pulmonary edema
The patient reports persistent tinnitus
Her roommate reports finding an empty bottle of over-the-counter pain medication beside her bed earlier that day

1.description: Salicylate poisoning results from excessive ingestion of aspirin or other salicylate-containing products. Toxicity causes direct stimulation of the respiratory center, producing respiratory alkalosis, followed by accumulation of organic acids leading to high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Patients develop tinnitus, nausea, vomiting, tachypnea, hyperthermia, and altered mental status. Diagnosis is based on serum salicylate levels and characteristic acid-base abnormalities. Complications include pulmonary edema, cerebral edema, seizures, multiorgan failure, and death.
1.management: Administer activated charcoal when appropriate and alkalinize serum and urine with intravenous bicarbonate. Severe toxicity may require urgent hemodialysis.

clues.text: A 32-year-old woman presents to her family physician with recurrent kidney stones, chronic fatigue, and intermittent muscle weakness
She has a history of dry eyes and dry mouth and has required treatment for multiple episodes of nephrolithiasis over the past several years
Examination reveals mild proximal muscle weakness. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable
Serum bicarbonate and potassium are decreased. Arterial blood gas demonstrates a normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and urine pH remains persistently above 5.5
Renal ultrasound demonstrates bilateral medullary nephrocalcinosis with multiple small renal calculi
Despite systemic acidemia, the distal nephron is unable to appropriately acidify the urine

1.description: Distal renal tubular acidosis is a disorder characterized by impaired hydrogen ion secretion in the distal nephron, resulting in an inability to acidify urine. Common causes include autoimmune diseases such as Sjögren syndrome, medications, and hereditary disorders. Patients develop normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, nephrolithiasis, and nephrocalcinosis. Diagnosis is suggested by persistent urine pH above 5.5 despite systemic acidosis. Management focuses on alkali replacement and correction of electrolyte abnormalities. Complications include recurrent stones, nephrocalcinosis, chronic kidney disease, and bone disease.
1.management: Treat with oral alkali therapy and potassium supplementation when needed. Address underlying causes and monitor for nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, and kidney dysfunction.

clues.text: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic because of poor growth, fatigue, and intermittent muscle weakness over the past several years
His parents report that he drinks large amounts of water and urinates frequently. He has also experienced several episodes of dehydration during minor illnesses
Examination reveals short stature and mild proximal muscle weakness. No edema or hypertension is present
Serum bicarbonate and potassium are decreased. Arterial blood gas demonstrates a normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and urinalysis reveals glucosuria despite normal blood glucose levels
Radiographs of the wrists and knees demonstrate findings consistent with rickets
Further evaluation demonstrates generalized proximal tubular dysfunction with urinary wasting of phosphate, glucose, amino acids, and bicarbonate

1.description: Proximal renal tubular acidosis is caused by impaired bicarbonate reabsorption in the proximal tubule, resulting in renal bicarbonate wasting and normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. It may occur in isolation or as part of Fanconi syndrome. Causes include inherited disorders, monoclonal gammopathies, medications, and heavy metal exposure. Patients present with growth failure, muscle weakness, polyuria, and bone disease. Diagnosis is based on characteristic laboratory findings and evidence of proximal tubular dysfunction. Complications include rickets, osteomalacia, nephrolithiasis, and chronic kidney disease.
1.management: Provide bicarbonate replacement and correct electrolyte abnormalities. Treat the underlying cause and supplement phosphate or vitamin D when bone disease is present.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man presents to his family physician with progressive fatigue, generalized weakness, and occasional palpitations over the past several weeks
He has a long-standing history of type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by diabetic kidney disease and is taking an ACE inhibitor for hypertension
Examination reveals mild muscle weakness. Blood pressure is elevated, and there are no signs of volume depletion
Serum potassium is elevated, serum bicarbonate is decreased, and arterial blood gas demonstrates a normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Renal ultrasound demonstrates bilaterally small echogenic kidneys consistent with chronic kidney disease
Further evaluation demonstrates hypoaldosteronism or aldosterone resistance resulting in impaired distal potassium and hydrogen ion secretion

1.description: Type 4 renal tubular acidosis is a disorder caused by aldosterone deficiency or resistance, leading to impaired distal secretion of potassium and hydrogen ions. It most commonly occurs in patients with diabetic nephropathy, chronic kidney disease, or medications affecting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Patients often present with asymptomatic hyperkalemia or nonspecific weakness. Diagnosis is based on hyperkalemia with normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause and correcting hyperkalemia. Complications include cardiac arrhythmias and progressive kidney dysfunction.
1.management: Address underlying hypoaldosteronism, discontinue contributing medications when possible, and treat hyperkalemia. Some patients benefit from mineralocorticoid replacement or diuretic therapy.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man in the intensive care unit develops increasing fatigue and tachypnea two days after admission for septic shock
He received several liters of intravenous fluid resuscitation during the first 24 hours of hospitalization and has no prior history of kidney disease
Examination reveals mild tachypnea and signs of improving hemodynamic status. There are no focal neurologic deficits or signs of ongoing shock
Serum chloride is elevated, serum bicarbonate is decreased, and arterial blood gas demonstrates a normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Chest radiography and abdominal imaging reveal no new abnormalities to explain the acid-base disturbance
Review of the medication administration record reveals large-volume administration of 0.9% sodium chloride solution preceding the development of the acidosis

1.description: Saline-infusion hyperchloremia is an iatrogenic cause of normal-anion-gap metabolic acidosis resulting from large-volume administration of 0.9% sodium chloride. Excess chloride lowers serum bicarbonate concentration and produces a hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis without accumulation of unmeasured anions. It commonly occurs during aggressive fluid resuscitation in critically ill or perioperative patients. Diagnosis relies on recognizing the temporal relationship between saline administration and laboratory abnormalities. Complications include reduced renal perfusion, worsening acidosis, prolonged hospitalization, and diagnostic confusion with other acid-base disorders.
1.management: Reduce or discontinue chloride-rich fluids and consider balanced crystalloids. Monitor acid-base status and renal function while treating the underlying illness.

clues.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to her family physician with two days of painful urination, urinary frequency, and a persistent urge to void
She is sexually active and reports a similar episode approximately one year ago that resolved with antibiotic treatment
Examination reveals mild suprapubic tenderness without costovertebral angle tenderness, fever, or systemic signs of illness
Urinalysis demonstrates positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, pyuria, and bacteriuria
Renal and bladder ultrasonography demonstrates no structural abnormalities when performed
Urine culture grows a lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus

1.description: Urinary tract infection is a bacterial infection of the urinary tract, most commonly caused by Escherichia coli. Risk factors include female sex, sexual activity, prior urinary tract infections, urinary tract abnormalities, and catheterization. Patients typically present with dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort. Diagnosis is based on symptoms and urinalysis, with urine culture used in selected cases. Management involves antimicrobial therapy. Complications include recurrent infection, pyelonephritis, sepsis, and renal scarring in severe or untreated cases.
1.management: First-line treatment of uncomplicated UTI includes nitrofurantoin (100 mg twice daily, 5 days), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (1 DS tablet twice daily, 3 days), or fosfomycin (single 3 g dose); fluoroquinolones are reserved as alternatives.

clues.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, chills, nausea, and worsening left-sided flank pain over the past two days
She reports several days of painful urination and urinary frequency before the onset of her current symptoms. She has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections
Examination reveals fever, tachycardia, and marked left costovertebral angle tenderness
Urinalysis demonstrates pyuria, bacteriuria, positive leukocyte esterase, and white blood cell casts. Leukocytosis is present

clues.detail: US shows a bulky and hypoechoic left kidney with decreased vascularity. No evident calculus or hydronephrosis is seen. The right kidney has normal features

clues.text: Urine culture grows a lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus that ascends from the lower urinary tract

1.description: Acute pyelonephritis is a bacterial infection of the renal parenchyma and collecting system, most commonly caused by ascending infection with Escherichia coli. Risk factors include female sex, urinary tract obstruction, vesicoureteral reflux, diabetes, and recurrent urinary tract infections. Patients present with fever, flank pain, costovertebral angle tenderness, and urinary symptoms. Diagnosis is based on urinalysis, urine culture, and imaging when complicated disease is suspected. Complications include renal abscess, sepsis, emphysematous infection, and permanent renal scarring.
1.management: Initiate appropriate antibiotic therapy and ensure adequate hydration. Hospitalize patients with severe illness, sepsis, obstruction, or inability to tolerate oral medications.

clues.text: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever, chills, painful urination, and increasing pelvic discomfort over the past three days
He has no prior history of prostate disease
Examination reveals suprapubic tenderness. Digital rectal examination demonstrates a markedly tender, warm, and enlarged prostate
Urinalysis demonstrates pyuria, bacteriuria, and positive leukocyte esterase. Complete blood count reveals neutrophilic leukocytosis
Transrectal ultrasonography demonstrates an enlarged prostate without evidence of abscess formation
He recently underwent urinary catheterization following a surgical procedure

1.description: Acute bacterial prostatitis is an infection of the prostate gland, most commonly caused by ascending gram-negative urinary pathogens such as Escherichia coli. Risk factors include urinary tract instrumentation, catheterization, bladder outlet obstruction, and immunosuppression. Patients present with fever, dysuria, pelvic pain, and obstructive urinary symptoms. Diagnosis is based on clinical findings and urinalysis with culture. Management requires prolonged antibiotic therapy. Complications include prostatic abscess, urinary retention, bacteremia, sepsis, and chronic prostatitis.
1.management: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg po x 14-30 d or Septra DS 1 tab po BID x 14-30 d. Monitor for urinary retention, abscess formation, and sepsis, with hospitalization for severe disease.

clues.text: A 67-year-old man presents to his family physician because he has been waking up several times each night to urinate and feels that his urinary stream has become progressively weaker over the past year.
He reports increasing urinary frequency, urgency, and a sensation of incomplete bladder emptying
Digital rectal examination reveals a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, rubbery prostate without nodules or areas of induration
Urinalysis is unremarkable, serum creatinine is normal, and prostate-specific antigen is mildly elevated for age
Bladder ultrasonography demonstrates a significant postvoid residual volume with mild bladder wall trabeculation
Histologic examination demonstrates nodular hyperplasia involving the periurethral (transition) zone of the prostate

1.description: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant proliferation of stromal and glandular tissue within the transition zone of the prostate. It is an age-related condition driven by androgen signaling, particularly dihydrotestosterone. Patients develop lower urinary tract symptoms including urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia, weak stream, and incomplete emptying. Diagnosis is primarily clinical and supported by physical examination and assessment of postvoid residual urine. Complications include urinary retention, recurrent urinary tract infections, bladder stones, hydronephrosis, and kidney dysfunction.
1.management: Lifestyle modifications and α₁-blockers provide symptomatic relief. Larger prostates may benefit from 5α-reductase inhibitors, while refractory obstruction may require surgery.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents to her family physician because she experiences sudden urges to urinate that are difficult to postpone and has had several episodes of urine leakage on the way to the bathroom
She reports that symptoms have gradually worsened over the past year. She has had three prior vaginal deliveries and denies dysuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary tract infections
Physical examination is unremarkable, with no pelvic masses, neurologic deficits, or signs of pelvic organ prolapse
Urinalysis is normal, urine culture is negative, and postvoid residual volume is minimal

clues.detail: Bladder US is normal with negligible post-void urine

clues.text: Urodynamic testing demonstrates involuntary detrusor contractions during the bladder filling phase

1.description: Overactive bladder is a symptom syndrome characterized by urinary urgency, usually accompanied by frequency, nocturia, and urge urinary incontinence. It is most commonly caused by detrusor overactivity and becomes more prevalent with advancing age. Diagnosis is clinical after excluding infection, obstruction, and other causes of urinary symptoms. Routine testing is often normal. Management begins with behavioral interventions and pelvic floor therapy, followed by pharmacologic treatment when needed. Complications include falls, sleep disturbance, social isolation, and reduced quality of life.
1.management: Begin with bladder training, fluid management, and pelvic floor exercises. Persistent symptoms may require antimuscarinic therapy, β3-adrenergic agonists, or specialist referral.

clues.text: A 72-year-old man presents to his family physician because of gradually worsening urinary hesitancy, nocturia, and lower back discomfort over the past several month
He has otherwise been healthy and has not sought medical attention regularly.
Digital rectal examination reveals a firm, irregular nodule involving one lobe of the prostate
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is elevated above the expected range for age and has increased significantly from a previous measurement

clues.detail: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI shows mild median lobe hypertrophy invading into the urinary bladder. Loss of normal T2 high signal intensity of the left half of the peripheral zone, which is replaced by a T2 hypointense lesion which is crossing the midline, breaching prostatic capsule and invading left neurovascular bundle

clues.text: Biopsy demonstrates small malignant glands lacking a basal cell layer

1.description: Prostate adenocarcinoma is the most common cancer of the prostate and typically arises in the peripheral zone. Risk factors include advanced age, family history, African ancestry, and inherited mutations such as BRCA2. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with urinary symptoms, hematuria, or bone pain from metastases. Diagnosis relies on PSA testing, MRI, and prostate biopsy. Management depends on risk stratification and stage. Complications include metastatic bone disease, urinary obstruction, spinal cord compression, and death.
1.management: Management ranges from active surveillance to surgery, radiation, and androgen deprivation therapy. Advanced disease may require systemic therapy and treatment of metastases.

clues.text: A 64-year-old man presents to his family physician with intermittent left-sided flank discomfort and increasing fatigue over the past several months
He has a 40-pack-year smoking history, obesity, and long-standing hypertension
Examination reveals mild left flank tenderness and a palpable abdominal mass. His blood pressure is elevated
Hemoglobin is elevated above the reference range, and urinalysis demonstrates microscopic hematuria

clues.detail: CT shows a large exophytic mass lesion originating from the lower pole of the left kidney. Heterogenous post-contrast enhancement is seen with a central less enhancing core demonstrating partial central necrosis

clues.text: Histopathology demonstrates malignant cells with abundant clear cytoplasm arranged in nests surrounded by a delicate vascular network

1.description: Renal cell carcinoma is the most common primary malignancy of the kidney, arising from renal tubular epithelial cells. Major risk factors include smoking, obesity, hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and inherited syndromes such as Von Hippel–Lindau disease. Patients may present with hematuria, flank pain, constitutional symptoms, or paraneoplastic syndromes, although many tumors are discovered incidentally. Diagnosis relies on contrast-enhanced imaging and histopathology. Complications include renal vein invasion, metastasis, paraneoplastic syndromes, and death.
1.management: Localized disease is treated with partial or radical nephrectomy. Advanced disease may require targeted therapy, immunotherapy, and management of metastatic complications.

clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents to his family physician after noticing a painless enlargement of his right testicle while showering several weeks ago
He has a history of an undescended testicle that required surgical correction during childhood. He is otherwise healthy
Examination reveals a firm, non-tender intratesticular mass that does not transilluminate. No signs of infection are present
Serum testing demonstrates elevated β-hCG and lactate dehydrogenase levels
Scrotal ultrasonography demonstrates a solid hypoechoic intratesticular mass with increased vascularity
CT imaging demonstrates para-aortic lymphadenopathy

1.description: Testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in young men and is usually a germ cell tumor. Major risk factors include cryptorchidism, family history, and prior testicular cancer. Patients typically present with a painless testicular mass or enlargement. Diagnosis relies on scrotal ultrasonography, serum tumor markers, and staging imaging after radical inguinal orchiectomy. Prognosis is excellent with appropriate treatment. Complications include metastatic disease, infertility, treatment-related toxicity, and recurrence.
1.management: Radical inguinal orchiectomy for diagnosis and treatment. Further management depends on histology and stage and may include surveillance, chemotherapy, or radiation. Never biopsy due to risk of lymphatogenous spread.

clues.text: A 26-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressively worsening headaches, dizziness, and difficulty maintaining balance over the past several months
His father died of kidney cancer at a young age, and several relatives have undergone surgery for tumors involving different organ systems
Neurologic examination reveals truncal ataxia and impaired coordination. Fundoscopic examination identifies abnormal retinal vascular lesions
Neurologic examination reveals truncal ataxia and impaired coordination. Fundoscopic examination identifies abnormal retinal vascular lesions
MRI of the brain demonstrates a cystic cerebellar lesion with an enhancing mural nodule. Abdominal CT reveals multiple bilateral renal masses and pancreatic cysts
Further evaluation identifies a catecholamine-secreting adrenal tumor in association with retinal and central nervous system hemangioblastomas

1.description: Von Hippel–Lindau syndrome is an autosomal dominant cancer predisposition disorder caused by mutations in the VHL tumor suppressor gene. Loss of VHL function leads to abnormal accumulation of hypoxia-inducible factors and increased angiogenesis. Patients are predisposed to retinal and central nervous system hemangioblastomas, clear cell renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, pancreatic cysts, and other tumors. Diagnosis relies on characteristic clinical findings, family history, and genetic testing. Complications include vision loss, neurologic deficits, metastatic renal cancer, and premature death.
1.management: Provide lifelong surveillance for associated tumors with regular imaging and screening. Surgical intervention is often required for symptomatic or high-risk lesions.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe left flank pain, fever, and shaking chills that began earlier that day
She has a history of recurrent nephrolithiasis and reports several days of dysuria and urinary frequency before the onset of flank pain
Examination reveals fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and marked left costovertebral angle tenderness. She appears acutely ill
Leukocytosis is present, serum creatinine is elevated, and urinalysis demonstrates pyuria, bacteriuria, positive nitrites, and hematuria
CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrates a proximal ureteral calculus with moderate hydronephrosis and perinephric inflammatory stranding
Blood and urine cultures grow the same uropathogen in the setting of an obstructed collecting system, creating a urologic emergency

1.description: A septic stone is an infected urinary tract obstruction caused by a ureteral or renal calculus that blocks urine drainage while infection is present. Obstruction prevents adequate source control and can rapidly lead to pyelonephritis, bacteremia, and septic shock. Patients present with fever, flank pain, urinary symptoms, and systemic toxicity. Diagnosis relies on urinalysis, cultures, laboratory evidence of infection, and imaging demonstrating both obstruction and infection. Complications include septic shock, acute kidney injury, multiorgan failure, and death.
1.management: Treat as a urologic emergency with immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics and urgent urinary decompression via nephrostomy tube or ureteral stent placement.

clues.text: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left-sided flank pain that began while he was at work and has become progressively more intense
He reports a history of poor fluid intake and experienced a similar episode several years ago that resolved spontaneously
The patient appears restless and unable to find a comfortable position. Examination reveals left costovertebral angle tenderness without peritoneal signs
Urinalysis demonstrates microscopic hematuria without evidence of infection. Serum creatinine is normal

clues.detail: Non-contrast CT abdomen shows mild left hydronephrosis and hydroureter with a 3mm calculus in the distal left ureter proximal to the left uterovesicular junction

clues.text: Stone analysis reveals calcium oxalate crystals with characteristic envelope-shaped morphology on urine microscopy

1.description: Nephrolithiasis is the formation of calculi within the urinary tract, most commonly composed of calcium oxalate. Risk factors include dehydration, hypercalciuria, obesity, dietary excesses, and certain metabolic disorders. Patients typically present with acute colicky flank pain, hematuria, nausea, and vomiting. Diagnosis is confirmed with urinalysis and imaging, particularly non-contrast CT. Management depends on stone size, location, and complications. Potential complications include urinary obstruction, hydronephrosis, infection, acute kidney injury, and recurrent stone formation.
1.management: Treat with analgesia, hydration, and medical expulsive therapy when appropriate. Refer for urologic intervention if stones are large, obstructing, infected, or persistent.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to his family physician because he has been having increasing difficulty achieving and maintaining erections sufficient for sexual intercourse over the past year
His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus. He reports preserved libido.
Examination reveals diminished peripheral pulses and reduced sensation in both feet. Genital examination is otherwise unremarkable
Hemoglobin A1c is elevated, while serum testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and prolactin levels are within normal limits
Penile Doppler ultrasonography demonstrates reduced arterial inflow to the corpora cavernosa following pharmacologic stimulation
There is an absence of spontaneous nocturnal erections

1.description: Erectile dysfunction is the persistent inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual activity. Common causes include vascular disease, diabetes mellitus, neurologic disorders, medications, endocrine abnormalities, and psychological factors. Patients often present with difficulty obtaining or sustaining erections. Diagnosis relies on a focused sexual and medical history, physical examination, and selective laboratory testing. Management targets reversible causes and underlying comorbidities. Complications include relationship difficulties, reduced quality of life, and identification of underlying cardiovascular disease.
1.management: Address reversible causes and optimize cardiovascular risk factors. First-line therapy typically involves phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, with additional treatments reserved for refractory cases

clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents to his family physician because of a gradually enlarging, painless swelling of the right side of his scrotum over the past several months
He denies urinary symptoms, fever, trauma, or recent sexual exposures. The swelling is more noticeable at the end of the day but is otherwise asymptomatic
Examination reveals a smooth, fluctuant, non-tender scrotal mass surrounding the testicle. The testis itself is difficult to palpate separately
Urinalysis is normal, and inflammatory markers are not elevated

clues.detail: Scrotal US demonstrates anechoic fluid collection surrounding a normal testicle

clues.text: When examined with a penlight in a dark room, the scrotal swelling transilluminates uniformly

1.description: Hydrocele is the accumulation of serous fluid within the tunica vaginalis surrounding the testicle. It may be congenital due to a patent processus vaginalis or acquired secondary to trauma, infection, malignancy, or idiopathic fluid imbalance. Patients typically present with painless scrotal enlargement and a sensation of heaviness. Diagnosis is based on physical examination and confirmed with scrotal ultrasonography. Most hydroceles are benign. Complications are uncommon but include discomfort, cosmetic concerns, and rarely masking an underlying testicular pathology.
1.management: Observe asymptomatic hydroceles. Symptomatic, enlarging, or persistent cases may require surgical hydrocelectomy after excluding underlying infection, malignancy, or hernia.

clues.text: A 36-year-old man presents to his family physician after noticing a painless lump in his scrotum while showering several weeks ago
He denies trauma, urinary symptoms, constitutional symptoms, or a family history of testicular cancer. The mass has not changed significantly in size
Examination reveals a smooth, mobile, non-tender cystic mass superior and posterior to the testicle that is clearly separate from the testicular parenchyma
Urinalysis and serum tumor markers are within normal limits
Scrotal ultrasonography demonstrates a well-circumscribed anechoic cyst arising from the head of the epididymis with a normal-appearing testicle
The lesion transilluminates and remains distinct from the testis

1.description: An epididymal cyst is a benign fluid-filled lesion arising from the epididymis, most commonly from the epididymal head. When the cyst contains spermatozoa, it is termed a spermatocele. These lesions are common in adult men and are often discovered incidentally. Patients typically present with a painless scrotal mass separate from the testicle. Diagnosis is confirmed with scrotal ultrasonography. Most lesions are asymptomatic and benign. Complications are uncommon but may include discomfort, enlargement, or anxiety regarding malignancy.
1.management: Reassure and observe asymptomatic lesions. Surgical excision may be considered for persistent pain, significant enlargement, or diagnostic uncertainty after imaging evaluation.

clues.text: A 22-year-old man presents to his family physician because of a dull aching discomfort in his left scrotum that becomes more noticeable after prolonged standing or exercise
He and his partner have been attempting to conceive for over a year without success. He otherwise feels well and has no history of genitourinary infections or trauma
Examination reveals a soft, tortuous mass superior to the left testicle that becomes more prominent with standing and the Valsalva maneuver
Routine bloodwork and hormone studies are normal. Semen analysis demonstrates reduced sperm motility and concentration
Scrotal ultrasonography demonstrates multiple dilated veins of the pampiniform plexus with retrograde blood flow during Valsalva
The scrotal mass is described as feeling like a "bag of worms" on palpation

1.description: Varicocele is an abnormal dilation of the pampiniform venous plexus within the spermatic cord, most commonly occurring on the left side because of venous drainage anatomy. It is a common cause of male infertility and may present with scrotal heaviness, discomfort, or be discovered incidentally. Diagnosis is usually clinical and confirmed with Doppler ultrasonography when needed. Management depends on symptoms and fertility goals. Complications include testicular atrophy, impaired spermatogenesis, infertility, and recurrent symptoms.
1.management: Observe asymptomatic cases. Symptomatic patients or those with infertility may benefit from surgical ligation or percutaneous embolization of the affected veins.

clues.text: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with gradually worsening left-sided scrotal pain and swelling over the past three days
He is sexually active with multiple partners and reports several days of dysuria before the onset of scrotal symptoms
Examination reveals a swollen, tender epididymis posterior to the testicle with overlying scrotal erythema. Elevation of the affected hemiscrotum partially relieves the pain
Urinalysis demonstrates pyuria, and nucleic acid amplification testing is positive for a sexually transmitted bacterial pathogen
Scrotal Doppler ultrasonography demonstrates an enlarged epididymis with increased blood flow and preserved testicular perfusion
The cremasteric reflex remains intact

1.description: Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted pathogens in younger men and enteric gram-negative bacteria in older men. Patients present with gradually progressive scrotal pain, swelling, dysuria, and occasionally fever. Diagnosis relies on history, physical examination, urine testing, and Doppler ultrasonography to exclude testicular torsion. Management includes antimicrobial therapy directed at the underlying pathogen. Complications include abscess formation, chronic pain, infertility, testicular infarction, and recurrent infection.
1.management: Treat with pathogen-directed antibiotics, analgesia, scrotal elevation, and rest. Exclude testicular torsion promptly and monitor for abscess or fertility complications

clues.text: A 19-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever and progressively worsening right testicular pain and swelling over the past two days
One week earlier, he developed facial swelling, malaise, and low-grade fever that resolved without medical attention
Examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and exquisitely tender testicle
Complete blood count demonstrates mild leukocytosis. Urinalysis is unremarkable, and bacterial urine cultures are negative
Scrotal Doppler ultrasonography demonstrates an enlarged testicle with increased blood flow and diffuse parenchymal edema
Further history reveals recent parotid gland swelling caused by a viral illness

1.description: Orchitis is inflammation of the testicle, most commonly caused by viral infection, particularly mumps virus in unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated individuals. It may also occur secondary to bacterial genitourinary infections. Patients present with testicular pain, swelling, erythema, and systemic symptoms such as fever. Diagnosis relies on clinical findings and scrotal ultrasonography to exclude testicular torsion. Management is primarily supportive for viral disease. Complications include testicular atrophy, infertility, chronic pain, and impaired testosterone production.
1.management: Provide analgesia, scrotal support, and supportive care. Treat underlying bacterial infection when present and monitor for testicular atrophy or fertility complications.

clues.text: A 24-year-old man presents to his family physician because of a painless groin bulge that becomes more noticeable when lifting weights and coughing
He reports that the swelling has been present intermittently since adolescence and often disappears when he lies down
Examination reveals a reducible right groin mass that enlarges with the Valsalva maneuver and extends into the ipsilateral hemiscrotum
Complete blood count, electrolyte panel, and inflammatory markers are within normal limits
Groin ultrasonography demonstrates abdominal contents protruding through the deep inguinal ring and coursing within the inguinal canal
The hernia sac follows the path of the spermatic cord and lies lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels

1.description: Indirect inguinal hernia occurs when abdominal contents protrude through a patent processus vaginalis and enter the inguinal canal via the deep inguinal ring. It is the most common type of inguinal hernia and is often congenital in origin, although symptoms may not appear until later in life. Patients present with a reducible groin bulge that enlarges with increased intra-abdominal pressure. Diagnosis is usually clinical. Complications include incarceration, bowel obstruction, strangulation, and ischemic bowel injury.
1.management: Elective surgical repair is recommended for symptomatic hernias. Urgent intervention is required for incarceration, strangulation, bowel obstruction, or compromised blood supply.

clues.text: A 16-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe left testicular pain that awakened him from sleep several hours ago
He reports a brief episode of similar pain a few weeks earlier that resolved spontaneously without medical attention
Examination reveals a swollen, exquisitely tender testicle positioned higher than the contralateral side. The cremasteric reflex is absent
Urinalysis is unremarkable, and inflammatory markers are within normal limits
Scrotal Doppler ultrasonography demonstrates markedly reduced or absent blood flow to the affected testicle
Surgical exploration identifies a congenital bell-clapper deformity allowing abnormal rotation of the spermatic cord

1.description: Testicular torsion occurs when the spermatic cord twists, obstructing venous and arterial blood flow to the testicle. It most commonly affects adolescents and is often associated with a bell-clapper deformity. Patients present with sudden severe unilateral testicular pain, swelling, nausea, and vomiting. Diagnosis is primarily clinical and supported by Doppler ultrasonography when it does not delay treatment. Testicular viability declines rapidly with time. Complications include testicular infarction, atrophy, infertility, and loss of the affected testicle.
1.management: Treat as a surgical emergency with immediate urologic consultation and urgent orchiopexy. Delay in intervention significantly increases the risk of testicular loss.

clues.text: A 29-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden scrotal swelling and pain shortly after being struck in the groin during a soccer game
He reports immediate discomfort after the injury, followed by progressive enlargement of the affected hemiscrotum over several hours
Examination reveals a tense, tender scrotal swelling with overlying ecchymosis. The testicle is difficult to palpate separately
Urinalysis is normal, and routine bloodwork demonstrates no evidence of infection or systemic illness
Scrotal ultrasonography demonstrates a complex heterogeneous fluid collection surrounding the testicle with internal echoes and septations
The scrotal swelling does not transilluminate when examined with a penlight

1.description: Hematocele is the accumulation of blood within the tunica vaginalis surrounding the testicle, most commonly following trauma, surgery, or testicular rupture. Patients present with acute scrotal pain, swelling, and discoloration after injury. Diagnosis relies on physical examination and scrotal ultrasonography, which demonstrates a complex fluid collection rather than simple anechoic fluid. Management depends on severity and associated injuries. Complications include infection, chronic pain, testicular compression, fibrosis, and missed testicular rupture.
1.management: Obtain urgent scrotal ultrasonography and assess for testicular injury. Small stable hematoceles may be observed, while large or expanding collections require surgery.

clues.text: A 23-year-old woman presents to her family physician with two days of painful urination and an increasing urge to urinate frequently
She recently became sexually active with a new partner and experienced a similar episode approximately one year ago
Examination reveals mild suprapubic tenderness without fever, flank pain, or costovertebral angle tenderness
Urinalysis demonstrates pyuria, bacteriuria, positive leukocyte esterase, and positive nitrites
Bladder ultrasonography, when performed, demonstrates a normal urinary tract without obstruction
Urine culture grows a lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus

1.description: Acute cystitis is a bacterial infection of the urinary bladder, most commonly caused by Escherichia coli. Risk factors include female sex, sexual activity, prior urinary tract infections, spermicide use, and urinary tract abnormalities. Patients typically present with dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort without systemic illness. Diagnosis is based on symptoms and urinalysis, with urine culture reserved for selected cases. Complications include recurrent infection, ascending infection causing pyelonephritis, and, rarely, sepsis.
1.management: Nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX, or fosfomycin are first-line for uncomplicated cystitis; fluoroquinolones are reserved as alternative agents due to resistance concerns.

clues.text: A 22-year-old man presents to a sexual health clinic with burning during urination and a new urethral discharge that began three days ago
He reports unprotected sexual intercourse with a new partner two weeks earlier and has no prior history of urinary tract disease
Examination reveals a mucopurulent discharge at the urethral meatus. The testes and epididymides are non-tender
Urinalysis demonstrates pyuria without significant bacteriuria.
A urethral swab demonstrates numerous neutrophils
Nucleic acid amplification testing identifies Chlamydia trachomatis

1.description: Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Patients typically present with dysuria, urethral discharge, and urethral irritation. Risk factors include unprotected sexual activity and multiple sexual partners. Diagnosis relies on nucleic acid amplification testing of urine or urethral specimens. Management involves pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy and partner notification. Complications include epididymitis, pelvic inflammatory disease, infertility, and reactive arthritis.
1.management: Treat with appropriate empiric antibiotics while awaiting test results. Ensure partner notification and treatment, and recommend abstinence until therapy is completed.

clues.text: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a painful penile erection that has persisted for more than six hours despite the absence of sexual stimulation
He has a history of sickle cell disease and recently began taking a new medication for erectile dysfunction
Examination reveals a rigid penile shaft with marked tenderness. The glans penis remains relatively soft
Corporal blood gas analysis demonstrates hypoxemia, hypercapnia, and acidosis
Penile Doppler ultrasonography demonstrates markedly reduced cavernosal arterial blood flow
Aspiration of the corpora cavernosa yields dark, deoxygenated blood consistent with an ischemic low-flow state

1.description: Priapism is a prolonged penile erection lasting more than four hours that occurs independently of sexual stimulation. Ischemic (low-flow) priapism is the most common type and is associated with sickle cell disease, medications, hematologic disorders, and intracavernosal therapies. Patients present with a painful persistent erection and risk progressive corporal ischemia. Diagnosis relies on history, corporal blood gas analysis, and Doppler ultrasonography. Complications include corporal fibrosis, erectile dysfunction, penile deformity, and permanent tissue injury.
1.management: Treat as a urologic emergency with corporal aspiration and intracavernosal sympathomimetic therapy. Address underlying causes promptly to prevent permanent erectile dysfunction

clues.text: A 71-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe penile pain and swelling that developed several hours after a urinary catheter was inserted
Examination reveals a markedly swollen glans penis with a tight constricting band of foreskin trapped behind the corona
Routine laboratory investigations are unremarkable. Urinalysis demonstrates no evidence of infection
Further physical examination demonstrates venous congestion and progressive distal penile edema
The retracted foreskin cannot be advanced back over the glans, creating a risk of ischemic injury to the distal penis
He is uncircumcised and reports that the foreskin was retracted during the procedure but was not returned to its normal position afterward

1.description: Paraphimosis occurs when a retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans penis and cannot be returned to its normal position. The resulting constriction impairs venous and lymphatic drainage, causing progressive edema that may eventually compromise arterial blood flow. Common risk factors include catheterization, penile procedures, and failure to replace the foreskin after retraction. Diagnosis is clinical. Complications include glans ischemia, tissue necrosis, urinary retention, infection, and permanent penile injury.
1.management: Treat as a urologic emergency with prompt manual reduction. Severe cases may require dorsal slit or circumcision to prevent ischemic complications.

clues.text: A 9-year-old boy is brought to his family physician because of difficulty retracting his foreskin and intermittent discomfort during urination.
His parents report recurrent episodes of foreskin irritation and inflammation over the past year. He is otherwise healthy and uncircumcised
Examination reveals a tight preputial opening that prevents retraction of the foreskin over the glans penis. Mild scarring is present at the foreskin tip
Urinalysis is normal without evidence of infection or hematuria
Observation during voiding demonstrates ballooning of the foreskin with urination
The foreskin cannot be retracted over the glans, distinguishing this condition from one in which a retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans

1.description: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin over the glans penis. Physiologic phimosis is common in young boys and usually resolves spontaneously, whereas pathologic phimosis results from scarring, chronic inflammation, or recurrent infection. Patients may present with difficulty retracting the foreskin, ballooning during urination, irritation, or recurrent balanitis. Diagnosis is clinical. Most cases are benign, but severe disease may impair hygiene or voiding. Complications include recurrent infection, urinary obstruction, and paraphimosis.
1.management: Treat pathologic cases with topical corticosteroids and gentle stretching. Persistent, recurrent, or severe symptoms may require circumcision or surgical intervention.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents to the emergency department with rapidly worsening scrotal pain, swelling, and fever over the past 24 hours
He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and recently noticed a small painful lesion near the perineum that he assumed would heal on its own
Examination reveals an erythematous, edematous scrotum with areas of dusky discoloration
Leukocytosis, elevated C-reactive protein, metabolic acidosis, and acute kidney injury are present
CT of the pelvis demonstrates extensive subcutaneous gas tracking along the fascial planes of the perineum and scrotum
Surgical exploration reveals rapidly progressive polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis involving the perineal and genital fascia

1.description: Fournier's gangrene is a life-threatening necrotizing fasciitis of the perineal, genital, and perianal regions. It is typically polymicrobial and most commonly affects patients with diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression, alcoholism, or recent genitourinary or anorectal infections. Patients present with severe pain, swelling, fever, systemic toxicity, and rapidly progressive soft tissue destruction. Diagnosis is primarily clinical and supported by imaging. Complications include septic shock, multiorgan failure, extensive tissue loss, and death.
1.management: Initiate immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics and aggressive resuscitation. Urgent surgical debridement is essential, often requiring multiple procedures and intensive care support.

clues.text: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden severe penile pain and swelling that began during sexual intercourse
He reports that his erect penis struck his partner's pelvis, immediately followed by loss of erection and worsening pain
Examination reveals marked penile swelling, ecchymosis, and deviation of the shaft. The penis appears enlarged and discolored
Urinalysis is normal
Penile ultrasonography demonstrates disruption of the tunica albuginea with an adjacent hematoma
The patient recalls hearing an audible "snap" immediately before rapid detumescence

1.description: Penile fracture is a traumatic rupture of the tunica albuginea surrounding the corpus cavernosum, typically occurring during vigorous sexual activity involving an erect penis. Patients classically report a snapping sound, immediate pain, rapid detumescence, and subsequent swelling and ecchymosis. Diagnosis is usually clinical but may be supported by ultrasonography or MRI. Associated urethral injury can occur. Complications include erectile dysfunction, penile curvature, painful erections, urethral stricture, and permanent deformity.
1.management: Obtain urgent urologic consultation and perform prompt surgical repair. Evaluate for associated urethral injury and monitor for long-term erectile dysfunction.

clues.text: A 61-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressively worsening right flank pain, nausea, and decreased urine output over the past two days
He has a history of recurrent urinary stone disease and recently experienced several episodes of intermittent flank discomfort
Examination reveals right costovertebral angle tenderness and mild abdominal fullness. He is afebrile and hemodynamically stable
Serum creatinine is elevated compared with baseline. Urinalysis demonstrates microscopic hematuria without significant pyuria

clues.detail: US demonstrates dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces with thinning of the surrounding renal parenchyma

clues.text: Further evaluation identifies urinary tract obstruction causing progressive dilation of the collecting system proximal to the blockage

1.description: Hydronephrosis is dilation of the renal pelvis and calyces resulting from obstruction of urinary outflow. Common causes include nephrolithiasis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, ureteral strictures, congenital anomalies, and malignancy. Patients may present with flank pain, decreased urine output, urinary symptoms, or be asymptomatic. Diagnosis is established with ultrasound or cross-sectional imaging demonstrating collecting system dilation. Management focuses on relieving the obstruction and treating the underlying cause. Complications include recurrent infection, nephrolithiasis, renal atrophy, and chronic kidney disease.
1.management: Relieve urinary obstruction promptly through medical, endoscopic, or surgical intervention. Monitor renal function and address the underlying cause to prevent permanent damage.

clues.text: A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fever, severe left flank pain, nausea, and increasing confusion over the past two days
She has a long-standing history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and recurrent urinary tract infections
Examination reveals fever, hypotension, tachycardia, and marked left costovertebral angle tenderness
Complete blood count demonstrates leukocytosis, serum creatinine is elevated, and urinalysis reveals pyuria, bacteriuria, and positive nitrites

clues.detail: Axial non-contrast CT demonstrates left renal enlargement with architectural distortion. There are bubbly and linear streaks of air within renal parenchyma and perinephric space with an intrarenal fluid collection. Bilateral perinephric fat stranding

clues.text: Urine and blood cultures grow a gas-producing gram-negative bacillus

1.description: Emphysematous pyelonephritis is a severe necrotizing renal infection characterized by gas formation within the renal parenchyma, collecting system, or surrounding tissues. It occurs most commonly in patients with diabetes mellitus and may be associated with urinary tract obstruction. Patients present with sepsis, fever, flank pain, and acute kidney injury. Diagnosis is confirmed by CT demonstrating intrarenal gas. Management requires aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and source control. Complications include septic shock, renal failure, multiorgan dysfunction, and death.
1.management: Initiate urgent resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Obtain prompt source control through drainage or nephrectomy when indicated, while closely monitoring for septic shock.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents to his family physician after noticing blood in his urine on several occasions over the past month. He denies dysuria or flank pain
He has a 13-pack-year smoking history and previously worked in the dye manufacturing industry for many years
Physical examination is largely unremarkable, with no costovertebral angle tenderness or palpable abdominal masses
Urinalysis demonstrates numerous red blood cells without evidence of infection

clues.detail: MRI reveals two papillary tumours on the posterior aspect of the bladder base

clues.text: Cystoscopy confirms multiple papillary frond-like lesions arising from the urothelium.

1.description: Urothelial cell carcinoma is the most common malignancy of the urinary tract and arises from the transitional epithelium lining the renal pelvis, ureters, bladder, and proximal urethra. Major risk factors include cigarette smoking, aromatic amine exposure, cyclophosphamide use, and chronic inflammation. Patients classically present with painless gross hematuria. Diagnosis relies on cystoscopy, urine cytology, and imaging. Management depends on stage and grade. Complications include local invasion, ureteral obstruction, recurrence, metastasis, and death.
1.management: Perform cystoscopic resection for diagnosis and staging. Additional intravesical therapy, systemic treatment, or surgery depends on tumor risk and extent

clues.text: A full-term newborn is noted to have an abdominal mass during a routine postnatal examination but is otherwise feeding and growing normally
Prenatal ultrasonography identified an abnormal kidney with multiple cystic structures, while the pregnancy and delivery were otherwise uncomplicated
Examination reveals a palpable, non-tender flank mass. Blood pressure and urine output are normal
Serum creatinine and electrolyte levels are within normal limits, and urinalysis is unremarkable
Renal ultrasound demonstrates multiple noncommunicating cysts of varying sizes replacing the affected kidney, with little identifiable normal renal parenchyma
Nuclear renal scintigraphy demonstrates absent function of the affected kidney with compensatory function of the contralateral kidney

1.description: Multicystic dysplastic kidney is a congenital malformation resulting from abnormal renal development, often due to early urinary tract obstruction or abnormal ureteric bud–metanephric mesenchyme interaction. The affected kidney is replaced by multiple noncommunicating cysts and lacks normal function. Most cases are unilateral and detected prenatally or during infancy. Diagnosis is established with ultrasonography and functional imaging when needed. Management is usually conservative. Complications include hypertension, recurrent urinary tract infections, contralateral renal abnormalities, and chronic kidney disease if bilateral.
1.management: Unilateral multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) is managed conservatively with serial ultrasound surveillance and blood pressure monitoring; most involute spontaneously. Nephrectomy is reserved for failure of involution, mass effect, hypertension, recurrent UTIs, or suspected malignancy.

clues.text: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with new-onset gross hematuria and dull left flank discomfort
He has end-stage kidney disease secondary to diabetic nephropathy
Examination is notable for pallor and mild left flank tenderness. His dialysis access is functioning normally, and there are no signs of infection
Serum creatinine is markedly elevated at baseline. Hemoglobin is decreased compared with previous measurements, while urinalysis demonstrates numerous red blood cells
Renal ultrasound demonstrates bilaterally shrunken kidneys containing numerous small cortical and medullary cysts that were not present before the onset of kidney failure
He has been receiving hemodialysis three times weekly for the past eight years

1.description: Dialysis-associated acquired cystic kidney disease is characterized by the development of multiple renal cysts in patients with long-standing chronic kidney disease, particularly those receiving dialysis. Unlike inherited cystic disorders, patients have no prior history of cystic kidneys before renal failure. The prevalence increases with dialysis duration. Diagnosis is established with renal imaging demonstrating numerous cysts in small atrophic kidneys. Management focuses on surveillance and complication monitoring. Complications include cyst hemorrhage, hematuria, infection, and significantly increased risk of renal cell carcinoma.
1.management: Monitor for hematuria, cyst complications, and renal malignancy. Continue renal replacement therapy as indicated and evaluate suspicious lesions with appropriate imaging.

clues.text: A 43-year-old man presents to his family physician with intermittent flank discomfort, increasing abdominal fullness, and several episodes of visible blood in his urine
His father required dialysis in his fifties, and several relatives have a history of kidney disease. He has recently been diagnosed with hypertension
Examination reveals elevated blood pressure and bilateral palpable flank masses. Mild costovertebral angle tenderness is present.
Serum creatinine is mildly elevated, and urinalysis demonstrates microscopic hematuria with mild proteinuria
Magnetic resonance angiography performed after a sudden severe headache identifies a saccular aneurysm arising from the circle of Willis

1.description: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease is an inherited disorder caused by mutations in PKD1 or PKD2, leading to progressive formation of renal cysts and enlargement of the kidneys. It is one of the most common inherited kidney diseases and typically presents in adulthood with hypertension, hematuria, flank pain, or declining renal function. Diagnosis is established by family history and renal imaging. Management focuses on blood pressure control and slowing disease progression. Complications include chronic kidney disease, cyst infection, nephrolithiasis, hepatic cysts, and intracranial (Berry) aneurysms.
1.management: Control blood pressure, monitor kidney function, and manage complications. Selected patients may benefit from disease-modifying therapy, while advanced disease requires renal replacement therapy.

clues.text: A newborn boy is admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit shortly after birth because of respiratory distress and difficulty maintaining adequate oxygenation
Prenatal ultrasonography demonstrated bilaterally enlarged echogenic kidneys and markedly reduced amniotic fluid volume during the third trimester
Examination reveals respiratory distress, a distended abdomen, and bilaterally enlarged palpable kidneys
Serum creatinine is elevated for age, and urinalysis demonstrates impaired concentrating ability. Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with renal dysfunction are present

clues.detail: US demonstrates enlarged echogenic kidneys without corticomedullary differentiation

clues.text: Further evaluation reveals congenital hepatic fibrosis with progressive portal hypertension during childhood

1.description: Autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease is a hereditary disorder caused by mutations in the PKHD1 gene, resulting in cystic dilation of the renal collecting ducts and congenital hepatic fibrosis. It typically presents in infancy or childhood with enlarged kidneys, renal dysfunction, and complications related to oligohydramnios. Diagnosis is established through prenatal or postnatal imaging and genetic testing when needed. Management is supportive and focuses on renal and hepatic complications. Major complications include pulmonary hypoplasia, portal hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and kidney failure.
1.management: Provide supportive management of renal insufficiency and hypertension while monitoring hepatic complications. Severe disease may ultimately require kidney or combined liver-kidney transplantation.

clues.text: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the nephrology clinic because of recurrent episodes of severe flank pain and multiple kidney stones despite adequate hydration
He has required several procedures for stone removal since early childhood, and his parents report a family history of unexplained kidney failure in a sibling
Examination is largely unremarkable except for mild hypertension. Growth parameters are below expected for age
Twenty-four-hour urine studies demonstrate markedly elevated oxalate excretion. Serum creatinine is mildly elevated

clues.detail: Fluoroscopy demonstrates heavily calcified kidneys and osteomalacia of the pelvic bones with bilateral protusio acetabuli

clues.text: Genetic testing identifies a mutation in the AGXT gene, resulting in deficiency of alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase

1.description: Primary hyperoxaluria type 1 is a rare autosomal recessive metabolic disorder caused by deficiency of alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase due to mutations in the AGXT gene. Excess hepatic oxalate production leads to recurrent calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, and progressive kidney injury. Patients often present in childhood with recurrent stones and declining renal function. Diagnosis relies on elevated urinary oxalate levels, genetic testing, and imaging. Management includes aggressive hydration and oxalate-lowering therapies. Complications include end-stage kidney disease and systemic oxalosis.
1.management: Hyperhydration, crystallization inhibitors (citrate), pyridoxine trial (responsive genotypes), lumasiran (RNAi therapy reducing oxalate production), and combined liver-kidney transplantation for advanced disease or ESKD

clues.text: A 63 year old man presents with gradually progressive muscle weakness, particularly with difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair.
He was recently diagnosed with small cell lung cancer.
Neurologic examination reveals absent patellar and Achilles tendon reflexes.
He also reports dry mouth, constipation, and erectile dysfunction.
After 10 seconds of sustained muscle contraction, both muscle strength and deep tendon reflexes transiently improve.
Serum testing is positive for antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCCs).

2.description: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is a rare autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder, in which antibodies target presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing acetylcholine release. Patients typically present with progressive proximal muscle weakness, hyporeflexia/areflexia, and autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth, constipation, and erectile dysfunction. About 60% of cases occur in people who have an underlying malignancy, most commonly small-cell lung cancer. Diagnosis is supported by anti-VGCC antibodies and electrophysiologic studies showing an incremental response with repetitive nerve stimulation. A characteristic finding of LEMS is improvement in strength and reflexes after brief exercise (post-exercise facilitation). Management includes treatment of the underlying malignancy when present and symptomatic therapy with amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine), pyridiostigmine, and immunomodulatory therapy in refractory cases.
2.management: treat underlying malignancy, amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine), pyridiostigmine, immomodulators if refractory to other treatment

clues.text: A 6-week-old infant presents with progressive, non-bilious vomiting occurring shortly after feeds.
Parents report poor weight gain and signs of dehydration despite continued feeding.
Physical exam reveals a palpable “olive-like” mass in the right upper abdomen.
Labs show hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis.
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates a thickened pyloric muscle with an elongated pyloric channel.
clues.detail: Abdominal ultrasound shows an elongated pyloric canal with increased pyloric muscle thickness. https://radiopaedia.org/cases/congenital-hypertrophic-pyloric-stenosis-3

clues.text: Pathology shows hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the pyloric circular smooth muscle causing gastric outlet obstruction.
